The headline: even with R&R’s well-known and well-named acts, the median act surfaces under multiple forms; the tail is heavy. A C0 that returns one cluster per surface form (i.e., no merging) is useless; a C0 that merges everything is also useless. The methods below try to land somewhere useful.

Within-document name variance: 1980 cross-section
Figure 2 sizes the data reduction happening upstream of the heatmaps below. Stage 1 counts every chunk C1 reviewed in each 1980 document (the input to FISCAL_MEASURE classification), split into the “relevant” chunks where C1 emitted at least one measure and the irrelevant chunks where it did not. Stage 2 keeps only the relevant subset — the same blue block from stage 1 redrawn on its own to make the funnel mouth visible. Stage 3 unfolds the relevant chunks into individual measure occurrences (one row per chunk × measure_rank pair), arranged by rank with the rank-1 layer marked in red. The shape of the funnel is the headline: Budget and Treasury chunks emit ≈ 5-10 measures each, so the rank-1 red band is a small slice of stage 3 — and that red slice is the pool the JW and embedding heatmaps below operate on. Rank ≥ 2 layers (grey) are out of scope for the heatmaps but are not noise; they record secondary measures C1 surfaced in the same chunk.


Show code
us_1980_docs <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
  dplyr::filter(year == 1980L) |>
  dplyr::distinct(doc_id) |>
  dplyr::mutate(series = sub("-[0-9]+$", "", doc_id)) |>
  dplyr::arrange(factor(series, levels = c("ERP", "BUDGET", "AR_TREASURY"))) |>
  dplyr::pull(doc_id)

build_funnel_df <- function(c1_s2_results, target_docs) {
  base <- c1_s2_results |>
    dplyr::filter(doc_id %in% target_docs)

  chunk_status <- base |>
    dplyr::group_by(doc_id, chunk_id) |>
    dplyr::summarize(has_fm = any(pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE"),
                     .groups = "drop")

  s1 <- chunk_status |>
    dplyr::count(doc_id, has_fm, name = "n") |>
    dplyr::transmute(doc_id, stage = "1. Chunks\nreviewed",
                     segment = ifelse(has_fm, "Relevant chunk",
                                              "Irrelevant chunk"), n)

  s2 <- chunk_status |>
    dplyr::filter(has_fm) |>
    dplyr::count(doc_id, name = "n") |>
    dplyr::transmute(doc_id, stage = "2. Relevant\nchunks",
                     segment = "Relevant chunk", n)

  s3 <- base |>
    dplyr::filter(pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE", !is.na(measure_name)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(rank_group = dplyr::case_when(
      measure_rank == 1L ~ "Rank 1",
      measure_rank == 2L ~ "Rank 2",
      measure_rank == 3L ~ "Rank 3",
      TRUE               ~ "Rank 4+"
    )) |>
    dplyr::count(doc_id, rank_group, name = "n") |>
    dplyr::transmute(doc_id, stage = "3. Surfaced\nname occurrences",
                     segment = rank_group, n)

  dplyr::bind_rows(s1, s2, s3) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      stage = factor(stage, levels = c("1. Chunks\nreviewed",
                                       "2. Relevant\nchunks",
                                       "3. Surfaced\nname occurrences")),
      doc_id = factor(doc_id, levels = target_docs),
      segment = factor(segment, levels = c("Rank 1", "Rank 2", "Rank 3",
                                           "Rank 4+", "Relevant chunk",
                                           "Irrelevant chunk"))
    )
}

funnel_df <- build_funnel_df(c1_s2_results, us_1980_docs)

funnel_palette <- c(
  "Rank 1"           = "#b2182b",
  "Rank 2"           = "#737373",
  "Rank 3"           = "#969696",
  "Rank 4+"          = "#bdbdbd",
  "Relevant chunk"   = "#4477AA",
  "Irrelevant chunk" = "#cccccc"
)

ggplot(funnel_df, aes(x = stage, y = n, fill = segment)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, position = position_stack(reverse = TRUE)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5, reverse = TRUE),
            colour = "white", size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = funnel_palette, name = NULL,
                    breaks = c("Relevant chunk", "Irrelevant chunk",
                               "Rank 1", "Rank 2", "Rank 3", "Rank 4+")) +
  facet_wrap(~ doc_id, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor    = element_blank(),
        legend.position     = "right",
        strip.text          = element_text(face = "bold"))





Figure 2: Per-document funnel. From C1 input to the rank-1 layer the heatmaps below operate on. Stage 1: chunks C1 reviewed, split into relevant (FISCAL_MEASURE emitted) and irrelevant. Stage 2: the relevant subset alone. Stage 3: all FISCAL_MEASURE occurrences (one per chunk × measure_rank pair) stacked by rank, with rank-1 marked in red. Stages 1-2 are counted in chunks; stage 3 in occurrences (so a single relevant chunk producing five measures contributes 1 to stage 2 and 5 to stage 3). The y-axis is free per facet to keep ERP-1980’s small counts legible alongside Treasury’s larger ones.








Figure 3 zooms into the rank-1 layer of Figure 2: it shows the pairwise Jaro-Winkler distance matrix among those rank-1 C1 measure names within each document (mirroring the Methods 2 + 3 pool used in Figure 4, so the two heatmaps carry the same acts). The heatmap pool is the within-doc surface forms intersected with the corpus-wide rank-1 pool (rownames(c0_us_embeddings)) — slightly larger than stage 3’s red layer because it includes a few names that are at rank ≥ 2 in this document but were rank-1 in some other document, while excluding rank-1 occurrences whose surface form is shared with another (so the same name shown once). Cells in the bottom band (JW < 0.15) would merge under single-linkage at the production threshold. Cells in the middle band (0.15-0.30) are near-misses — same measure with verbose-vs-bare phrasing, swapped word order, or abbreviation expansion. Cells in the top band (≥ 0.30) are genuinely distinct measures. Read each heatmap together with its numbered measure list (printed to its right). Off-diagonal cells just above the 0.15 line that link semantically-identical names are the failure cases that drive over-fragmentation; cells far above 0.30 linking surface forms that are the same act are the gap JW cannot close on its own.

Show code
jw_band_fill_doc <- ggplot2::scale_fill_stepsn(
  colours = c("#b2182b", "#f4a582", "#92c5de", "#2166ac"),
  breaks  = c(0.15, 0.30, 0.45),
  limits  = c(0, 0.6),
  oob     = scales::squish,
  name    = "JW"
)

make_jw_doc_panel <- function(this_doc_id) {
  measures_all <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
    dplyr::filter(doc_id == this_doc_id) |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name) |>
    dplyr::pull(measure_name)
  measures <- intersect(measures_all, rownames(c0_us_embeddings))
  n <- length(measures)
  n_dropped <- length(measures_all) - n
  if (n < 2L) return(NULL)

  dm <- stringdist::stringdistmatrix(measures, measures, method = "jw")
  df <- tidyr::expand_grid(i = seq_len(n), j = seq_len(n)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(jw = dm[cbind(i, j)]) |>
    dplyr::filter(j >= i)

  text_size <- if (n <= 10L) 3 else if (n <= 20L) 2.4 else 1.9

  heat <- ggplot(df, aes(j, i, fill = jw)) +
    geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", jw)),
              colour = ifelse(df$jw < 0.30, "white", "black"),
              size = text_size) +
    jw_band_fill_doc +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(n), position = "top",
                       expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_reverse(breaks = seq_len(n), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    coord_fixed() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
    theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

  legend_df <- tibble::tibble(
    y = rev(seq_len(n)),
    label = sprintf("%d. %s", seq_len(n),
                    stringr::str_trunc(measures, 90))
  )
  legend <- ggplot(legend_df, aes(x = 0, y = y, label = label)) +
    geom_text(hjust = 0, size = 2.7, family = "mono") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, n + 0.5), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_void()

  title <- sprintf("%s — %d rank-1 C1 measure names (%d non-rank-1 omitted)",
                   this_doc_id, n, n_dropped)
  heat + legend +
    patchwork::plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.4)) +
    patchwork::plot_annotation(title = title)
}

for (this_doc in us_1980_docs) {
  p <- make_jw_doc_panel(this_doc)
  if (!is.null(p)) print(p)
}









(a) Economic Report of the President













(b) Budget













(c) Treasury Annual Report










Figure 3: “Within-document JW distance. Pairwise Jaro-Winkler distance among rank-1 C1 measure names within each 1980 US document, one panel per series. Red cells (JW < 0.15) would merge under single-linkage at threshold 0.15; orange cells (0.15-0.30) are near-misses that did not merge but plausibly should have; blue cells (≥ 0.30) are distinct measures. Only the upper triangle is shown; the diagonal is JW = 0 by construction. The pool is each document’s distinct C1 surface forms intersected with the rank-1 corpus pool (rownames(c0_us_embeddings)); non-rank-1 surface forms are omitted, matching Figure 4.”



Figure 3 visualised what JW sees within each of the three 1980 US documents. Figure 4 is the same structure on the same documents and the same measure-name pool, but with pairwise embedding distance in place of JW. Distances are Euclidean on the L2-normalised E5 vectors c0_us_embeddings; because the embeddings are unit-norm, Euclidean distance is a monotonic transform of cosine distance (\(d_{\text{euc}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot d_{\text{cos}}}\)), so the figure mirrors what Methods 2 + 3 actually cluster on.
The colour bands are tighter than the JW figure’s because E5 packs everything into a narrow band of the unit sphere: corpus-wide pairwise Euclidean distances sit at median 0.45 with IQR 0.42-0.48. Bands at 0.40 / 0.45 / 0.50 therefore approximate “below corpus IQR” / “within IQR” / “above IQR” / “far above IQR” — a relative-rather-than-absolute reading, in keeping with the verdict text above.

Show code
emb_band_fill_doc <- ggplot2::scale_fill_stepsn(
  colours = c("#b2182b", "#f4a582", "#92c5de", "#2166ac"),
  breaks  = c(0.40, 0.45, 0.50),
  limits  = c(0, 0.6),
  oob     = scales::squish,
  name    = "Euclidean"
)

make_emb_doc_panel <- function(this_doc_id) {
  measures_all <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
    dplyr::filter(doc_id == this_doc_id) |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name) |>
    dplyr::pull(measure_name)
  measures <- intersect(measures_all, rownames(c0_us_embeddings))
  n <- length(measures)
  n_dropped <- length(measures_all) - n
  if (n < 2L) return(NULL)

  M <- c0_us_embeddings[measures, , drop = FALSE]
  dm <- as.matrix(stats::dist(M, method = "euclidean"))
  df <- tidyr::expand_grid(i = seq_len(n), j = seq_len(n)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(d = dm[cbind(i, j)]) |>
    dplyr::filter(j >= i)

  text_size <- if (n <= 10L) 3 else if (n <= 20L) 2.4 else 1.9

  heat <- ggplot(df, aes(j, i, fill = d)) +
    geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", d)),
              colour = ifelse(df$d < 0.45, "white", "black"),
              size = text_size) +
    emb_band_fill_doc +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(n), position = "top",
                       expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_reverse(breaks = seq_len(n), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    coord_fixed() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
    theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

  legend_df <- tibble::tibble(
    y = rev(seq_len(n)),
    label = sprintf("%d. %s", seq_len(n),
                    stringr::str_trunc(measures, 90))
  )
  legend <- ggplot(legend_df, aes(x = 0, y = y, label = label)) +
    geom_text(hjust = 0, size = 2.7, family = "mono") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, n + 0.5), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_void()

  title <- sprintf("%s — %d embedded C1 measure names (%d without embedding omitted)",
                   this_doc_id, n, n_dropped)
  heat + legend +
    patchwork::plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.4)) +
    patchwork::plot_annotation(title = title)
}

for (this_doc in us_1980_docs) {
  p <- make_emb_doc_panel(this_doc)
  if (!is.null(p)) print(p)
}









(a) Economic Report of the President













(b) Budget













(c) Treasury Annual Report










Figure 4: Within-document embedding distance. Pairwise Euclidean distance among L2-normalised E5 embeddings of distinct C1 measure names within each 1980 US document, one panel per series. Bands at 0.40 / 0.45 / 0.50 correspond to corpus-wide Q25 / median / Q75. Only the upper triangle is shown; the diagonal is 0 by construction. Measure names without an embedding (i.e., never appeared at rank 1 in the corpus) are omitted.



Reading Figure 4 against Figure 3, an immediate conclusion is that imposing the Rank-1 filter as in Figure 4 does not imply losing important fiscal changes. Indeed, doing so, refines the surfaced names and are more in tune with the acts Romer and Romer (n.d.) identify. Importantly, there is little within-document-within act name variance and it could be recupperated both with JW distance and with embeddings.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Tier-tagged eval set (descriptive)
This section documents c0_eval_gold_pairs, the chunk-tier-tagged pool used by the embedding-geometry probes in §“Embedding-geometry probe (F16 vs FP32)” and §“Eval-set noise probe”. It is not the headline gold for method comparison — that role belongs to R&R’s 49-act list, evaluated under the framework defined in §“Evaluation against RR’s act list” below. The structural reason for the split is in §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R”, which follows immediately.
The eval set projects the chunk-tier system through C1’s LLM output along the chain aligned_data → c1_chunk_data → c1_s2_results → c0_eval_gold_pairs:

c1_chunk_data (built by R/identify_chunk_tiers.R) emits (chunk_id, doc_id, act_name, tier) rows by string-matching gold passages and act-name subcomponents against chunk text. Tier 1 marks chunks containing a verbatim gold passage; Tier 2 marks chunks matching an act name via subcomponent decomposition or co-occurrence rules. Chunks matching multiple acts within the same tier are row-replicated, one row per (chunk × act). The Tier 1 / Tier 2 exclusion is chunk-level, not (chunk, act)-level: any chunk that is Tier 1 for some act is barred from Tier 2 consideration for every act, so Tier 2 evidence on mixed-tier multi-act chunks is dropped at the source.
c1_s2_results runs the C1 LLM on these tagged chunks via leave-one-act-out cross-validation; each (chunk × gold act) pair is its own fold with its own few-shot set. The LLM’s emitted measures[] array is exploded into one row per (chunk × gold act × measure_rank), so a chunk tagged with two gold acts is classified twice and may carry different measure_name strings under each gold tag.
build_c0_eval_gold_pairs() (in R/c0_aggregator.R) keeps only rows with measure_rank == 1, pred_label == FISCAL_MEASURE, and tier ∈ {1, 2}; renames act_name → gold_act_name; and flags surface forms paired with >1 distinct gold act as ambiguous for the evaluator to majority-vote.

Two consequences carry into every downstream metric. (i) The rank-1 filter discards non-primary measures the LLM emitted, so a chunk discussing N gold acts contributes at most N rows with only the rank-1 surface form for each. (ii) Chunks the LLM mislabeled NOT_FISCAL_MEASURE drop out entirely, silently truncating multi-act representation whenever the LLM’s per-fold output is inconsistent.


Show code
t1_slice    <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |> dplyr::filter(tier == 1L)
unamb_slice <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |> dplyr::filter(!ambiguous)
amb_slice   <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |> dplyr::filter(ambiguous)

n_rows_all  <- nrow(c0_eval_gold_pairs)
n_names_all <- dplyr::n_distinct(c0_eval_gold_pairs$measure_name)

fmt_pct <- function(x, total) {
  sprintf("%d (%d%%)", x, round(100 * x / total))
}

  ),

The two indented rows partition the Tier 1 + Tier 2 pool by whether each surface form maps to one (unambiguous) or more (ambiguous) gold acts. Percentages are shares of the Tier 1 + Tier 2 totals.

On ambiguity. Roughly 60% of the Tier 1+2 rows have a measure_name that’s been paired with more than one gold_act_name across chunks. Most cases trace to Tier 2’s permissive matching: a chunk may be tagged with act X because act X is mentioned somewhere, but the most-prominent measure C1 extracts is a different act. The evaluator handles this by majority-voting per measure_name within each bootstrap resample (so the contingency table operates on unique names). Tier 1 is shown as the cleaner anchor; Tier 1+2 is shown for statistical power.
Gold-set leakage caveat. The Tier 1/Tier 2 tags themselves were assigned by fuzzy-matching the gold act_name against the chunk text, so methods that exploit surface similarity (especially JW) will look artificially strong on this evaluation. Treat the per-act recall column as a complement — methods that nail F1 but miss specific acts may be exploiting the leakage.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
section: Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R
text:
Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R
c0_eval_gold_pairs has a structural ceiling on pairwise recall that comes from how the pool is constructed, not from any clusterer’s performance. build_c0_eval_gold_pairs() assigns every rank-1 measure C1 extracted in a chunk to every gold act that chunk was tier-tagged with — including chunks where C1’s primary measure was a different act. The act gold_act_name becomes a bag of co-occurring rank-1 measures rather than a list of surface variants of one act. Concretely, “Tax Reform Act of 1969” picks up members {ERISA, Asset Depreciation Range (ADR), H.R. 10947, tax bill passed in December} — distinct policy items, not paraphrases. No string-embedding clusterer can or should merge those.
Even on the Tier 1 slice (verbatim-passage tag, supposedly the clean anchor), the same pattern holds. “Deficit Reduction Act of 1984” Tier-1 members include {Economic Recovery Tax Act, Job Training Partnership Act, tax proposals (marginal income tax rate reductions and depreciation reform)}. The Tier 1 filter constrains where C1 emitted its measure, not what measure it emitted.
The §UMAP investigation in the 2026-05-28 commit cycle confirmed this empirically: 16 of 35 eligible Tier 1 acts have top-1 NN same-act rate = 0 on F16 raw embeddings (20/35 under the UMAP precision-friendly winner); across the 162-cell UMAP grid, cor(% acts reconstructed intact, F1) = +0.82 while cor(separation, pairwise_recall) = −0.07 — pairwise F1 is essentially a one-variable story about how often a gold act survives HDBSCAN as a single cluster, and that variable is capped by gold-pool construction upstream of the embedding. No UMAP variant strictly dominated the no-UMAP baseline on the original pairwise eval because both hit the same ceiling. See §“Reading the UMAP results” for the full diagnostic.
The pivot. Method comparison now scores against R&R’s authoritative 49-act list in us_shocks.csv, which is independent of any pipeline output. The pairwise-P/R evaluation against c0_eval_gold_pairs is preserved for the embedding-geometry probes (where median within/between separation is the metric, not a partition recall) and the eval-set noise probe (where the temporal mismatch is the diagnostic) — but no longer drives method selection.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Evaluation against RR’s act list

Reference: what us_shocks.csv provides
c0_us_rr_acts collapses us_shocks (one row per act × quarter × measure_type) to one row per distinct act with year_signed = year(Date signed). 49 acts spanning 1945–2007. The first few rows:


Show code
tar_load(c0_us_rr_acts)
c0_us_rr_acts |>
  utils::head(5) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 5: RR canonical-act list. First five rows of c0_us_rr_acts, sorted by year_signed.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                act_id
                act_name
                year_signed
              
        
        
        
                
                  1
                  Revenue Act of 1945
                  1945
                
                
                  2
                  Social Security Amendments of 1947
                  1947
                
                
                  3
                  Revenue Act of 1948
                  1948
                
                
                  4
                  Revenue Act of 1950
                  1950
                
                
                  5
                  Social Security Amendments of 1950
                  1950
                
        
      
    






This list is the metric’s reference: a predicted cluster is “good” iff some member surface form (and the cluster’s representative year) matches an entry in this list under the gates below.


Matching gates
Two complementary name gates, evaluated side by side in every metric table that follows. Both combined with a year window.
Keyword containment (precision-friendly). For each RR act, decompose the act name via generate_subcomponents() (the function R/identify_chunk_tiers.R already uses for Tier 2 chunk tagging — same decomposition, no new code path) into a set of searchable terms: the full canonical name, “and”-splits, parentheticals, embedded “{X} Act of {YYYY}” patterns, Public Law numbers, action+subject phrases. The shared BROAD_EXCLUSIONS list strips overly generic fragments. A cluster keyword-matches an RR act iff any of its surface forms (after squish_for_matching() normalisation) contains any of the act’s subcomponent terms. Property: immune to name-rewriting — a method that re-emits the canonical act name as cluster representative gets no extra credit because the test runs against the cluster’s members, not its canonical.
JW-min (continuous, abbreviation-sensitive). For each (cluster, RR act) pair, compute min over cluster members of stringdist::stringdist(member, rr_act_name, method = "jw") on lowercased strings. Threshold ≤ 0.30. Property: catches near-canonical matches that keyword-containment misses (e.g. typographic variants); also catches false matches across acts whose names share rare tokens. The JW gate is the relaxed one — it rewards methods that emit RR-style canonical names because those land at JW = 0 against the act.
The gap between coverage under keyword and coverage under JW is itself diagnostic: a method whose keyword and JW coverage agree is recovering the canonical forms via aggregation; a method with much higher JW coverage than keyword coverage is mostly re-emitting canonical names without aggregating much.


Year window
Cluster representative year = median of years extracted from members’ surface forms via extract_year_from_string() (regex \b(19|20)\d{2}\b), falling back to the mode of doc_year from c0_us_measure_pool when no member name carries an extractable year. Year gate: |cluster_year - year_signed| ≤ 2. Width ±2 chosen to (i) align with the Method 3 year-blocking variants so methods that respect the year prior get credit, and (ii) absorb retrospective references whose mode doc_year lags the signing year by 1–2.
Joint match gate per name gate: name match AND year match. A cluster that name-matches an act but is 30 years off does not match.


Headline metrics
Under each name gate, four numbers per variant. The arrow in each header gives the direction of improvement — ↑ greater is better, ↓ lower is better:

Coverage (↑ greater is better) = |{RR acts with ≥1 matching cluster}| / 49. Recall-flavoured: a higher value means more of R&R’s 49 acts were recovered by some cluster.
Spurious rate (↓ lower is better) = |{clusters with no RR-act match}| / n_clusters. Precision-flavoured; this is the metric the asymmetric-cost framing weights most. A lower value means fewer junk clusters that match nothing in R&R’s list.
Fragmentation index (↓ lower is better) = mean over matched RR acts of (# distinct clusters claiming that act). ≥ 1 by construction. Equals 1 (the ideal) when each matched act has exactly one cluster; grows when methods scatter one act across several clusters.
Year alignment (↑ greater is better) = of name-matched pairs (name gate only, ignoring the year gate), share where the year gate also passes. Tests whether name and year signals agree — low values under the JW gate indicate the JW threshold is producing cross-decade false positives.

Coverage and spurious rate are the headline pair. Fragmentation is the precision-flavoured tiebreak between methods at similar coverage. Year alignment is mostly diagnostic.
The shortlist tables in §Synthesis (e.g. Table 23, Table 25) carry three additional count columns that anchor these rates in absolute numbers; all three are descriptive context for the rates above, not gates with a preferred direction:

n clusters = total clusters the variant emitted on the corpus pool. Read it as the denominator behind the spurious rate and as a coarse aggregation-effort signal: a value near the surface-form pool size means the method barely merged anything (near-singletons), while a much smaller value means aggressive merging. Not “greater/lower is better” on its own — it’s only meaningful read against coverage and spurious rate (few clusters with high coverage is good; few clusters with low coverage means over-merging).
Clusters matched = |{clusters matching ≥1 RR act under the joint gate}| = n clusters × (1 − spurious rate). The count of “useful” clusters; the spurious rate is its complement expressed as a share.
RR acts matched = |{of the 49 RR acts hit by ≥1 cluster}| = coverage × 49. The numerator behind coverage, in act counts rather than a share.

Comparing Clusters matched against RR acts matched reads off fragmentation directly: when the two are equal each matched act has exactly one cluster (fragmentation = 1); when Clusters matched exceeds RR acts matched, the surplus clusters are the fragmentation.


Bootstrap CI
The four metrics are bootstrapped over two different sampling units, because they are proportions over different populations. n_boot = 1000 in the pipeline (the smoke test uses 100 for speed); both bootstraps take the 0.025 and 0.975 percentiles.
Coverage, fragmentation, and year alignment — resample the 49 RR acts. These are act-indexed, so resampling acts with replacement perturbs which acts the method is held accountable for — the noise the CI should capture (acts vary in difficulty for any given clusterer). This bootstrap does not perturb the cluster set; clusters are deterministic outputs of each method × hyperparameter cell.
Spurious rate — resample the clusters. Spurious rate is a proportion over the method’s emitted clusters (the denominator is n_clusters, which does not depend on the act set), so the act resample is the wrong sampling unit for it: dropping acts from the reference can only reclassify matched clusters as spurious, never the reverse, biasing the metric strictly upward and producing a CI that sits entirely above the point estimate. We therefore CI spurious rate with a separate cluster-level bootstrap: flag each cluster matched/unmatched against the full 49-act reference, then resample clusters with replacement. As an ordinary proportion bootstrap this centers the CI on the point. The interpretation shifts accordingly — the spurious CI answers “if the method had emitted a slightly different set of clusters” rather than “if R&R’s act list were a different draw.” Point estimates are identical to a single-pass computation under either reading; only the spurious CI columns reflect the cluster resample.


Worked example
Table 6 demonstrates the matching gates on one act and two contrasting clusters. The first cluster contains the canonical surface form (Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981) and matches under both gates. The second is a hypothetical singleton cluster of just ERTA: the keyword check fails (the subcomponent term is the full canonical name; “erta” does not contain “economic recovery tax act of 1981”), and the JW distance from “erta” to the canonical name is well above 0.30. An ERTA-only cluster therefore receives no credit — the method must merge ERTA with the canonical form for either gate to pass, which is exactly the aggregation behaviour C0 is meant to demonstrate.


Show code
erta_act <- "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981"
erta_subs <- generate_subcomponents(erta_act)
member_a  <- "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981"
member_b  <- "ERTA"

kw_a <- any(stringr::str_detect(squish_for_matching(member_a),
                                 stringr::fixed(tolower(erta_subs$term))))
kw_b <- any(stringr::str_detect(squish_for_matching(member_b),
                                 stringr::fixed(tolower(erta_subs$term))))
jw_a <- stringdist::stringdist(tolower(member_a), tolower(erta_act),
                                method = "jw")
jw_b <- stringdist::stringdist(tolower(member_b), tolower(erta_act),
                                method = "jw")

tibble::tibble(
  `Cluster member`              = c(member_a, member_b),
  `Subcomponent terms`          = c(paste(erta_subs$term, collapse = "; "),
                                    paste(erta_subs$term, collapse = "; ")),
  `Keyword check`               = c(ifelse(kw_a, "PASS", "FAIL"),
                                    ifelse(kw_b, "PASS", "FAIL")),
  `JW distance`                 = sprintf("%.2f", c(jw_a, jw_b)),
  `JW ≤ 0.30`                   = c(ifelse(jw_a <= 0.30, "PASS", "FAIL"),
                                    ifelse(jw_b <= 0.30, "PASS", "FAIL"))
) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 6: Worked example. Matching two hypothetical clusters against Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 under the keyword and JW gates.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Cluster member
                Subcomponent terms
                Keyword check
                JW distance
                JW ≤ 0.30
              
        
        
        
                
                  Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981
                  Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981
                  PASS
                  0.00
                  PASS
                
                
                  ERTA
                  Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981
                  FAIL
                  0.48
                  FAIL

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 1 — JW single-linkage
Before reading the threshold sweep, Figure 5 shows what JW is being asked to recover. Each row is a gold act with ≥2 surface forms in the eval pool; the box and jittered points show the pairwise JW distances among the surface forms tagged to that act. Vertical dashes mark the swept thresholds (0.10–0.30). An act whose distribution sits entirely left of a threshold line is fully recoverable at that threshold; an act whose distances spread past 0.30 cannot be merged without dragging in surface forms from neighbouring acts (i.e., over-merge). Ambiguous names are kept under every gold act they’re tagged to, matching the descriptive intent of the figure rather than the evaluator’s majority-vote.


Show code
within_act_jw <- function(pool) {
  pool |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
    dplyr::group_by(gold_act_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(dplyr::n() >= 2L) |>
    dplyr::group_modify(~ {
      names <- .x$measure_name
      d <- stringdist::stringdistmatrix(names, names, method = "jw")
      tibble::tibble(jw_distance = d[upper.tri(d)])
    }) |>
    dplyr::ungroup()
}

tier1_pairs  <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::filter(tier == 1L) |>
  within_act_jw()
tier12_pairs <- within_act_jw(c0_eval_gold_pairs)

plot_within <- function(df, title) {
  df |>
    dplyr::mutate(gold_act_name = forcats::fct_reorder(
      stringr::str_trunc(gold_act_name, 50),
      jw_distance, .fun = stats::median)) |>
    ggplot(aes(jw_distance, gold_act_name)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30),
               linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = "#4477AA", alpha = 0.3,
                 linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.15, size = 0.7, alpha = 0.6,
                colour = "#4477AA") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, NA),
                       breaks = c(0, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.5)) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Within-act pairwise JW distance", y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9)
}

p_a <- plot_within(tier1_pairs,  "A. Tier 1 only")
p_b <- plot_within(tier12_pairs, "B. Tier 1 + Tier 2")

p_a + p_b + patchwork::plot_layout(ncol = 2)





Figure 5: Within-act JW distances. Vertical dashes mark the swept thresholds. Acts with a single surface form contribute no pairs and are omitted.








What this means for Method 1. The median within-act JW distance is ~0.47 on both tiers, while the loosest threshold swept is 0.30. The typical pair of surface forms that ought to be merged is therefore geometrically out of reach for JW at any threshold the table considers; only a small minority of pairs (near-duplicates differing in spacing, punctuation, or abbreviation expansion) sit below 0.30. The implication is unavoidable: JW single-linkage cannot canonicalize this pool on its own. The threshold sweep in Table 7 below is best read as quantifying the asymmetric coverage/spurious cost given that fundamental ceiling, not as a search for a workable operating point. A method that can bridge ERTA ↔︎ Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 — semantic, not lexical — is required, which is what Phase B is for.
Figure 6 checks the same geometry from a different angle: instead of pairwise distances among surface forms, it measures each surface form’s JW distance to its gold-act label. If C0 had the gold canonical list as an oracle and tried to map surface forms to it by JW, the swept thresholds tell us how many surface forms it would catch. The answer mirrors Figure 5: median ~0.41 on both tiers, well above 0.30. A handful of exact matches sit at 0 (surface form is the gold label string verbatim), but most surface forms are too lexically distant from their canonical to be matched without semantic reasoning.


Show code
name_to_gold_jw <- function(pool) {
  pool |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(jw_distance = stringdist::stringdist(
      measure_name, gold_act_name, method = "jw"))
}

tier1_to_gold  <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::filter(tier == 1L) |>
  name_to_gold_jw()
tier12_to_gold <- name_to_gold_jw(c0_eval_gold_pairs)

plot_to_gold <- function(df, title) {
  df |>
    dplyr::mutate(gold_act_name = forcats::fct_reorder(
      stringr::str_trunc(gold_act_name, 50),
      jw_distance, .fun = stats::median)) |>
    ggplot(aes(jw_distance, gold_act_name)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30),
               linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = "#CC6677", alpha = 0.3,
                 linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.15, size = 0.7, alpha = 0.6,
                colour = "#CC6677") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, NA),
                       breaks = c(0, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.5)) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Surface-name → gold-label JW distance", y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9)
}

p_a <- plot_to_gold(tier1_to_gold,  "A. Tier 1 only")
p_b <- plot_to_gold(tier12_to_gold, "B. Tier 1 + Tier 2")

p_a + p_b + patchwork::plot_layout(ncol = 2)





Figure 6: Surface-name → gold-label JW distance. Same panels, threshold lines, and visual style as Figure 5; each point is one (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair.








The within-act figures pin down what JW can mechanically do; the ladders below quantify the consequences on the RR-aligned eval the §Framing committed to. Each row is one variant (threshold × year-blocking); each cell carries a 95% bootstrap CI (act-indexed metrics resample the 49 RR acts, spurious rate resamples the clusters — see §“Bootstrap CI”). Column definitions are the headline four metrics defined in §“Evaluation against RR’s act list”:

Coverage — share of 49 RR acts hit by ≥1 cluster under the joint name + year ±2 gate.
Spurious — share of clusters that match no RR act under the joint gate. The precision-flavoured column the asymmetric-cost framing weights most.
Fragmentation — mean number of clusters claiming each matched RR act (≥1).
Year alignment — diagnostic; share of name-matched pairs that also pass year ±2.

Two tables — one per name gate — so the keyword-vs-JW gap is visible side by side. Coverage will typically be higher under the JW gate (it accepts more surface forms); the keyword-vs-JW gap diagnoses how much of the JW coverage is recovery-by-near-canonical-match rather than recovery-by-aggregation.


Show code
shape_jw_rr_ladder <- function(gate_name) {
  format_rr_method_ladder(c0_jw_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      `JW threshold` = as.numeric(
        stringr::str_extract(variant_id, "(?<=t)\\d\\.\\d{2}")),
      blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                "Year ±2", "Unblocked")
    ) |>
    dplyr::arrange(blocking, `JW threshold`) |>
    dplyr::select(-variant_id)
}

render_jw_rr_ladder <- function(ladder) {
  panel_starts <- ladder |>
    dplyr::mutate(.row = dplyr::row_number()) |>
    dplyr::group_by(blocking) |>
    dplyr::summarize(start = min(.row), .groups = "drop") |>
    dplyr::arrange(start)
  group_index <- stats::setNames(as.list(panel_starts$start),
                                 panel_starts$blocking)

  display <- ladder |>
    dplyr::select(-blocking) |>
    dplyr::relocate(`JW threshold`) |>
    dplyr::mutate(`JW threshold` = sprintf("%.2f", `JW threshold`))

  display |>
    tinytable::tt() |>
    tt_theme_report() |>
    tinytable::group_tt(i = group_index)
}




Show code
render_jw_rr_ladder(shape_jw_rr_ladder("keyword"))

Figure 7 plots the two ladder tables as threshold sweeps so the trade-offs read at a glance. Each panel column is a name gate (keyword vs JW-min ≤ 0.30), each row is one headline metric (with its greater/lower-is-better direction in the strip label), and the two lines per panel are the unblocked vs year-±2 variants. Bands are the 95% bootstrap CIs (see §“Bootstrap CI”; spurious rate is cluster-resampled, the other three act-resampled). Read the coverage row against the spurious-rate row at each threshold: the asymmetric-cost framing wants the threshold that keeps spurious low without collapsing coverage.


Show code
jw_rr_sweep_df <- c0_jw_rr_metrics |>
  dplyr::filter(metric %in% c("coverage", "spurious_rate",
                              "fragmentation_index", "year_alignment")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    threshold = as.numeric(
      stringr::str_extract(variant_id, "(?<=t)\\d\\.\\d{2}")),
    Blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                              "Year ±2", "Unblocked"),
    gate = factor(gate, levels = c("keyword", "jw"),
                  labels = c("Keyword gate", "JW-min ≤ 0.30 gate")),
    metric = factor(metric,
      levels = c("coverage", "spurious_rate",
                 "fragmentation_index", "year_alignment"),
      labels = c("Coverage ↑", "Spurious rate ↓",
                 "Fragmentation ↓", "Year alignment ↑"))
  )

ggplot(jw_rr_sweep_df,
       aes(threshold, value, colour = Blocking, fill = Blocking)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), alpha = 0.15,
              colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6) +
  facet_grid(metric ~ gate, scales = "free_y", switch = "y") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Unblocked" = "#CC6677",
                                 "Year ±2"   = "#4477AA")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Unblocked" = "#CC6677",
                               "Year ±2"   = "#4477AA")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30)) +
  labs(x = "JW threshold", y = NULL, colour = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        strip.placement = "outside",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 7: JW headline metrics vs threshold. Columns: name gate (keyword vs JW-min ≤ 0.30). Rows: the four headline metrics, with ↑ (greater is better) / ↓ (lower is better) in the strip label. Colour: unblocked vs year-±2 blocking. Bands are 95% bootstrap CIs (spurious rate cluster-resampled, the other three act-resampled; see §Bootstrap CI). Y-axes are free per metric row.








Figure 8 shows the raw counts behind those rates on the same axes: the cluster budget the method emits (n clusters), how many of those carry an RR match (Clusters matched), and how many of the 49 RR acts get covered (RR acts matched). These are the numerator and denominator pieces the headline rates are built from, so the figure makes the mechanism legible: the spurious rate is high because n clusters runs into the thousands while Clusters matched stays in the dozens. n clusters is identical across the two gate columns by construction (it depends on the clustering, not the matching gate); only the lower two rows respond to the gate.


Show code
jw_rr_counts_df <- c0_jw_rr_metrics |>
  dplyr::distinct(variant_id, gate, n_clusters,
                  n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(n_clusters, n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched),
    names_to = "outcome", values_to = "count") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    threshold = as.numeric(
      stringr::str_extract(variant_id, "(?<=t)\\d\\.\\d{2}")),
    Blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                              "Year ±2", "Unblocked"),
    gate = factor(gate, levels = c("keyword", "jw"),
                  labels = c("Keyword gate", "JW-min ≤ 0.30 gate")),
    outcome = factor(outcome,
      levels = c("n_clusters", "n_clusters_matched", "n_rr_acts_matched"),
      labels = c("n clusters", "Clusters matched", "RR acts matched"))
  )

ggplot(jw_rr_counts_df,
       aes(threshold, count, colour = Blocking)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6) +
  facet_grid(outcome ~ gate, scales = "free_y", switch = "y") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Unblocked" = "#CC6677",
                                 "Year ±2"   = "#4477AA")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30)) +
  labs(x = "JW threshold", y = NULL, colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        strip.placement = "outside",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 8: JW match counts vs threshold. Same layout as Figure 7 but plotting raw counts rather than rates. Columns: name gate. Rows: total clusters emitted, clusters carrying an RR match, and RR acts covered (out of 49). Colour: unblocked vs year-±2 blocking. Y-axes are free per row; n clusters is gate-invariant so its two columns are identical. Counts are exact (no bootstrap CI).









Reading the match counts
Three mechanics make the count rows legible, and each corrects an intuition the rates alone invite.
First, year-±2 blocking lifts n clusters at every threshold because it forbids merges, not because it drops names. Blocking is a clustering-side constraint: only name pairs within ±2 years are eligible to merge (run_jw_clusters masks the rest). No name leaves the pool, so the count cannot fall. Pairs that JW distance alone would have fused but that sit more than two years apart stay separate, so consolidation drops and the cluster count rises. The natural guess that “filtering should shrink the output” has the sign backwards: same elements, fewer permitted merges, more clusters. Note also that blocking’s lift on the lower two rows (Clusters matched, RR acts matched) is a different effect from its lift on n clusters: the extra clusters come from forbidden merges, but the extra matches come from clusters staying year-coherent enough to clear the year gate.
Second, the coverage collapse at high thresholds is a year-gate failure, not names going missing. A cluster name-matches on any of its member surface forms (minimum JW distance, any keyword hit across members), so a blob that swallows two acts’ names still name-matches both. What it cannot preserve is a single representative year: the cluster year is the median of its members’ years, and single-linkage chaining at a loose threshold smears that median across a wide span. Acts whose true year drifts more than two years from the blob’s median then fail the year gate and drop out of RR acts matched. The decisive evidence is in the figure itself: at t = 0.30 the unblocked variant covers 8 acts while year-±2 blocking covers 31. Blocking touches only the year-eligibility of merges, so a 4× swing can only be the year gate at work. (Even the blocked variant slips from 46 to 31 at t = 0.30, because ±2 pairwise eligibility still lets a single-linkage chain span more than two years end to end.)
Third, Clusters matched falls with the threshold for two compounding reasons, only one of which is overmerging. By construction Clusters matched = n clusters × (1 − spurious rate), and both factors shrink as the threshold loosens: the cluster budget collapses (≈1,640 down to ≈180 in the unblocked JW gate) and the match rate falls (about 13% down to under 2%). The overmerge-and-year-smear story explains the rate term; the shrinking cluster budget, the larger arithmetic factor, explains the count term. Reading the whole fall as overmerging would miss that most of it is simply far fewer clusters left to match.


Interpreting the threshold sweep
Read across the four rows, the conservative end (JW threshold 0.10) is weakly best on three of the four metrics and worst on the fourth. Coverage, spurious rate, and year alignment all favour the low threshold; fragmentation favours the high one. That single crossing is the whole trade-off, and it is consistent with fragmentation being a consolidation (over-segmentation) metric that rewards aggressive merging while the other three reward keeping clusters fine-grained. Note that the recall-flavoured metric here is coverage (did we recover all 49 acts), not fragmentation.
But “0.10 dominates” overstates what the sweep shows, and three qualifications matter:

The dominance is mostly inside the noise. Under year-±2 blocking, coverage is flat at 0.939 from t = 0.10 through 0.25 (JW gate), so 0.10 is tied, not ahead; unblocked, the coverage CIs are wide (act-resampled, n = 49) and overlap heavily across 0.10–0.20. Year alignment is the same story, and its real driver is the gate, not the threshold: the JW gate sits near 0.18 at every threshold while the keyword gate runs 0.7–0.9 at low thresholds (crashing toward 0.1–0.35 as the threshold loosens). The only metric on which 0.10 is cleanly best is the spurious rate, whose tight cluster-resampled CIs barely overlap.
The spurious “win” is least-bad, not good, and partly structural. The spurious rate is 0.86–0.99 everywhere; even the best cell leaves ~86% of clusters matching no RR act. That floor is largely a target-size mismatch: the pool holds 1,976 distinct measure names but R&R’s exogenous list is only 49 acts, so most clusters are legitimately non-RR measures. Optimising a metric that is structurally pinned near 1 reads more like choosing the least-bad column than recovering precision.
The fragmentation improvement is confounded with collapsing coverage. Fragmentation is averaged only over matched acts. At t = 0.30 it reaches 1.00, but only 8 of 49 acts match there, so the denominator has shrunk to the few easy cases. The apparent consolidation gain is partly coverage collapsing onto its cleanest survivors, not merging getting better.

The interpretation to confront is that t = 0.10 is close to the identity transform. It collapses only about 17% of the pool’s 1,976 distinct names into the 1,638 clusters it emits, so “conservative threshold wins” comes dangerously close to “do almost no aggregation and you cover the most acts.” Read skeptically, the sweep is not evidence that JW recovers truth best when conservative; it is evidence that the JW step is barely earning its keep, with the raw measure pool already covering ~46/49 acts and every merge only costing coverage.
Whether the conservative choice is genuinely right therefore depends on the asymmetric cost we adopted: over-merging (collapsing two distinct RR acts into one cluster) is the costly, unrecoverable error, while under-merging (fragmentation) is cheap and recoverable downstream. Under that cost model, accepting high fragmentation at t = 0.10 to protect coverage is defensible, but only because fragmentation has been explicitly priced as cheap. The conclusion is conditional on that pricing, not unconditional. Year-±2 blocking reinforces it: by flattening the coverage curve it makes the threshold choice nearly irrelevant for coverage, leaving 0.10’s edge resting on the spurious column alone.


Reading the JW results
One reading the threshold and count subsections above do not cover is the keyword-vs-JW gate gap as a diagnostic. The JW-min ≤ 0.30 gate accepts more surface forms than the keyword gate, so it always covers more RR acts: by roughly 27–29 percentage points unblocked (t = 0.10–0.25, falling to 6 pp at t = 0.30) and 10–18 points under year-±2 blocking (in raw acts, 3–16 of 49). That gap measures how much of JW’s coverage is recovery-by-near-canonical-match — typographic variants and short abbreviations within JW distance 0.30 — rather than recovery-by-aggregation. Because JW single-linkage never mints a canonical name (it just keeps whatever surface forms it sees), the bonus here is almost entirely near-duplicate recovery, not renaming.
This is the comparison the benchmark exists to enable. A stateful method that emits one canonical name per cluster (Method 5) should open this gate gap much wider, because its coverage will come from genuine renaming rather than lexical luck. The JW ladder is the floor that gain is measured against.


Bottom line for Method 1
JW single-linkage cannot canonicalise this measure pool on its own. The geometric ceiling from Figure 5 is binding: most of the surface-form pairs that ought to merge sit beyond JW distance 0.30, geometrically out of reach for JW at any threshold considered. The RR-aligned ladder confirms it: at every variant the spurious rate stays above 86%, fragmentation ranges from 1.0 to 7.5 (1.5–3 under the keyword gate, but 4.8–7.5 under the JW gate at conservative thresholds), and even the best variant misses ~8 of 49 RR acts under the keyword gate. The gap between ERTA and Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 is semantic, not lexical, and JW has no machinery to bridge it.
What JW remains useful for is the role its mechanics actually support: a precision-friendly blocker — a cheap pre-filter that catches near-duplicate surface forms (spacing, punctuation, abbreviation expansion) at near-zero false-positive cost. Method 4’s hybrid pipeline below uses exactly this property: JW (or a cheap analog) prunes the pair space, and a semantic verifier handles the merges that surface similarity cannot. The load-bearing canonicalisation work, though, has to come from a method that understands meaning — Methods 2–5 are the candidate replacements.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Embedding-geometry probe (F16 vs FP32)
Method 1 closed on the observation that the gap to canonicalise this pool is semantic, not lexical. The next three sections test whether multilingual embeddings can bridge it. Before reading the eval, one preliminary: the Methods 2/3 embeddings come from a community F16 port of intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct (jeffh/intfloat-multilingual-e5-large-instruct:f16) running locally under Ollama. F16 compresses the reference FP32 weights and could in principle distort the embedding geometry that HDBSCAN relies on. Two cheap probes on the gold eval pool check that the port preserves the semantic structure HDBSCAN needs.
The probes run on a 2×2 grid: precision (F16 local vs FP32 reference via DeepInfra) × eval slice (full unambiguous pool vs Tier 1 only). The FP32 reference disentangles two distinct failure modes the F16-only probe couldn’t separate: “F16 quantization broke the geometry” (FP32 passes where F16 fails) vs “the model itself can’t do this task” (both precisions fail). The Tier 1 slice in turn strips Tier 2’s permissive IKA tagging, which §“Eval-set noise probe” below shows is genuinely noisy. Reading the four cells together discriminates quantization noise, model-capacity ceiling, and label noise.


Show code
dplyr::bind_rows(
  dplyr::mutate(c0_f16_quantization_probe,       Precision = "F16 (local Ollama)",  `Eval slice` = "Full pool (Tier 1 + 2)"),
  dplyr::mutate(c0_f16_quantization_probe_tier1, Precision = "F16 (local Ollama)",  `Eval slice` = "Tier 1 only"),
  dplyr::mutate(c0_fp32_reference_probe,         Precision = "FP32 (DeepInfra)",    `Eval slice` = "Full pool (Tier 1 + 2)"),
  dplyr::mutate(c0_fp32_reference_probe_tier1,   Precision = "FP32 (DeepInfra)",    `Eval slice` = "Tier 1 only")
) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Precision,
    `Eval slice`,
    `Top-1 NN same-act rate`    = sprintf("%.3f", top1_nn_same_act_rate),
    `Median within-act cosine`  = sprintf("%.3f", median_within_act_cosine),
    `Median between-act cosine` = sprintf("%.3f", median_between_act_cosine),
    `Separation`                = sprintf("%.3f", separation),
    `Eval names`                = n_eval_names,
    `Eval acts`                 = n_eval_acts
  ) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 9: Embedding-geometry probe. A 2×2 (precision × eval slice) grid. Top-1 NN same-act rate is the share of eval names whose nearest-neighbour by cosine shares a gold act (restricted to acts with ≥2 members so the metric is well-defined). Separation = median within-act cosine − median between-act cosine; positive means the embedding preserves the act-level structure. F16 rows come from the local Ollama port (jeffh/intfloat-multilingual-e5-large-instruct:f16); FP32 rows from DeepInfra-hosted intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct. Same instruction prefix on both.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Precision
                Eval slice
                Top-1 NN same-act rate
                Median within-act cosine
                Median between-act cosine
                Separation
                Eval names
                Eval acts

                  Full pool (Tier 1 + 2)

F16, Tier 1: separation 0.017 (floor 0.05), top-1 NN rate 0.308 (floor 0.40) — FAIL
FP32, Tier 1: separation 0.017 (floor 0.05), top-1 NN rate 0.269 (floor 0.40) — FAIL
Reading Tier 1 vs Tier 1 keeps the gold-partition noise floor constant on both rows so the F16↔︎FP32 contrast is apples-to-apples.
Verdict — embedding-model ceiling, not quantization. Both F16 and FP32 fail the same floors, so the issue is not F16 specifically — multilingual-e5-large-instruct itself does not produce the relative cosine spread the floors require on this pool. Two interpretations to weigh: (i) E5-instruct embeddings live in a tight band of the unit sphere by design, and the 0.05/0.40 floors may be inappropriate for this family; (ii) the model is genuinely under-resolving fiscal-act semantics and a stronger or differently-tuned embedding family is needed. Either way, the FP32 migration is not the fix. Read Methods 2/3 below as the empirical end-to-end test — if HDBSCAN’s Tier 1 numbers are competitive with JW, the floors were the over-strict reading.
E5-family embeddings always live in a narrow band of the unit sphere, so absolute cosines aren’t crisp — the probe targets the relative gap between within-act and between-act pairs, which is the property HDBSCAN cares about. Methods 2 and 3 below continue to read c0_us_embeddings (the F16 matrix); the FP32 numbers above are diagnostic only.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
section: Eval-set noise probe: surface-year vs doc-year mismatch
text:
Eval-set noise probe: surface-year vs doc-year mismatch
An alternative explanation for a soft F16 verdict — and a noise source the within-act geometry from §Method 1 already hinted at — is that the gold pairs themselves are temporally noisy. If C1 sometimes catches contemporary discussions of acts passed decades earlier (e.g. a 2010 ERP paragraph referring back to the Revenue Act of 1964), the resulting (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair will look lexically distant — not because the model misclassified, but because the surface form drifts as the act becomes historical context rather than current policy. Figure 9 probes this directly: for each eval tuple whose surface form contains a 4-digit year, plot the gap between the document’s publication year and the year referenced in the surface form against the JW distance to the gold label.


Show code
year_pattern <- "\\b(19|20)\\d{2}\\b"

chunk_doc_year <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
    dplyr::distinct(chunk_id, doc_year = year)

noise_diag <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name, chunk_id, tier) |>
    dplyr::left_join(chunk_doc_year, by = "chunk_id") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
        surface_year = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(measure_name, year_pattern)),
        year_diff = doc_year - surface_year,
        jw_distance = stringdist::stringdist(measure_name, gold_act_name,
            method = "jw"
        ),
        tier_label = factor(tier,
            levels = c(1L, 2L),
            labels = c("Tier 1", "Tier 2")
        )
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(surface_year), !is.na(doc_year))

ggplot(noise_diag, aes(year_diff, jw_distance, colour = tier_label)) +
    geom_hline(
        yintercept = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30),
        linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_vline(
        xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted",
        colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.6) +
    geom_smooth(aes(colour = tier_label),
        se = FALSE,
       linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
    scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
    labs(
        x = "Doc year − surface-referenced year",
        y = "JW distance (surface name → gold label)",
        colour = NULL
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")





Figure 9: Temporal mismatch in eval pairs. Each point is one (measure_name, gold_act_name, chunk_id) tuple whose surface form contains a 4-digit year. x = doc publication year − year referenced in the surface form (large positive ⇒ retrospective reference); y = JW distance from surface form to gold label. Tuples whose surface form has no extractable year are omitted.








Figure 10 repeats the panel on the semantic axis: y is cosine distance between the surface-name and gold-label embeddings (E5-multilingual F16, L2-normalised — so cosine distance = 1 − dot product, on the same scale as the F16 probe). The interpretation flips relative to JW: if retrospective references drift only lexically (paraphrase) but stay semantically anchored, the cloud should stay flat as x grows; if they drift semantically as well (different terminology or focus when describing the act decades later), the cloud should rise. Reading the two figures together separates the two failure modes.


Show code
surface_in_emb <- intersect(noise_diag$measure_name,
                            rownames(c0_us_embeddings))
gold_in_emb    <- intersect(noise_diag$gold_act_name,
                            rownames(c0_us_gold_embeddings))

noise_diag_cos <- noise_diag |>
  dplyr::filter(measure_name %in% surface_in_emb,
                gold_act_name %in% gold_in_emb)

S <- c0_us_embeddings[noise_diag_cos$measure_name, , drop = FALSE]
G <- c0_us_gold_embeddings[noise_diag_cos$gold_act_name, , drop = FALSE]
noise_diag_cos$cos_distance <- 1 - rowSums(S * G)

ggplot(noise_diag_cos, aes(year_diff, cos_distance, colour = tier_label)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.6) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = "Doc year − surface-referenced year",
       y = "Cosine distance (surface name → gold label)",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")





Figure 10: Eval-pair temporal mismatch (cosine). Same panel as Figure 9 but with cosine distance (E5-multilingual F16 embeddings, L2-normalised) on y. Pairs are dropped when either the surface form or the gold label is absent from its respective embedding matrix.








Finally, Figure 11 puts the two distance measures head-to-head on the full eval pool (no year filter): one point per distinct (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair, x is the JW distance, y is the cosine distance. The correlation answers whether the embedding is buying us anything JW couldn’t already give us — a strong positive correlation would mean E5 is essentially re-deriving lexical similarity, while a weak correlation means the two distance measures disagree on which pairs are similar, and there is genuine semantic signal in the embedding above and beyond surface overlap. Both Pearson (linear) and Spearman (rank) correlations are reported because the JW–cosine relationship is plausibly nonlinear.


Show code
pair_min_tier <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::group_by(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
  dplyr::summarize(tier = min(tier, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  dplyr::mutate(tier_label = factor(tier, levels = c(1L, 2L),
                                    labels = c("Tier 1", "Tier 2")))

distance_pairs <- pair_min_tier |>
  dplyr::filter(measure_name  %in% rownames(c0_us_embeddings),
                gold_act_name %in% rownames(c0_us_gold_embeddings)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(jw_distance = stringdist::stringdist(
    measure_name, gold_act_name, method = "jw"))

S <- c0_us_embeddings[distance_pairs$measure_name, , drop = FALSE]
G <- c0_us_gold_embeddings[distance_pairs$gold_act_name, , drop = FALSE]
distance_pairs$cos_distance <- 1 - rowSums(S * G)

cor_pearson  <- stats::cor(distance_pairs$jw_distance,
                           distance_pairs$cos_distance, method = "pearson")
cor_spearman <- stats::cor(distance_pairs$jw_distance,
                           distance_pairs$cos_distance, method = "spearman")

ggplot(distance_pairs, aes(jw_distance, cos_distance, colour = tier_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("label", x = Inf, y = -Inf,
           label = sprintf("Pearson  r = %.2f\nSpearman ρ = %.2f\nn = %d pairs",
                           cor_pearson, cor_spearman, nrow(distance_pairs)),
           hjust = 1.05, vjust = -0.2, size = 3.2,
           label.size = 0, fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.7)) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = "JW distance (surface name → gold label)",
       y = "Cosine distance (surface name → gold label)",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")





Figure 11: JW vs cosine distance. For every distinct (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair in the eval pool. Colour marks the cleanest tier in which the pair appears (Tier 1 if at least one chunk pairs the surface form with the gold act via verbatim match). Pairs whose surface form or gold label is missing from its embedding matrix are dropped. Annotated correlations include the full pool; the dashed line is an unweighted linear fit.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 2 — Multilingual embedding + HDBSCAN
Switching from string distance to embedding distance is what the within-act geometry from §Method 1 demanded. Method 2 embeds each measure name with intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct, L2-normalises the vectors so cosine ranking equals euclidean ranking, then runs HDBSCAN over the resulting distance matrix. Three min_cluster_size values are swept — 2 (most permissive), 3, and 5 (close to the median gold-act size). Singleton “noise” points HDBSCAN refuses to merge are kept as their-own-clusters; under the RR-aligned eval they contribute to the spurious rate denominator (singleton clusters with no RR-act match raise spurious; singletons that do contain a canonical name still match).


Show code
shape_hdb_rr_ladder <- function(gate_name, blocking_filter) {
  format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      `min_cluster_size` = as.integer(
        stringr::str_extract(variant_id, "(?<=_m)\\d+(?=_)")),
      .blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                 "yw2", "unblocked")
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(.blocking == blocking_filter) |>
    dplyr::arrange(`min_cluster_size`) |>
    dplyr::select(-variant_id, -.blocking) |>
    dplyr::relocate(`min_cluster_size`)
}

render_hdb_rr_ladder <- function(ladder) {
  ladder |>
    tinytable::tt() |>
    tt_theme_report()
}

Right: coloured by gold act for names in the unambiguous eval pool; names outside the eval pool in grey. Same 2D coordinates in both panels.









Reading the HDBSCAN results
The headline comparison is Table 10 against Table 7: if embeddings buy us what JW couldn’t, HDBSCAN should hold or improve coverage while collapsing the spurious-rate gap. The UMAP scatter Figure 12 is diagnostic — predicted clusters that overlap a single gold colour on the right panel are correct merges; predicted clusters spanning multiple gold colours are over-merges (note: the gold panel uses c0_eval_gold_pairs so the colour palette inherits the gold-pool noise documented in §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R” — read it as a relative-structure visualisation, not as ground truth on which clusters are correct). The §Synthesis at the bottom of the notebook applies the precision-friendly shortlist rule (lowest spurious, then highest coverage) to pick a candidate variant across methods.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 3 — Year-blocked variants
Method 3 reruns the same HDBSCAN sweep with the year-window mask from §Method 1: pairs whose chunk years differ by more than 2 are forced to maximum distance and so cannot merge. The prior is that the same act tends to surface in a narrow temporal window, so the mask should reduce cross-decade over-merges without sacrificing coverage.
Partial redundancy with the match gate. The RR-aligned eval already applies a year ±2 window between cluster representative year and year_signed, so a cluster that would have over-merged across decades will fail the year gate anyway and not match the wrong act. Year-blocking at clustering time and year ±2 at match time are not the same operation, though: clustering-time blocking prevents the merge from happening (the cluster doesn’t form), while match-time blocking lets the merge happen but disqualifies the resulting cluster from claiming an act of the wrong year. So year-blocking still helps by reducing the count of cross-decade clusters that would otherwise raise the spurious rate denominator. The expected pattern: spurious rate drops modestly under year-blocking; coverage and fragmentation hold.

Hyperparameter sweep (Methods 2 + 3)
The four no-UMAP tables above are six variants — min_cluster_size ∈ {2, 3, 5} crossed with {unblocked, year ±2}. title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Methods 2 + 3 with UMAP dimensionality reduction
The F16 and FP32 embedding-geometry probes above both failed the separation ≥ 0.05 and top-1 NN same-act rate ≥ 0.40 floors on the gold pool. The verdict text considered two interpretations — F16 quantization noise, and the embedding-model ceiling — but did not weigh a third: high-dimensional distance concentration. intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct returns 1024-dimensional vectors, and the unblocked HDBSCAN above operates on the full-dimensional cosine distance matrix. Density-based clustering is known to degrade above k≈50 dim through hubness and farthest-to-nearest ratio compression; the standard fix in the BERTopic / Top2Vec neighbourhood is to interpose UMAP between the embedding and the clusterer, collapsing the ambient dim to ~5–10 and (optionally) packing within-cluster points tightly via min_dist = 0. This section ports that recipe into Methods 2 + 3 and reads the with-vs-without delta against the no-UMAP baseline above.
The sweep grid is the cartesian product of three UMAP hyperparameters with the existing HDBSCAN grid:

n_neighbors ∈ {5, 15, 30} — uwot default 15; the small end emphasises local structure, the large end emphasises global structure.
n_components ∈ {2, 5, 10} — uwot default 2; BERTopic’s standard is 5; 10 is the upper end of clustering practice.
min_dist ∈ {0.0, 0.01, 0.1} — uwot default 0.01; 0.0 is BERTopic’s tight-packing default; 0.1 matches the existing diagnostic UMAP plot at Figure 12.
Inner sweep matches the no-UMAP variants: min_cluster_size ∈ {2, 3, 5} and year_window ∈ {unblocked, ±2}.

That gives 27 UMAP cells × 6 HDBSCAN cells = 162 variants. Metric "cosine" is fixed (the input embeddings are L2-normalised); UMAP is reduced once per UMAP cell and reused across the inner HDBSCAN sub-cells. The seed is fixed at the target level (run_hdbscan_umap_clusters_grid(seed = 20260526L)) so the entire sweep is deterministic under tar_make(). Distance metric inside HDBSCAN switches from cosine on the raw embeddings to Euclidean on the UMAP output (UMAP does not preserve L2-normalisation, so cosine on the reduced space is not meaningful); the year-window mask uses a max(d) + 1 sentinel instead of the cosine 1.0 cap.


Show code
# Parse the four sweep dimensions out of the variant_id once so every chunk
# below can read them by name.
parse_umap_variant <- function(metrics_or_clusters_df) {
  metrics_or_clusters_df |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      umap_n_neighbors  = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=nn)\\d+")),
      umap_n_components = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=nc)\\d+")),
      umap_min_dist     = as.numeric(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=md)\\d+\\.\\d+")),
      min_cluster_size  = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=_m)\\d+(?=_)")),
      blocking          = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                         "year ±2", "unblocked")
    )
}




Show code
# Per-(blocking, min_cluster_size) winner under the precision-friendly rule
# rewritten for the RR-aligned framework: lowest spurious rate first, then
# highest coverage. Returns variant_ids to join back against the formatted
# ladder for display.
pick_umap_rr_winners <- function(gate_name) {
  spurious_per_variant <- c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "spurious_rate", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, spurious = value)
  coverage_per_variant <- c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "coverage", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, coverage = value)
  spurious_per_variant |>
    dplyr::inner_join(coverage_per_variant, by = "variant_id") |>
    parse_umap_variant() |>
    dplyr::group_by(blocking, min_cluster_size) |>
    dplyr::arrange(spurious, dplyr::desc(coverage), .by_group = TRUE) |>
    dplyr::slice_head(n = 1L) |>
    dplyr::ungroup() |>
    dplyr::select(variant_id, blocking, min_cluster_size,
                  umap_n_neighbors, umap_n_components, umap_min_dist)
}

assemble_umap_rr_shortlist <- function(gate_name) {
  winners <- pick_umap_rr_winners(gate_name)
  formatted <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics,
                                       gate = gate_name)
  winners |>
    dplyr::inner_join(formatted, by = "variant_id") |>
    dplyr::arrange(blocking, min_cluster_size) |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      `Blocking`         = blocking,
      `min_cluster_size`,
      `n_neighbors`      = umap_n_neighbors,
      `n_components`     = umap_n_components,
      `min_dist`         = sprintf("%.3f", umap_min_dist),
      Coverage, Spurious, Fragmentation, `Year alignment`,
      `n clusters`
    )
}

Figure 15: Embedding-geometry probe in UMAP space. F16 vs FP32, the same model precisions contrasted in Table 9 at no-UMAP. Columns facet the two probe metrics; rows facet embedding precision (F16 = local Ollama community port, FP32 = DeepInfra-hosted reference). The F16 panels (top row) reproduce the earlier UMAP read; the FP32 panels (bottom row) add the missing precision contrast in UMAP space. Each bar counts UMAP cells (out of 27) whose probe value falls in the bin; fill marks eval slice (Full pool = Tier 1 + 2 unambiguous, Tier 1 = verbatim-passage matches only). Dashed lines are the F16/FP32 floors carried over from Table 9: 0.40 for top-1 NN; 0 for separation (Euclidean — the 0.05 cosine floor at Table 9 does not translate). Identical UMAP grid, seed (20260526), and probe logic across the four panels; the only thing that varies between F16 and FP32 rows is the input embedding matrix.









Hyperparameter marginal scan (UMAP grid)
The 162-variant UMAP grid sweeps four knobs at once — three UMAP hyperparameters (n_neighbors, n_components, min_dist) crossed with min_cluster_size, all under {unblocked, year ±2}. Figure 16 and Figure 17 scan that grid one knob at a time: each column is a hyperparameter, the x-axis steps through its three swept values, and every point is one of the variants holding that value while the other three knobs vary. Because three knobs vary at every x-position, each column is a marginal view — the spread of points at a given x is the variation induced by the other three knobs, and the crossbar is the median over them. Rows are outcomes (as in Method 1), colour is year-blocking, and the two gates split across sub-panels. A knob that mattered would tilt its column’s medians; a flat column means the front does not move along that knob — the empirical read developed in §“Reading the UMAP results” below.


Show code
# UMAP marginal-scan plotter. Pivots the four swept knobs into one long column
# so facet_grid(outcome ~ knob, scales = "free") gives a one-knob-at-a-time
# marginal view; at each x-value the other three knobs vary, shown as jittered
# points with a median crossbar. kind = "headline" reads the four rate metrics;
# kind = "counts" reads the three cluster counts.
plot_umap_rr_scan <- function(gate_name, kind = c("headline", "counts")) {
  kind <- match.arg(kind)
  base <- c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics |>
    dplyr::filter(gate == gate_name) |>
    parse_umap_variant()

  if (kind == "headline") {
    dat <- base |>
      dplyr::filter(metric %in% c("coverage", "spurious_rate",
                                  "fragmentation_index", "year_alignment")) |>
      dplyr::transmute(
        variant_id, blocking, y = value,
        facet_row = factor(metric,
          levels = c("coverage", "spurious_rate",
                     "fragmentation_index", "year_alignment"),
          labels = c("Coverage ↑", "Spurious rate ↓",
                     "Fragmentation ↓", "Year alignment ↑")),
        n_neighbors = umap_n_neighbors, n_components = umap_n_components,
        min_dist = umap_min_dist, min_cluster_size)
  } else {
    dat <- base |>
      dplyr::distinct(variant_id, blocking,
                      n_clusters, n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched,
                      umap_n_neighbors, umap_n_components,
                      umap_min_dist, min_cluster_size) |>
      tidyr::pivot_longer(
        cols = c(n_clusters, n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched),
        names_to = "facet_row", values_to = "y") |>
      dplyr::transmute(
        variant_id, blocking, y,
        facet_row = factor(facet_row,
          levels = c("n_clusters", "n_clusters_matched", "n_rr_acts_matched"),
          labels = c("n clusters", "Clusters matched", "RR acts matched")),
        n_neighbors = umap_n_neighbors, n_components = umap_n_components,
        min_dist = umap_min_dist, min_cluster_size)
  }

  long <- dat |>
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols = c(n_neighbors, n_components, min_dist, min_cluster_size),
      names_to = "hyperparameter", values_to = "hp_value") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      Blocking = dplyr::if_else(blocking == "year ±2", "Year ±2", "Unblocked"),
      hyperparameter = factor(hyperparameter,
        levels = c("n_neighbors", "n_components", "min_dist", "min_cluster_size")),
      hp_level = factor(hp_value, levels = sort(unique(hp_value))))

  ggplot(long, aes(hp_level, y, colour = Blocking)) +
    geom_point(position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.18,
                                               dodge.width = 0.7),
               alpha = 0.22, size = 0.6) +
    stat_summary(aes(group = Blocking), fun = median,
                 fun.min = median, fun.max = median, geom = "crossbar",
                 position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
                 width = 0.55, linewidth = 0.4) +
    facet_grid(facet_row ~ hyperparameter, scales = "free", switch = "y") +
    scale_colour_manual(values = hdb_rr_blocking_pal) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, colour = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "top", strip.placement = "outside",
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
          axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))
}
