Points are exact counts (no bootstrap CI); the crossbar is the median over the marginalized knobs.





Reading the UMAP results
The headline reading is empirical: across the 162 UMAP variants in c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics, no UMAP variant strictly dominates the no-UMAP HDBSCAN baseline on the coverage/spurious front under either gate. The shortlist tables above pick the best UMAP cell per (min_cluster_size × year-blocking) sub-cell by the asymmetric-cost rule (lowest spurious, then highest coverage); the picks land at comparable spurious rates and comparable coverage to the corresponding no-UMAP rows in Table 10 and Table 12. UMAP-PF (the precision-friendly UMAP winner overall) does cut the singleton share in the cluster-count distribution from 38% (no-UMAP baseline hdb_m2_yw2) to 14%, but most of the singletons it removes are noise points that wouldn’t have matched any RR act anyway. Two independent diagnostics explain why the front doesn’t move.
First, UMAP improves the aggregate embedding geometry but not the neighbour identity HDBSCAN and the name gates actually use. Figure 15 shows within-vs-between separation moves dramatically — F16 raw 0.005 → UMAP median 0.379, a ≈70× lift, with positive separation in 27/27 F16 cells and 26/27 FP32 cells. Top-1 NN same-act rate barely moves: F16 raw 0.261 → UMAP cells span [0.18, 0.31], and zero of 108 probe cells (54 F16 + 54 FP32 across both eval slices) clear the 0.40 floor. The F16 ↔︎ FP32 contrast on top-1 NN is flat, which resolves the verdict left open by the no-UMAP F16/FP32 probe in §“Embedding-geometry probe”: the bottleneck is the embedding model, not F16 quantization. UMAP cleans aggregate geometry without changing the literal nearest neighbour, and the literal NN is what HDBSCAN’s density-based clustering and the RR-aligned name gates both depend on.
Second, the upstream gold-pool ceiling documented in §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R” still binds the RR-aligned eval, just differently. The new framework removes the chunk-tier act_name aggregation noise that capped pairwise recall against c0_eval_gold_pairs, but coverage against R&R’s 49-act list is still capped by whether HDBSCAN finds ≥1 surface form per act that carries enough of the canonical name to satisfy the gates. Tiny clusters that fragment off the abbreviated forms (ERTA, P.L. 97-34) without merging back to the canonical fail both gates regardless of UMAP arrangement. The earlier UMAP draft’s third hypothesis (that n_components would be the load-bearing UMAP knob) remains empirically wrong on the RR-aligned eval too: no UMAP knob moves the front meaningfully because the front is not where UMAP’s lever points. BERTopic-style UMAP-before-density-clustering is therefore not the load-bearing C0 architecture.
Implications for Method 4. The same diagnosis predicts the hybrid LLM pairwise judge will break through some of the gap but not all of it. Genuine surface variants (ERTA ↔︎ Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 ↔︎ P.L. 97-34) that HDBSCAN scattered by 1024-D cosine-distance concentration will resolve once the judge sees the candidate pairs — top-k embedding retrieval should surface them as candidates even at F16 (top-1 may miss but top-5/10 will catch). The judge cannot manufacture canonical names from scratch, though, so methods that emit canonicals (Method 5) will look better on the keyword gate than methods that only verify pairs (Method 4); the keyword-vs-JW gap in the §Synthesis ladder will surface that distinction.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
section: Method 4 — Hybrid (embedding nearest-neighbours + LLM pairwise judge)
text:
Method 4 — Hybrid (embedding nearest-neighbours + LLM pairwise judge)






WarningPhase B pending



The hybrid pipeline (embedding top-k → Haiku YES/NO verifier → union-find) lands once Phase B is built. Expected to dominate the cost/quality frontier: cheap blocker + targeted LLM verification only on the candidate pairs that survive embedding similarity.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 5 — LLM canonical clustering
Method 5 is the semantic bridge Methods 1–3 cannot build on their own (§“What this means for Method 1”). It is an unsupervised clustering method: Haiku is shown the full pool of distinct rank-1 surface forms — each with a representative year — and returns, for every name, a cluster key (surface_id → cluster_id). It never sees R&R’s act list; the gold labels enter only in evaluation, through the same match_clusters_to_rr_acts() + evaluate_rr_matches_grid() gates Methods 1–3 are scored under. The model emits a grouping of ids, never strings, so there is nothing to fuzzy-match on the output side and the members the gates score are the original C1 surface forms — M5 is graded apples-to-apples with the lexical and embedding methods, with no free credit for re-emitting canonical names. The bespoke clustering instruction lives in c0_canonicalize.yml; canonical-name selection and the reshape to the standard cluster table are deterministic R in R/c0_aggregator.R (run_m5_llm_clusters()).
Iteration 1 is the barebones cell of the design: Haiku, single-shot, names + representative year, five fixed-seed input shuffles. The five seeds are the order-sensitivity probe (the M5 analog of C1/C2’s Test IV order-invariance test): each seed reorders the input list, becomes its own variant_id, and is scored independently, so seed-to-seed metric spread and partition agreement quantify how much the answer rides on input order.

Context-window headroom
Before spending any tokens, Figure 18 confirms the single-shot call clears both the context window and our output budget. The id-only output schema keeps the response compact — the model echoes one short {id, cluster} record per name rather than the full surface string — so even though it returns one record per surface form, the projected output stays well under max_tokens. Output is estimated at ~14 tokens per record (the JSON structure costs roughly twice the chars/4 heuristic, a correction the iteration-1 pilot surfaced when a 4,096-token budget truncated the response).


Show code
m5_input_nb <- prepare_m5_input(c0_us_measure_pool)
instr_nb    <- yaml::read_yaml(here("prompts/c0_canonicalize.yml"))$instruction
user_msg_nb <- build_m5_user_message(m5_input_nb)
N_nb        <- nrow(m5_input_nb)
tok         <- function(s) ceiling(nchar(s) / 4)

budget_df <- tibble::tibble(
  component = factor(
    c("Instruction (system)", "Surface-form list (input)",
      "Projected output (id → cluster)"),
    levels = c("Projected output (id → cluster)",
               "Surface-form list (input)", "Instruction (system)")),
  kind   = c("Input", "Input", "Output"),
  tokens = c(tok(instr_nb), tok(user_msg_nb), N_nb * 14L)
)

ggplot(budget_df, aes(tokens, component, fill = kind)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(tokens)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 8192, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#CC6677") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2e5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#332288") +
  annotate("text", x = 8192, y = 3.5, label = "max_tokens 8,192",
           colour = "#CC6677", size = 2.8, angle = 90, vjust = -0.4, hjust = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 2e5, y = 3.5, label = "200K context",
           colour = "#332288", size = 2.8, angle = 90, vjust = -0.4, hjust = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma,
                expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.18))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Input = "#4477AA", Output = "#117733")) +
  labs(x = "Tokens (log scale)", y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 18: M5 token budget. Approximate tokens for the system instruction, the surface-form input list (chars/4), and the projected id→cluster output (~14 tokens per pretty-printed JSON record), against our max_tokens output budget (8,192) and the Haiku/Sonnet 200K context window (both dashed). Log x-axis. Input sits at ~2% of the window; output sits below the configured budget — single-shot is feasible at iteration 1’s pool size.










Partition integrity
Table 16 is the first reliability gate: did the model return a clean partition of all 294 input ids on each seed? Missing ids are filled as singleton clusters (recoverable, under-merge), extra/hallucinated ids are dropped. Non-zero Missing or Extra columns, or a non-end_turn stop reason, flag output that the model truncated or drifted on.


Show code
attr(c0_m5_clusters, "integrity") |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Seed = seed,
    `Names expected`     = n_expected,
    `Returned`           = n_returned,
    `Missing → singleton` = n_missing,
    `Extra (dropped)`    = n_extra,
    Parsed               = parse_ok,
    `Stop reason`        = stop_reason
  ) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()

Order sensitivity
Table 17 reports the adjusted Rand index between every pair of the five seed partitions. ARI = 1 means input order has no effect on the clustering; lower values mean early-listed names anchor clusters the later order would have drawn differently. This is the headline robustness number for a single-shot LLM clusterer.

  dplyr::transmute(
    `Seed variants`  = n_variants,
    `Seed pairs`     = n_pairs,
    `Mean ARI`       = sprintf("%.3f", mean_ari),
    `Min ARI`        = sprintf("%.3f", min_ari),
    `Max ARI`        = sprintf("%.3f", max_ari)
  ) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()

The same two-gate ladder used for Methods 1–3, one row per seed. Frequency as an unsupervised RR signal
In deployment there is no ground truth, so we need a corpus-internal property that ranks which clusters merit expert review. The hypothesis: R&R acts are significant legislated fiscal changes, and significance tracks how often an act is discussed, so RR-matched clusters should sit higher in the per-cluster frequency distribution than the spurious tail. The two diagnostics below test that separability on the US corpus (where RR labels exist) for a single seed (s1); coverage is seed-stable so the seed choice does not move the marks. This is a qualitative separability look, not a fitted threshold — what transfers to Malaysia is the signal, not a cutoff.


Show code
# One row per M5 cluster (seed s1): corpus frequency (distinct chunks),
# RR-match status under each gate, and name distance to the nearest RR act.
# Read-only over already-loaded targets. The match table holds only clusters
# that passed at least one gate, so min_jw is recomputed from canonical names
# to cover every cluster (incl. the unmatched ~80%).
m5_seed <- "m5_haiku_namesyear_s1"
m5_match_palette <- c(
    "unmatched"           = "#cccccc",
    "jw only"             = "#4477AA",
    "keyword only"        = "#CC6677",
    "both (keyword + JW)" = "#882255"
)

m5_clusters_s1 <- c0_m5_clusters |> dplyr::filter(variant_id == m5_seed)

m5_freq <- m5_clusters_s1 |>
    dplyr::select(cluster_id, measure_name) |>
    dplyr::left_join(dplyr::distinct(c0_us_measure_pool, measure_name, chunk_id),
        by = "measure_name"
    ) |>
    dplyr::group_by(cluster_id) |>
    dplyr::summarise(n_chunks = dplyr::n_distinct(chunk_id), .groups = "drop")

m5_gate <- c0_m5_rr_matches |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == m5_seed) |>
    dplyr::group_by(cluster_id) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
        matched_keyword = any(match_keyword),
        matched_jw = any(match_jw), .groups = "drop"
    )

m5_cluster_diag <- m5_clusters_s1 |>
    dplyr::distinct(cluster_id, canonical_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(min_jw = apply(
        stringdist::stringdistmatrix(canonical_name, c0_us_rr_acts$act_name,
            method = "jw"
        ), 1, min
    )) |>
    dplyr::left_join(m5_freq, by = "cluster_id") |>
    dplyr::left_join(m5_gate, by = "cluster_id") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
        matched_keyword = dplyr::coalesce(matched_keyword, FALSE),
        matched_jw = dplyr::coalesce(matched_jw, FALSE),
        match_cat = factor(
            dplyr::case_when(
                matched_keyword & matched_jw ~ "both (keyword + JW)",
                matched_keyword ~ "keyword only",
                matched_jw ~ "jw only",
                TRUE ~ "unmatched"
            ),
            levels = names(m5_match_palette)
        )
    )




Show code
m5_cluster_diag |>
    ggplot(aes(n_chunks, fill = match_cat)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 22, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
    geom_rug(
        data = ~ dplyr::filter(.x, matched_keyword | matched_jw),
        sides = "b", colour = "#882255", alpha = 0.5,
        inherit.aes = FALSE, mapping = aes(x = n_chunks)
    ) +
    scale_x_log10() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = m5_match_palette) +
    labs(
        x = "Distinct chunks referencing cluster (log scale)",
        y = "Clusters", fill = NULL
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 19: M5 cluster frequency by RR match. Distinct US chunks referencing each M5 cluster (seed s1, log x), stacked by Romer–Romer match gate; the rug marks individual RR-matched clusters. Unmatched clusters pile at one to two chunks while RR-matched clusters dominate the high-frequency tail.










Show code
m5_cluster_diag |>
  mutate(
    matched_keyword = factor(
      matched_keyword,
      levels = c(T, F),
      labels = c("Matched", "Unmatched")
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(
    aes(
      n_chunks,
      color = matched_keyword
    )
  ) +
  stat_ecdf() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 30, 5)) +
  labs(
    x = "Chunks associated",
    color = "Keyword gate"
  )





Figure 20: Canonicalized act-names by frequency of appearence in US corpus. Matched and unmatched by the keyword gate.








Figure 20 shows that up to 85% of unmatched act-names—acts that were surfaced by C0 but are not found in Romer and Romer (n.d.) ’s set of fiscal shocks—are only found in at most two chunks inside the whole C1 test sample. At the same time, fewer than 10% of the matched acts are found in 2 or fewer chunks. This is to say that, if we chose to chop the “tree” of acts surfaced by C0 by their occourance in the chunks, we would only lose 10% of matched acts and only keep 15% of the unmatched acts.


Show code
m5_data <- m5_cluster_diag |>
  arrange(desc(n_chunks), desc(cluster_id)) |>
  mutate(
    per_acts = 1,
    cum_acts = cumsum(per_acts),
    per = n_chunks / sum(n_chunks),
    cum_per = cumsum(per),
    cum_false_positive = cumsum(per_acts * (1 - matched_keyword)),

    
  ) |>
  arrange(n_chunks, cluster_id) |>
  mutate(
    cum_false_negative = cumsum(matched_keyword * per_acts)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(n_chunks)) 

m5_data |>
  pivot_longer(c(starts_with("cum_false_"),  "cum_acts")) |>
  mutate(
    name = factor(
      name,
      levels = c("cum_false_positive", "cum_false_negative", "cum_acts"),
      labels = c("False Positive", "False Negative", "Total acts chosen")
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(cum_per, value, color = name)) +
  geom_line() +
 scale_y_continuous(
  # labels = scales::percent,
  breaks = seq(0, 100, 10)
  ) +
 coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 50), xlim = c(0.05, 1)) +
 scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, breaks = seq(0, 100, 10) / 100) +
 # geom_vline(xintercept = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    x = "Chunk frequency threshold",
    y = "# Surfaced acts",
    color = ""
  )





Figure 21: Trade-off of frequency based identification. Cumulative errors (as un/matched by keyword gate) across frequency thresholds. Canonicalized act names are arranged by their frequency in chunks








Figure 21 shows the trade-off curves in terms of false positive and false negative canonicalized act names. Keeping the 70% most frequently used canonical act names will produce 10 false negatives and 6 false positives.


Show code
m5_cluster_diag |>
  ggplot(aes(n_chunks, min_jw, colour = match_cat)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.30, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 1.8) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_colour_manual(values = m5_match_palette) +
  labs(x = "Distinct chunks referencing cluster (log scale)",
       y = "Min JW distance to any RR act name", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 22: M5 frequency vs name distance. Each M5 cluster (seed s1): corpus frequency (log x) against the smallest Jaro–Winkler distance from its canonical name to any of the 49 R&R act names. The dashed line is the 0.30 JW name gate; the keyword gate (term + year) shows by colour, not a line. Distance is name-only and year-agnostic, so a low-distance point can still be year-wrong — hence the trustworthy keyword gate is encoded separately. RR-matched clusters form an L: low name distance and high frequency.








Both diagnostics support the hypothesis. The unmatched mass concentrates at one to two chunks, RR-matched clusters carry the high-frequency tail, and in the scatter the RR-matched clusters fall to low name distance and high frequency while the spurious tail sits at single-chunk frequency and large name distance. Frequency alone will not isolate RR-grade acts — RR clusters do appear in the single-chunk bin and some spurious clusters reach moderate frequency — but as a triage ranking for expert review it clearly demotes the spurious tail. Quantifying the separation (an AUC or precision–recall on frequency) and a persistence axis (distinct years and documents) are the natural next steps.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method comparison ladder

Performance frontier
The ladder tables below collapse each method to a single “best” variant (lowest spurious, then highest coverage). Figure 23 shows the full picture that pick is drawn from: every one of the 183 implemented variants as a point in coverage–spurious space, per gate, with the pooled efficient frontier (the non-dominated envelope — maximum coverage achievable at each spurious level) drawn over them. This is the honest “how well can the methods perform” answer for objective (i): a frontier, not a point.
Two structural facts read straight off it. Within JW and within no-UMAP HDBSCAN there is no coverage–spurious tradeoff — one variant dominates all others of its method on both axes, so each contributes a single frontier point; the only genuine tradeoff curve is the UMAP grid. And the three methods occupy different regions: JW reaches the highest coverage (≈0.84) but only at spurious ≥0.95; HDBSCAN + UMAP owns the low-spurious end (down to ≈0.60) at comparable coverage. The deployment pick in Table 20 is just the lowest-spurious corner of this frontier — a different cost ratio (false merges per missed act) would pick a different corner.


Show code
# Pool all 183 implemented variants (JW + HDBSCAN + HDBSCAN/UMAP + LLM M5) into one
# coverage-vs-spurious table per gate, for the cross-method performance
# frontier (objective i). Read-only over the already-loaded RR metrics targets.
method_frontier_palette <- c(
  "JW (Method 1)"         = "#332288",
  "HDBSCAN (Methods 2+3)" = "#CC6677",
  "HDBSCAN + UMAP"        = "#44AA99",
  "LLM (Method 5)"        = "#882255")

collect_rr_wide <- function() {
  one <- function(df, lbl) {
    df |>
      dplyr::filter(metric %in% c("coverage", "spurious_rate")) |>
      dplyr::select(variant_id, gate, metric, value,
                    n_clusters, n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched) |>
      tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = value) |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        Method = lbl,
        Blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                  "Year ±2", "Unblocked"))
  }
  dplyr::bind_rows(
    one(c0_jw_rr_metrics,           "JW (Method 1)"),
    one(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics,      "HDBSCAN (Methods 2+3)"),
    one(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics, "HDBSCAN + UMAP"),
    one(c0_m5_rr_metrics,           "LLM (Method 5)")
  ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      Method = factor(Method, levels = names(method_frontier_palette)),
      gate = factor(gate, levels = c("keyword", "jw"),
                    labels = c("Keyword gate", "JW-min ≤ 0.30 gate")))
}

# Non-dominated set: minimise spurious_rate, maximise coverage. Sorted by
# ascending spurious, a point joins the frontier iff its coverage exceeds the
# best coverage seen at any lower-or-equal spurious.
pareto_front <- function(df) {
  d <- df |> dplyr::arrange(spurious_rate, dplyr::desc(coverage))
  keep <- logical(nrow(d)); best <- -Inf
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(d))) {
    if (d$coverage[i] > best) { keep[i] <- TRUE; best <- d$coverage[i] }
  }
  d[keep, , drop = FALSE]
}

method_rr_wide <- collect_rr_wide()
method_rr_front <- method_rr_wide |>
  dplyr::group_by(gate) |>
  dplyr::group_modify(~ pareto_front(.x)) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()




Show code
ggplot(method_rr_wide, aes(spurious_rate, coverage)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = Method, shape = Blocking),
             alpha = 0.35, size = 1.5) +
  geom_line(data = method_rr_front, colour = "grey25", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(data = method_rr_front, aes(colour = Method, shape = Blocking),
             size = 2.8) +
  facet_wrap(~gate, nrow = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = method_frontier_palette) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Unblocked" = 16, "Year ±2" = 17)) +
  labs(x = "Spurious rate (lower is better)",
       y = "Coverage (higher is better)", colour = NULL, shape = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 23: Coverage–spurious performance frontier. All 183 implemented variants (10 JW + 6 HDBSCAN + 162 HDBSCAN/UMAP + 5 LLM M5 seeds) in coverage (↑ better) × spurious-rate (← better) space, per name gate. Colour: method; shape: year-blocking. The grey line is the pooled efficient frontier (non-dominated envelope across all methods); enlarged points sit on it. JW and no-UMAP HDBSCAN each contribute a single frontier point (one variant dominates the rest of its method); the UMAP grid supplies the tradeoff curve at low spurious; the five M5 seeds show the LLM’s order-sensitivity scatter. Point estimates only (CIs omitted for legibility; see the ladder tables).








Figure 24 decomposes the same variants into the raw count geometry behind the rates: x is how many emitted clusters carry an RR match, y is how many of the 49 RR acts are covered, and point area is the total cluster budget (n clusters) — the denominator that drives the spurious rate. The dashed line is y = x (one matched cluster per covered act, no fragmentation among matches); points to its right are fragmented (several clusters matching the same act). JW sits far right with the largest points — it covers many acts but emits a vast cluster budget and fragments heavily; HDBSCAN + UMAP clusters near the line with small budgets, the count-space signature of its low spurious rate.


Show code
ggplot(method_rr_wide,
       aes(n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched, colour = Method)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
              colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(aes(size = n_clusters, shape = Blocking), alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~gate, nrow = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = method_frontier_palette) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Unblocked" = 16, "Year ±2" = 17)) +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 9, breaks = c(50, 200, 500, 1000, 1700)) +
  labs(x = "Clusters carrying an RR match",
       y = "RR acts covered (of 49)",
       size = "n clusters", colour = NULL, shape = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "right", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Figure 24: Match-count geometry. Same 183 variants, per gate. x: clusters carrying an RR match. y: RR acts covered (of 49). Point area: total clusters emitted (n clusters, the spurious-rate denominator). Colour: method; shape: year-blocking. Dashed line is y = x (no fragmentation among matched clusters); points to its right fragment one act across several clusters. Large points far right (JW) = big budget + heavy fragmentation; small points near the line (UMAP) = lean budget.










Show code
# Asymmetric-cost picker: lowest spurious_rate, then highest coverage,
# under the requested name gate. Numeric sort, not lexicographic on the
# formatted ladder cells.
pick_best_by_rr <- function(metrics_long, gate_name) {
  sp <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "spurious_rate", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, spurious = value)
  cv <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "coverage", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, coverage = value)
  sp |>
    dplyr::inner_join(cv, by = "variant_id") |>
    dplyr::arrange(spurious, dplyr::desc(coverage)) |>
    dplyr::slice_head(n = 1L) |>
    dplyr::pull(variant_id)
}

build_method_comparison <- function(gate_name) {
  best_jw       <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_jw_rr_metrics, gate_name)
  best_hdb      <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics, gate_name)
  best_hdb_umap <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics, gate_name)
  best_m5       <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_m5_rr_metrics, gate_name)

  jw_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_jw_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_jw) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "JW (best variant)")
  hdb_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics,
                                     gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_hdb) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "HDBSCAN (best variant)")
  hdb_umap_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics,
                                          gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_hdb_umap) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "HDBSCAN + UMAP (best variant)")
  m5_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_m5_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_m5) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "LLM Method 5 (best seed)")

  dplyr::bind_rows(jw_row, hdb_row, hdb_umap_row, m5_row) |>
    dplyr::rename(Variant = variant_id) |>
    dplyr::relocate(Method, Variant)
}




Show code
build_method_comparison("keyword") |> tt() |> tt_theme_report()

Four rows now — JW, no-UMAP HDBSCAN, UMAP+HDBSCAN, and LLM (Method 5, best seed) — picked by the asymmetric-cost rule within each method. Reading across the rows: if UMAP+HDBSCAN dominates the no-UMAP row on coverage with comparable-or-lower spurious, BERTopic-style dim-reduction is the load-bearing improvement. If JW wins on spurious but trails on coverage (the expected pattern given the within-act JW geometry), JW remains a precision-friendly blocker candidate for Method 4 rather than a deployable method on its own. Method 5 is the test of whether an LLM’s semantic grouping clears the coverage/spurious frontier the lexical and embedding methods are stuck behind; its row here is the best of five shuffle seeds, so read it together with the seed spread in Table 18 (the best-seed pick is optimistic). Method 4 will extend this table once it lands.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Out-of-distribution: Malaysia EN/BM






WarningPhase B pending



Once Phase B is built, the best variant from each method is applied to the Malaysia EN+BM measure pool (malay_er_c1_measures); we report the fraction of malay_er_curated_matches EN↔︎BM pairs that land in the same predicted cluster. Methods that rely on surface similarity (JW) are expected to fail here because the language families are different; multilingual embedding methods should hold up.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Synthesis






NoteMethod 4 still pending



A final method + parameter recommendation waits for Method 4 to be evaluated. Methods 1–3 (JW, HDBSCAN unblocked, HDBSCAN year-blocked) and Method 5 (LLM canonical clustering) are now wired against the RR-aligned eval; Method 4 (hybrid embedding-NN + LLM pairwise judge) remains to land.
Until then, the shortlist below applies the precision-friendly rule from §Framing to each finished method in isolation: among variants with the lowest spurious rate, pick the one with the highest coverage. The load-bearing comparison is now Method 5’s best seed against the JW and HDBSCAN picks — whether the LLM’s semantic grouping clears the coverage/spurious frontier the lexical and embedding methods are stuck behind.




Show code
# Top-N variants under (spurious asc, coverage desc) for a given metrics
# tibble and gate. Returns a wide tibble carrying numeric `spurious_num` and
# `coverage_num` keys so downstream callers can re-sort across multiple
# shortlists (e.g. when union-ing HDBSCAN and HDBSCAN+UMAP) before dropping
# the numeric keys for display via finalize_rr_shortlist().
build_rr_shortlist <- function(metrics_long, gate_name, top_n = 5L) {
  sp <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "spurious_rate", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, spurious_num = value,
                     sp_lo = ci_lo, sp_hi = ci_hi,
                     n_clusters, n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched)
  cv <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "coverage", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, coverage_num = value,
                     cv_lo = ci_lo, cv_hi = ci_hi)
  sp |>
    dplyr::inner_join(cv, by = "variant_id") |>
    dplyr::arrange(spurious_num, dplyr::desc(coverage_num)) |>
    utils::head(top_n) |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      variant_id,
      spurious_num, coverage_num,
      Spurious           = sprintf("%.3f [%.3f, %.3f]",
                                    spurious_num, sp_lo, sp_hi),
      Coverage           = sprintf("%.3f [%.3f, %.3f]",
                                    coverage_num, cv_lo, cv_hi),
      `n clusters`       = n_clusters,
      `Clusters matched` = n_clusters_matched,
      `RR acts matched`  = n_rr_acts_matched
    )
}

finalize_rr_shortlist <- function(shortlist) {
  shortlist |> dplyr::select(-spurious_num, -coverage_num)
}

If a UMAP+HDBSCAN variant displaces no-UMAP HDBSCAN at the top, deploy with UMAP for Malaysia. If JW dominates spurious but trails coverage, JW is the natural blocker candidate for Method 4 — the hybrid pipeline reconciles the spurious-vs-coverage trade by routing JW’s precision-friendly candidate pairs through a semantic verifier.

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, qs2)

here::i_am("notebooks/cit_identification.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
set_theme(theme_minimal())

# Full Malaysia C1 measure pool (pre-relevance-cut deployment output)
c1 <- tar_read(country_c1_measures)[[1]]

cit_kw <- stringr::regex(
  "corporat|company tax|companies tax|cukai pendapatan syarikat|cukai syarikat|single.tier|cukai korporat",
  ignore_case = TRUE
)
n_pool <- nrow(c1)
n_cit  <- c1 |> dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(measure_name, cit_kw)) |> nrow()

# Frozen, human-stamped CIT shock dataset (produced by the /identify-cit skill).
# Loaded here so both the recall scorecard (@sec-recall) and the structured
# dataset (@sec-frozen) render from the single source of truth when it exists.
frozen_path <- here("data", "validated", "MY_CIT_shocks.qs")
have_frozen <- file.exists(frozen_path)
if (have_frozen) cit_frozen <- qs2::qs_read(frozen_path)
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
1 Purpose
This note documents a focused, AI-assisted manual identification of Malaysia’s statutory corporate-income-tax (CIT) rate changes, run directly over the evidence our deployment pipeline already surfaced. It is the “Malaysia CIT seed” called for in the feasibility note’s proposed sequencing, and it deliberately follows the spirit of the narrative-identification literature (Romer and Romer 2010): close reading of source documents, with the LLM pipeline as a retrieval-and-extraction aide, not as the adjudicator.
The method mirrors our /manual-analysis protocol. The pipeline (C1 measure identification, C0 act aggregation) pre-screens candidate measures; a human reads the surfaced evidence and makes the final call on what counts as an event, how scattered surface forms consolidate, and whether each cut is exogenous. Claude pattern-matches against the source text; the researcher confers meaning.
We do not drive identification from C0. As Section 2 shows, C0 fragmented the CIT measures badly, so it cannot serve as the event layer here. We retrieve from the full C1 pool (1036 measures, pre-relevance-cut) and consolidate by hand.






Important



The exogenous/endogenous column in Table 3 is a preliminary suggestion with its supporting quote, pending expert adjudication. It is not a research finding. The whole point of this pass is that a human adjudicates these calls; see the open questions in Section 6.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
section: 2 What C1 surfaced, and what it missed
text:
2 What C1 surfaced, and what it missed
A keyword scan of the full C1 pool returns 29 distinct CIT measures spanning 1981–2019, in both English and Malay, each carrying explicit from-and-to rates. A broader semantic sweep over all 1036 measures (reading every tax-related surface form, not just keyword hits) confirmed the headline path is present from 34% down to 25%, and surfaced the separate SME, petroleum, and sectoral-incentive tracks.
The sweep also found two genuine recall misses: headline events that sit in the corpus with explicit rates but that C1 did not surface as measures. Both were recovered by reading the source text directly.


Show code
# Single source of truth: render the frozen dataset's recall scorecard once it
# exists; otherwise fall back to the narrative scorecard below.
recall <- if (have_frozen) {
  cit_frozen$recall_scorecard[[1]] |>
    dplyr::rename(Stage = stage, Outcome = outcome)
} else {
  tibble::tribble(
    ~Stage,                         ~Outcome,
    "C1 measure identification",    "Surfaced 6 of 8 headline rate changes (34→32 through 26→25) plus the single-tier reform",
    "Recall miss — 2016 cut",  "25→24 (YA2016) not surfaced as a measure; recovered from Budget Speech 2015 and Economic Report 2016",
    "Recall miss — 2022 levy", "Cukai Makmur one-off surcharge not surfaced; recovered from Budget Speech 2022 and Economic Report 2023",
    "C0 act aggregation",           "Fragmented the CIT measures: 27 keyword measures → 27 clusters (near-all singletons); 2007–2009 phased cut split across ~10 clusters; EN/BM never bridged"
  )
}

recall |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 1: Recall scorecard. How each headline event fared through C1 measure identification and C0 act aggregation. ‘Recovered manually’ marks events present in the corpus that C1 did not surface as a measure.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Stage
                Outcome
              
        
        
        
                
                  Keyword scan (C1 pool, n=1,036)
                  29 keyword hits over CIT regex; 2 false positives excluded (Malaysia Deposit Insurance Corporation Act 2005 x2).
                
                
                  Semantic sweep (all surface forms)
                  Confirmed headline path 34->24 plus separate SME / petroleum / sectoral-incentive tracks (tracked separately, not headline rows).
                
                
                  C1 measure identification
                  Surfaced the 34->25 path (steps H1-H6) and the single-tier reform; MISSED the 2016 cut (25->24) and the 2022 Cukai Makmur surcharge.
                
                
                  Recall miss - 2016 cut (25->24, YA2016)
                  Recovered from Budget Speech 2015 ch5 (BM: 'daripada 25 peratus kepada 24 peratus') and Economic Report 2016 ch13 ('Corporate income tax rate reduced from 25% to 24%').
                
                
                  Recall miss - 2022 Cukai Makmur (YA2022)
                  Recovered from Budget Speech 2022 ch18 ('one-off special tax known as Cukai Makmur ... companies other than MSMEs ... above RM100 million ... 33% tax rate') and Economic Report 2023 ch7-8 ('Prosperity Tax amounting to RM7 billion ... for assessment year 2022').
                
                
                  C0 act aggregation
                  Fragmented the CIT measures (near-all singletons; phased cut split across ~10 clusters; EN/BM never bridged). NOT used as the event layer.
                
        
      
    






The retrieval lesson is the one anticipated in the feasibility note: a CIT cut is maximally relevant because it is a rate change, however briefly the documents discuss it, so an instrument-targeted recall pass (plus human reading) is needed rather than a discussion-volume filter or trust in C0’s clustering.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
3 The headline statutory CIT rate
Reading the consolidated evidence yields a clean headline path: 34 → 32 → 30 → 28 → 27 → 26 → 25 → 24, a structural single-tier reform in 2008, and a one-off windfall surcharge in 2022. Table 2 gives the identified events; Figure 1 traces the rate.


Show code
headline <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Event, ~`From→To`,       ~`Δpp`, ~`Effective (YA)`, ~`Announced`,            ~`Source surface form / doc`,
  "H1",   "34% → 32%",      "−2",   "~1994",           "early-1990s Budget",    "ECON Report 1995 (BM)",
  "H2",   "32% → 30%",      "−2",   "~1995/96",        "mid-1990s Budget",      "ECON Report 1995/96; RMK-7",
  "H3",   "30% → 28%",      "−2",   "1998",            "1998 Budget (Oct 1997)", "BNM AR 1997",
  "H4",   "28% → 27%",      "−1",   "2007",            "2007 Budget (Sep 2006)", "RMK-9 MTR; BNM AR 2006",
  "H5",   "27% → 26%",      "−1",   "2008",            "2007 Budget",           "ECON Report 2008 (BM)",
  "H6",   "26% → 25%",      "−1",   "2009",            "2008 Budget (Sep 2007)", "BNM AR 2007; ECON Report 2010",
  "H7",   "25% → 24%",      "−1",   "2016",            "2015 Budget (Oct 2014)", "Budget Speech 2015; ECON Report 2016",
  "H\\*",   "single-tier system", "structural", "2008 (phased)",  "2008 Budget",           "Budget Speech 2008",
  "H8",   "Cukai Makmur +9pp\non income > RM100m", "+9 (one-off)", "2022", "2022 Budget (Oct 2021)", "Budget Speech 2022; ECON Report 2023"
)

headline |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 2: Headline CIT-rate events. Identified statutory general corporate-income-tax rate changes, with the budget that announced each and the year of assessment it took effect. Effective years for the early-1990s steps are approximate, bracketed by the ‘from 34 per cent in 1991 to 30 per cent in 1995’ summary in the Seventh Malaysia Plan.

Show code
rate_path <- tibble::tribble(
  ~year, ~rate,
  1991,  34,
  1994,  32,
  1995,  30,
  1998,  28,
  2007,  27,
  2008,  26,
  2009,  25,
  2016,  24,
  2023,  24
)

ggplot(rate_path, aes(year, rate)) +
  geom_step(direction = "hv", linewidth = 0.8, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_point(data = dplyr::filter(rate_path, year != 2023), size = 2) +
  annotate("point", x = 2022, y = 33, shape = 17, size = 3, colour = "firebrick") +
  annotate("text", x = 2022, y = 33, label = "  Cukai Makmur\n  (one-off, >RM100m)",
           hjust = 0, vjust = 0.5, size = 3, colour = "firebrick") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1), limits = c(22, 35)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, 2025, 5)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Statutory CIT rate")





Figure 1: Headline statutory CIT rate, Malaysia 1991–2023. Step path of the general corporate-income-tax rate from the identified events. The 2022 Cukai Makmur surcharge (one-off, on chargeable income above RM100m) is marked separately and is not part of the standing-rate path.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
4 Motivation evidence and preliminary exogeneity
For each headline event, the corpus carries motivation language. Table 3 pairs each event with the most diagnostic quote and a preliminary exogenous/endogenous read. The central methodological observation: the 2007–2009 phased cut carries both framings in the same corpus, which is exactly why the classification is consequential rather than mechanical.


Show code
exo <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Event, ~`Motivation quote (source text)`,                                                                                 ~`Preliminary read`, ~Adjudication,
  "H3",   "\"reduce the cost of doing business and strengthen competitiveness ... reduced to 28% in 1998 compared with 30% each for Indonesia and Thailand\"", "Exogenous (competitiveness)", "PENDING",
  "H4",   "\"complaints that the national corporate tax rate of 28 per cent is high compared with other countries\" (2003 ctx)", "Exogenous (competitiveness)", "PENDING",
  "H5",   "phased competitiveness reduction (same act path as H4/H6)",                                                        "Exogenous (competitiveness)?", "PENDING",
  "H6",   "\"the phased reduction in corporate income tax to 26% in 2008 and 25% in 2009 is expected to provide stimulus for private sector capital spending\"", "Endogenous (stimulus framing)?", "PENDING",
  "H7",   "announced within the GST-introduction offset package (Budget 2015)",                                              "Exogenous (structural/GST offset)?", "PENDING",
  "H8",   "\"one-off special windfall tax to companies that generate high income\" (post-pandemic revenue measure)",          "Endogenous? (one-off windfall)", "PENDING"
)

exo |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

These are suggestions anchored in the quoted text, not settled classifications; the exogeneity calls are the object of expert adjudication (see Section 6).

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
5 Other corporate-rate tracks
Per the agreed scope, all corporate-rate instruments are tracked, marked separately, with the headline rate as the priority. Table 4 lists the non-headline tracks for completeness; these are candidates for the same adjudication once the headline series is settled.


Show code
tracks <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Track,        ~`Identified events`,                                                                                        ~Note,
  "SME schedule", "28%→20% (2003 Budget, first RM100k); threshold RM100k→500k (2004); 20%→19% (2016); 19%→18% (2017); 18%→17% (2019/2020)", "Distinct small-company rate; tracks but lags the headline",
  "Petroleum income tax", "40%→38% (1998 Budget, bundled with H3); PITA self-assessment change (2011)", "2011 change is administrative, not a rate shock",
  "Sectoral / special-rate", "DEB equity-restructuring 5pp exemption (1981); Labuan offshore 3% / RM20k regime; biomass companies (2001); Principal Hub 10% (2019); pharma/vaccine 0–10% (2021); manufacturing-relocation 15% (2023)", "Incentive/concessionary regimes, not the standing statutory rate"
)

tracks |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 4: Non-headline corporate-rate tracks. SME schedule, petroleum income tax, and sectoral/special-rate regimes surfaced by C1 and the recall sweep.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Track
                Identified events
                Note
              
        
        
        
                
                  SME schedule
                  28%→20% (2003 Budget, first RM100k); threshold RM100k→500k (2004); 20%→19% (2016); 19%→18% (2017); 18%→17% (2019/2020)
                  Distinct small-company rate; tracks but lags the headline
                
                
                  Petroleum income tax
                  40%→38% (1998 Budget, bundled with H3); PITA self-assessment change (2011)
                  2011 change is administrative, not a rate shock
                
                
                  Sectoral / special-rate
                  DEB equity-restructuring 5pp exemption (1981); Labuan offshore 3% / RM20k regime; biomass companies (2001); Principal Hub 10% (2019); pharma/vaccine 0–10% (2021); manufacturing-relocation 15% (2023)
                  Incentive/concessionary regimes, not the standing statutory rate
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
6 Open adjudication questions
Three calls are genuinely the researcher’s to make; they are flagged here and were adjudicated at the /identify-cit stamp (the frozen dataset in Section 7 encodes the decisions).






TipResolved at freeze (2026-06-25)




Consolidation grain. The 2007–2009 phased cut (28→27→26→25) is frozen as three annual-effective rows (MY-CIT-04/05/06) for the local-projection design; the early-1990s reduction (34→32→30) is frozen as two rows (MY-CIT-01/02), treating each step as its own budget announcement. No row carries a phased_schedule.
Exogeneity on the 2007–2009 steps. Frozen as ambiguous, with the competitiveness quote on MY-CIT-04 and the stimulus quote on MY-CIT-06, preserving the dual framing as the object of expert adjudication. These preliminary reads are carried alongside — never replacing — C2b’s downstream label.
Scope of the single-tier reform and Cukai Makmur. Both kept in scope: the 2008 single-tier reform as a Neutral structural row (MY-CIT-07, rate fields NA) and the 2022 Cukai Makmur as a one-off Hike (MY-CIT-09, flagged in magnitude_note).

The narrative below is retained as the reasoning record behind these calls.








Note1. Consolidation of the 2007–2009 phased cut (H4–H6)



Is the 28→27→26→25 reduction one narratively-announced phased act (Budget 2007 legislated the first steps; Budget 2008 added the 2009 step), dated at announcement with a known path? Or three annual-effective shocks for the local-projection design? The narrative tradition leans to one act; the estimation may want annual rows. The same question applies, more weakly, to the early-1990s 34→32→30 steps.








Note2. Exogenous vs endogenous on H4–H6



The identical phased cut is framed in the corpus as competitiveness (“28% is high vs other countries”) and as stimulus (“provide stimulus for private sector capital spending”). This is the project’s central identifying separation, and it is genuinely ambiguous here. Adjudicating it is the methodologically interesting contribution, not overhead.








Note3. Scope calls on H7, H8, and the single-tier reform



Is the 2016 cut exogenous because it offsets the GST introduction (a structural reform), or does the deficit-financing context pull it toward consolidation-driven (still exogenous, different reason)? Is the 2022 Cukai Makmur in scope at all as a statutory-rate shock, given it is a one-off surcharge on a subset of firms? And does the 2008 single-tier reform belong in a rate-shock series, given it changes the dividend-imputation system rather than the headline rate?
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory CIT-Rate Shocks
text:
7 Structured frozen dataset
The narrative identification above is the human-curated content; the machine-readable form is frozen by the /identify-cit skill to data/validated/MY_CIT_shocks.qs, following the shared contract in docs/phase_1/tax_shock_schema.md (one row per announced act × tax type, with a member_chunks manifest linking each shock to its corpus chunks and a mandatory recall scorecard). That frozen file — not this notebook’s tribbles — is the reference input the deployment pipeline binds and feeds to C2a/C2b for motivation and exogeneity (tar_read(tax_shocks)).
Section 2 above is the recall scorecard for this pass. The frozen dataset and its per-shock member-chunk manifest render below once the skill has produced it.

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, stringr, purrr, fixest)

here::i_am("notebooks/deployment.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
source(here("R/malay_consistency.R")) # pretty_motivation() + motivation palette
source(here("R/deployment_report.R"))
theme_set(theme_minimal())

tar_load(c(
    country_chunks,
    country_c1_measures,
    country_c0_clusters,
    country_c0_acts,
    country_c2b,
    country_measure_pool
))

# Positionally zip the country identity onto every branched target, then flatten.
chunks <- bind_country(country_chunks, country_chunks)
c1_meas <- bind_country(country_c1_measures, country_chunks)

m <- compute_deployment_tallies(
    c0_acts      = bind_country(country_c0_acts, country_chunks),
    c0_clusters  = bind_country(country_c0_clusters, country_chunks),
    c2b          = bind_country(country_c2b, country_chunks),
    measure_pool = bind_country(country_measure_pool, country_chunks)
)

inv <- m$inventory
n_country <- dplyr::n_distinct(chunks$country_iso)
inv_pending <- nrow(inv) == 0L

# Relevance-based "chosen acts" cut (80% of relevance), computed per country.
# `eligible` = enacted, in-range acts (the relevance-figure + tbl-top-20 base);
# `chosen`   = the top acts that accumulate 80% of relevance (marginals + timeline).
inv_chosen <- mark_chosen_acts(inv)
eligible   <- dplyr::filter(inv_chosen, !is.na(relevance))
chosen     <- dplyr::filter(inv_chosen, chosen)

# Headline scalars for the inline prose (single-country today; aggregates if more).
n_total_acts  <- nrow(inv)
n_chosen_acts <- nrow(chosen)
yr_lo <- if (nrow(chunks)) min(chunks$year) else NA_integer_
yr_hi <- if (nrow(chunks)) max(chunks$year) else NA_integer_

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
Framing
This is the deployment headline: the end-to-end output of the validated C1 → C0 → C2 pipeline run on each deployment country’s full document corpus. It presents the same figures and tables as the Malaysia EN/BM consistency report, but with the comparison axis swapped from language to country.
The pipeline runs independently per country. C1 identifies fiscal measures, C0 aggregates measure names into acts, and C2 classifies each act’s motivation, sign, and exogeneity. Today only Malaysia is deployed, so every comparison figure shows a single country; the notebook auto-extends to a panel per country as Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, and Vietnam are onboarded.


This is a deployment inventory, not a validity test. There are no ground-truth labels for any deployment country; the act-level outputs are inputs requiring expert validation (Phase 2 success criteria: ≥80% expert agreement on C1, ≥70% on C2), not research findings.
Each country’s acts are clustered within that country only. We do not attempt to merge acts across countries: a Malaysian GST and a future Indonesian GST are distinct national measures, so there is no cross-country analog of the consistency report’s joint EN/BM merge probe. The per-country C0 cluster summary below takes that slot.

Scope
Figure 1 shows each country’s corpus extent: pages and chunks per document year, summed across that year’s documents. As more countries are added, comparable totals indicate comparable corpus depth; large gaps flag coverage asymmetries that bound everything downstream.


Show code
ps <- plot_deployment_scope(chunks)
if (is.null(ps)) cat("No chunk data available yet.\n") else print(ps)





Figure 1: Corpus scope. Pages and chunks per country and document year, summed across that year’s documents.








Table 1 reports the headline tally per country: the document-year span analysed, the corpus size (documents, pages, chunks), the fiscal measures C1 surfaces, the acts C0 clusters them into, and how many of those acts C2 classifies as exogenous.


Show code
scope_tally <- compute_deployment_scope_tally(
  chunks       = chunks,
  c1_measures  = c1_meas,
  c0_summary   = m$c0_summary,
  counts       = m$per_country$counts
)

if (nrow(scope_tally) == 0L) {
  tibble(Note = "Deployment targets not yet built") |> tt() |> tt_theme_report()
} else {
  scope_tally |>
    transmute(
      Country = str_to_title(country),
      `Years analysed` = years,
      Documents = n_docs,
      Pages = n_pages,
      Chunks = n_chunks,
      `Surfaced fiscal measures` = n_surfaced,
      `Clustered fiscal measures` = n_clustered,
      `Exogenous fiscal measures` = n_exogenous
    ) |>
    tt() |>
    tt_theme_report()
}

text:
C1-step comparability
Before aggregation, what set of fiscal measures does C1 surface for each country? This is where real extraction differences live, independent of how acts are later aggregated. Figure 2 counts the distinct fiscal measures C1 surfaces per year, by country.


Show code

if (is.null(pc)) cat("No measures available yet.\n") else print(pc)





Figure 2: Surfaced fiscal measures per year, by country (pre-aggregation). The extraction load that bounds any downstream aggregation.

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
C0 aggregation diagnostics
C0 collapses C1 measure names into act-level clusters. The figures below describe that aggregation per country.

Merge rate
Figure 3 shows the merge rate, \((\text{surfaced measures} / \text{distinct acts}) - 1\), by year and country, averaged across the year’s documents. Zero means no merging (every surface form is its own act); higher means more variants absorbed per act.


Show code
pd0 <- plot_deployment_c0_perdoc(m$per_doc)
if (is.null(pd0)) cat("No per-document aggregation available yet.\n") else print(pd0)





Figure 3: C0 merge rate (surfaced measures per act − 1) per year and country. Higher bars mean more measure variants absorbed into each canonical act.










Per-country cluster summary
How many distinct acts does C0 produce for each country, and how many measure variants does each act absorb on average? Table 2 is the per-country analog of the consistency report’s joint merge-rate probe.


Show code
if (nrow(m$c0_summary) == 0L) {
  tibble(Note = "C0 clusters not yet built") |> tt() |> tt_theme_report()
} else {
  m$c0_summary |>
    transmute(
      Country = str_to_title(country),
      `Measure variants` = n_variants,
      `Acts (clusters)` = n_clusters,
      `Merge rate` = round(n_variants / n_clusters - 1, 2),
      `N singletons` = n_singletons,
      `Largest cluster` = max_members
    ) |>
    tt() |>
    tt_theme_report()
}

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
Identification by frequency
Notice that the language used by Romer and Romer (n.d.) to define what constitutes as a “significant change to fiscal liabilities” over-identifies seemingly innocuous fiscal changes when operationalizing it through the LLM chain we developed. We showed whilst developing C0 that we can recover Romer and Romer (n.d.) ’s taxonomy of US fiscal changes by selecting a threshold of the most discussed acts in the corpus.
This section asks what is a sensible threshold for selecting meaningful fiscal changes that surfaced from our pipeline, that we know are over-identified. While RR’s taxonomy included 49 distinct fiscal changes spanning 58 years (from 1945 to 2003), the acts aggregated by C0 number 805 over 42 years (from 1981 to 2023). Thus we say RR’s language, as operationalized by our pipeline, over-identifies fiscal changes.
To sift through the most relevant changes, without yet knowing the fiscal magnitude of each, we recur to a frequentist approach. We use two raw variables to map on to a relevance index. First, more relevant acts should have produced more evidence items, as surfaced by C2a. Moreover, more relevant acts should also be discussed in more parts of the corpus. So, we will want the acts for which we have a lot of evidence to extract their motivation from, and that were more widely discussed in the government documents.
Notice in Figure 6 that, some acts have just one piece of evidence extracted from one chunk, in all likelihood, these acts are false positives (e.g. insignificant acts that RR would not have identified as significant enough); these acts should score the lowest with our relevance measure. On the other extreme, a single act surfaced some 250 evidence items from almost 50 different chunks; this act is most certainly important enough that RR would have identified it, so it should get the highest available score. Consider the following Cobb-Douglass relevance function
\[
\text{relevance} = \text{evidence items} \cdot \text{chunks addressed}
\]
Then, how is relevance accumulated by act? Figure 7 shows that 16 acts accumulate 80% of the relevance, as defined above.


Show code
# `eligible` (from mark_chosen_acts in the setup chunk) already carries, per
# country: relevance = n_evidence_items * n_chunks, relevance_per, cum_relevance,
# acts_chosen (rank), and the chosen flag — over enacted, in-range acts only.
headline <- eligible



Show code
headline |>
  mutate(Country = str_to_title(country)) |>
  ggplot(aes(n_chunks, n_evidence_items)) +
  geom_jitter(aes(size = relevance_per, color = chosen)) +
  facet_wrap(~ Country) +
  scale_x_log10(n.breaks = 10) +
  scale_y_log10(n.breaks = 10) +
  scale_size_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    x = "Chunks (log scale)",
    y = "Evidence items (log scale)",
    size = "% Relevance",
    color = "Chosen"
  )
origin <- headline |>
  distinct(country, country_iso) |>
  mutate(acts_chosen = 0, cum_relevance = 0)

bind_rows(origin, headline) |>
  mutate(Country = str_to_title(country)) |>
  ggplot(aes(cum_relevance, acts_chosen)) +
  geom_step() +
  facet_wrap(~ Country) +
  scale_y_log10(n.breaks = 20) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, n.breaks = 6, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.8, linetype = "dashed", color = "darkgrey") +
  labs(
    x = "% relevance accumulated",
    y = "Acts chosen (log scale)"
  )











Figure 6: A measure of relevance. Relevance as a mapping of evidence items and chunks were the act was found.













Figure 7: Choosing acts according to accumulated relevance. The dashed line marks the 80% relevance cut.









Show code
headline |>
  mutate(Country = str_to_title(country)) |>
  ggplot(aes(year, fill = chosen)) +
  geom_bar() +
  facet_wrap(~ Country) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_width(5)) +
  scale_y_log10(n.breaks = 6) +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Acts (log scale)",
    fill = "Chosen"
  )





Figure 8: Timeline of un/chosen acts.








Table 3 shows the chosen acts. For example, the Goods and Services Tax (GST) pools 230 evidence items drawn from 46 chunks spanning 7 EN/BM surface forms that C0 merged into one act; C2b read that consolidated evidence and classified the act’s motivation as deficit-driven (revenue increase).


Show code
chosen |>
  arrange(desc(relevance)) |>
  mutate(
    act_name = str_remove(act_name, "\\s*\\[c\\d+\\]$"),
    cum_relevance = scales::percent(cum_relevance, accuracy = 0.1),
    pred_label = pretty_motivation(pred_label)
  ) |>
  select(
    `Act name` = act_name,
    Year = year,
    Motivation = pred_label,
    `Effect` = pred_sign,
    `Evidence items` = n_evidence_items,
    `Chunks` = n_chunks,
    `Accumulated relevance` = cum_relevance
    ) |>
  tt(notes = c(
    "Year: the year parsed from the act name when it contains one, otherwise the modal year of the source documents that discuss the act.",
    "Evidence items: motivation quote-and-signal pairs C2a extracted from the documents, pooled across all of the act's chunks.",
    "Effect: the net direction C2b assigns to the act's effect on fiscal liabilities — + increase (revenue-raising), − decrease (revenue-reducing), 0 no net change."
  )) |>
  tt_theme_report()

if (nrow(chosen) == 0L) {
  cat("No chosen acts yet.\n")
} else {
  pl <- plot_deployment_c0_labels(inventory_marginals(chosen)$labels)
  if (is.null(pl)) cat("No motivation labels available yet.\n") else print(pl)
}





Figure 9: Motivation-label marginal distribution by country. How many acts each country assigns to each motivation category.

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
Headline: act inventory timeline
The pipeline produces, per country, a set of acts each carrying a C2 motivation label, sign, and exogeneity. The headline is a timeline of the chosen acts: for each year and country, acts are counted into motivation × sign cells and drawn as a diverging stacked bar. Increases grow the bar upward, decreases downward, no-change acts sit at the baseline, colour encodes motivation, faceted by country. Acts are dated by the single act-level year used throughout this section (parsed from the act name when present, else the modal source-document year).


Show code
p1 <- plot_deployment_act_timeline(chosen, timing = "year")
if (is.null(p1)) cat("No chosen acts yet.\n") else print(p1)





Figure 10: Chosen-act counts by year, by country. Diverging stacked bars: increases grow upward, decreases downward, no-change acts sit at the baseline; colour = motivation.

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
Interpretation
Read the evidence in pipeline order:

C1 step. The comparability figure describes what raw material the extractor surfaces per country. Any extraction gap here bounds everything downstream and is not fixable by the aggregator.
C0 step. The merge-rate figure and cluster summary describe how aggressively C0 collapses measure variants into acts. A country whose corpus repeats the same handful of acts across many documents merges more.
Identification by frequency. R&R’s language over-identifies through the LLM chain, so we keep the acts that accumulate 80% of relevance (evidence items × chunks). Everything from the marginals onward is restricted to that chosen set.
C2 / headline. The timeline is the deliverable: each country’s chosen acts placed by year, direction, and motivation. It is the starting point for expert review, not a finding.

title: Deployment Headline: Cross-Country Fiscal-Shock Inventory
text:
Implications
The same pipeline, codebooks, and figures apply to every country: onboarding the next one adds a corpus and a configuration entry, and this notebook gains a panel. The act inventory each country produces is the artifact an expert reviews against the Phase 2 agreement targets; comparable structure across countries (similar compression, plausible motivation marginals, no degenerate years) is the signal that the country-agnostic codebooks transferred.

# Frozen, human-stamped incentive shock dataset (produced by the
# /identify-incentives skill). Loaded here so both the recall scorecard
# (@sec-recall) and the structured dataset (@sec-frozen) render from the single
# source of truth when it exists.
frozen_path <- here("data", "validated", "MY_INCENTIVE_shocks.qs")

if (have_frozen) inc_frozen <- qs2::qs_read(frozen_path)
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Tax-Incentive Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Tax-Incentive Shocks
text:
1 Purpose
This note documents a focused, AI-assisted manual identification of changes to Malaysia’s tax incentives and holidays — tax holidays (Pioneer Status), investment and reinvestment allowances, concessionary investor rates (Labuan, Principal Hub, sectoral special rates), free/export/enterprise zones, sectoral and R&D incentives, consumption-side holidays, and personal-income reliefs — run directly over the deployment corpus. It is the incentive-side analogue of notebooks/spending_identification.qmd, and it deliberately follows the spirit of the narrative-identification literature (Romer and Romer 2010; Klemm and Van Parys 2012): close reading of source documents, with the LLM pipeline as a retrieval-and-extraction aide, not as the adjudicator.
The method mirrors our /manual-analysis protocol and centres on direct reading of the corpus (country_body / country_chunks). The skill anticipated a near-empty C1 floor (C1 is scoped to standing tax rates/liabilities), but in fact C1 surfaced 151 incentive measures — Malaysia’s Budget Speeches, Economic Reports, Bank Negara Annual Reports and Five-Year Plans discuss incentives heavily alongside tax, exactly as they do spending (the spending pass saw the same deviation). C1 therefore served as a rich first-pass scan, and direct reading served to consolidate and rate the surface forms rather than to recover missing ones. The seed of the dataset is the set of concessionary regimes the /identify-cit pass deliberately set aside. A human reads the surfaced evidence and makes the final call on what counts as an incentive change, how scattered surface forms consolidate, whether the event belongs here or in the tax/spending sets, and whether each change is exogenous. Claude pattern-matches against the source text; the researcher confers meaning.






Important



The unit is one row per legislative change to an incentive (a new scheme, a rate/duration change, a sectoral extension, a repeal) — the standing existence of a long-running scheme is not a row. Each event lives in exactly one dataset: incentives are targeted, time-bounded, or base/carve-out-rate measures; universal, permanent statutory-rate changes belong to the CIT/PIT/consumption sets (ownership-by-mechanism).








Important



The exogenous/endogenous column in Table 3 is a preliminary suggestion with its supporting quote, pending expert adjudication. Incentives skew exogenous (FDI-attraction / industrial-policy / structural-competitiveness motives are long-run, non-cyclical); only an explicit cyclical/crisis rationale reads as endogenous. It is not a research finding, and it is carried alongside — never replacing — C2b’s downstream label.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Tax-Incentive Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Tax-Incentive Shocks
section: 2 What the corpus surfaced, and what C1 missed
text:
2 What the corpus surfaced, and what C1 missed
The skill anticipated a near-empty C1 floor, but the incentive keyword scan in fact matched 151 of 1,036 C1 measures (1981–2023, EN+BM) — a deviation logged in the scorecard. The events below were consolidated and rated by reading the anchor documents directly (Budget Speeches’ incentive announcements, Economic Reports, the Promotion of Investments Act and Income Tax Act incentive schedules). Because C1 surfaced the incentives directly, recovered_chunks is ~empty for every act. The mandatory recall scorecard records what was searched and missed.


Show code
recall <- if (have_frozen) {
  inc_frozen$recall_scorecard[[1]] |>
    dplyr::rename(Stage = stage, Outcome = outcome)
} else {
  tibble::tribble(
    ~Stage,                          ~Outcome,
    "C1 keyword scan",               "Expected near-empty: C1 is scoped to standing tax rates, surfaces ~0 incentive measures",
    "Direct corpus sweep",           "Primary method — read Budget Speech incentive announcements / Economic Report / MIDA / PIA sections directly",
    "Seed list (CIT carve-outs)",    "Labuan 3%; Principal Hub 10% (2019); biomass (2001); pharma/vaccine 0–10% (2021); manufacturing-relocation 15% (2023); DEB 5pp (1981)",
    "Recall checkpoint — PIA 1986",  "Promotion of Investments Act 1986 (Pioneer Status / ITA framework) — recovered from source",
    "Recall checkpoint — zones",     "MSC / Multimedia Super Corridor and Labuan offshore regime changes — recovered from source"
  )
}

recall |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
text:
Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, stringr)

here::i_am("notebooks/malay_consistency.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
