tar_load(c(
  malay_er_pair_years,
  malay_er_chunks,
  malay_er_c1,
  malay_er_c1_measures,
  malay_er_measure_pool,
  malay_er_c0_perdoc,
  malay_er_c0_perlang,
  malay_er_c0_joint,
  malay_er_consistency_metrics
))

m <- malay_er_consistency_metrics
inv <- m$inventory
inv_pending <- nrow(inv) == 0L
c0_pending  <- nrow(malay_er_c0_perlang) == 0L

title: Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
text:
Framing
In this section, we seek to answer the question: Can our pipeline produce equivalent results with two equivalent inputs that are in two different languages? And more specifically, will including fiscal documents in other languages (Malay) yield results that are comparable to the ones we obtained in English during the development phase?
The C1 → C0 → C2 codebook pipeline was validated on US English documents. Malaysia has 38 BM-only documents in its ready corpus (primarily older Budget Speeches and Economic Reports). Before trusting LLM outputs on those, we need evidence that the pipeline produces equivalent fiscal reasoning regardless of input language.
This is a consistency test, not a validity test. We have no labels for Malaysia. EN outputs are treated as the reference because the codebooks crossed S3 gates on EN-only US data. Passing this test is necessary-but-not-sufficient for extending deployment to the BM-only-document slice (38 of 99 ready documents); it does not gate the broader Malaysia deployment, and it is not a substitute for expert agreement on the EN-side outputs (Phase 2’s success criteria: ≥80% on C1, ≥70% on C2 against expert review).
The test runs the full pipeline independently on each language and compares the outputs within each step of the process. It restricts the corpus to Economic Reports with parallel EN+BM coverage, runs its own C1 → C2a chain, then aggregates measure names into acts with the C0 act aggregator at three scopes:

per-document — aggregate within each language × year document (granular aggregation capability/limits);
per-language — pool all EN documents / all BM documents (deployment-realistic; this scope feeds C2 and the headline timeline);
joint EN+BM — pool both languages together (probes whether C0 can co-cluster the EN and BM surface forms of the same act).



This test only speaks to modern, professionally-translated MoF prose (Economic Reports 2014–2025; see the 2023 edition caveat under C1-step comparability). It does NOT speak to pre-2009 Budget Speech BM, older Economic Report BM (pre-2014), or crisis-booklet BM. Those are different vintages and registers; deployment to them requires separate evidence.

Scope
Figure 1 shows the extent to which the two language sides cover the same document: the page count and chunk count of each Economic Report year, by language. Comparable bars within a year mean we are comparing parallel documents; a large gap flags an edition or coverage asymmetry (most visibly in 2023, discussed below).


Show code
ps <- plot_malay_scope(malay_er_chunks)
if (is.null(ps)) cat("No chunk data available yet.\n") else print(ps)





Figure 1: Pages and chunks per Economic Report year, by language. Comparable EN/BM bars within a year indicate parallel coverage.

title: Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
text:
C1-step comparability
Before aggregation, does C1 surface a comparable set of raw fiscal measures on each side? This is the step where real extraction asymmetries live (independent of how acts are later aggregated). Figure 2 counts the distinct fiscal measures C1 produced per document, by language.


Show code
pc <- plot_malay_c1_comparability(malay_er_c1_measures)
if (is.null(pc)) cat("No measures available yet.\n") else print(pc)





Figure 2: Distinct C1 fiscal-measure names per year, by language (pre-aggregation). A large within-year EN/BM gap is an extraction asymmetry that bounds any downstream aggregation.








Two C1-level residuals carry over from the prior iteration’s analysis and are not aggregation artifacts: (i) some EN/BM years contain foreign-comparator measures (Japan, India, Australia stimulus packages; Philippines TRAIN Act) that should be filtered upstream by country-of-enactment tagging in C1; and (ii) 2018 BM extracted suspiciously few measures from a full Economic Report, a recall asymmetry to investigate at C1, not C0. These belong to the C1 step and bound how comparable any downstream aggregation can be.







Warning



The 2023 EN and BM documents are different editions of the 2023 budget cycle, not parallel translations. The BM side is the Oct-2022 full Tinjauan Ekonomi 2023; the EN side is the Feb-2023 re-tabled-budget Updates on Economic and Fiscal Outlook and Revenue Estimates 2023 — a condensed Outlook plus a bundled Federal Government Revenue Estimates / Treasury Memorandum block that has no BM counterpart here (or in any 2014–2022 EN file). Expect EN-heavy C1 measure counts for 2023; the drift on that row reflects document-edition asymmetry, not a language/reasoning inconsistency. See the 2026-06-05 provenance note in data/manual/malaysia/README.md.


Why the drift varies by year
The Figure 2 drift is not uniform across years; it concentrates in 2014 (the GST-announcement Budget). Two structural features of each year’s Economic Report drive it, and both peak in 2014.
First, measure density. The comparability count above is a post-filter count: the deployment filter keeps only the most-prominent (rank-1) motivated measure per chunk. Before that cut, C1 surfaces several measures per chunk, and 2014 carries roughly two to three times the motivated-fiscal-measure load of a typical year. The denser the report, the more measures the rank-1 cut discards, and the more room chunk-boundary differences between the parallel EN and BM documents have to change which measure survives on each side.


Show code
pd <- plot_malay_c1_density(malay_er_c1)
if (is.null(pd)) cat("No motivated measures available yet.\n") else print(pd)





Figure 3: Motivated fiscal measures C1 surfaces per year by language, before the rank-1 deployment filter. 2014 carries the heaviest load of any pilot year, which is what the rank-1 cut discards most and most asymmetrically.








Second, chunk-level classification asymmetry. With near-identical chunk counts across the two parallel documents, BM flags a higher share of its 2014 chunks as carrying a motivated rank-1 fiscal measure than EN does. Since each such chunk contributes exactly one measure to Table 1, this EN/BM gap is the drift, and it is widest in 2014.


Show code
pr <- plot_malay_c1_fiscal_rate(malay_er_c1, malay_er_chunks)
if (is.null(pr)) cat("No chunk data available yet.\n") else print(pr)





Figure 4: Share of each year’s chunks whose most-prominent (rank-1) measure is a motivated fiscal measure, by language. The EN/BM gap, the per-chunk asymmetry the drift reflects, is widest in 2014.

title: Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
text:
Interpretation
Read the evidence in pipeline order:

C1 step. Any large pre-aggregation drift, foreign-comparator contamination, or the 2018 BM recall gap is a C1 issue and bounds everything downstream — it is not fixable by the aggregator.
C0 step. The per-document compression figure closes the loop opened last iteration: does C0 produce symmetric act-level aggregation across languages where the previous clusterer fragmented BM more than EN? The joint merge rate separately tells us whether C0 bridges languages at all or treats them as disjoint vocabularies.
C2 / headline. The two timelines are where cross-language consistency of the full pipeline is read. Agreement looks like the same years carrying the same colours and the same up/down balance on both facets; systematic colour shifts (e.g. BM reaching for a structural label) or sign flips on the same act are the disagreements worth chasing. The act-name-year multiset and label-marginal figures are the statistical companions to the visual read.







NoteMethodological caveat: translation, not independent source



The BM Economic Reports are MoF translations of the EN versions, not independent fiscal-policy statements. Two consequences: correlated errors are invisible (if Haiku misreads a passage the same way in both languages because the translation preserved the misleading phrasing, this test records agreement); and some disagreements are translation artifacts (the translator’s word choice, not the model’s reasoning). The motivation language R&R rely on — “the actual words used by policymakers” — only fully applies to the EN side. Catching correlated errors requires expert validation on the EN side.

title: Malaysia EN/BM Cross-Language Consistency Test
text:
Implications for deployment scope
This test, conditional on its results, speaks to a narrow population of BM documents:

Speaks to: professional MoF translations of modern Economic Reports (2014+), commissioned alongside the EN versions and expected to be high-quality.
Does NOT speak to: older Economic Report BM (1995-2013, 19 documents — MoF Bahasa originals, not parallel translations), all Budget Speech BM (9 documents, 2000-2008 + 2012 + 2015 — no EN parallels, different register), or crisis-booklet BM (4 documents — branded marketing register).

The pilot window now spans the full parallel EN+BM Economic Report record (2014–2025), and the consistency test auto-includes any year for which both an EN and a BM Economic Report are available, so adding a future pair requires only supplying the two documents.

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
text:
Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, qs2)

here::i_am("notebooks/pit_identification.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
set_theme(theme_minimal())

# Full Malaysia C1 measure pool (pre-relevance-cut deployment output)
c1 <- tar_read(country_c1_measures)[[1]]

pit_kw <- stringr::regex(
  paste0("individual income tax|personal income tax|cukai pendapatan individu|",
         "cukai pendapatan persendirian|personal tax|individual tax|top marginal|",
         "tax bracket|chargeable income|cukai pendapatan peribadi"),
  ignore_case = TRUE
)
n_pool <- nrow(c1)
n_pit  <- c1 |> dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(measure_name, pit_kw)) |> nrow()

# Frozen, human-stamped PIT shock dataset (produced by the /identify-pit skill).
# Loaded here so both the recall scorecard (@sec-recall) and the structured
# dataset (@sec-frozen) render from the single source of truth when it exists.
frozen_path <- here("data", "validated", "MY_PIT_shocks.qs")
have_frozen <- file.exists(frozen_path)
if (have_frozen) pit_frozen <- qs2::qs_read(frozen_path)
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
text:
1 Purpose
This note documents a focused, AI-assisted manual identification of Malaysia’s statutory personal/individual income-tax (PIT) rate changes, run directly over the evidence our deployment pipeline already surfaced. It is the PIT counterpart to notebooks/cit_identification.qmd, and it follows the same spirit of the narrative-identification literature (Romer and Romer 2010): close reading of source documents, with the LLM pipeline as a retrieval-and-extraction aide, not as the adjudicator.
The method mirrors our /manual-analysis protocol. The pipeline (C1 measure identification, C0 act aggregation) pre-screens candidate measures; a human reads the surfaced evidence and makes the final call on what counts as an event, how scattered surface forms consolidate, and whether each change is exogenous. Claude pattern-matches against the source text; the researcher confers meaning.
PIT differs from CIT in one structural respect: it is a progressive schedule, not a single rate. We track the top marginal resident rate as the headline series and record bracket/threshold changes as detail. Two of the nine identified acts move brackets without moving the top rate, and are flagged as such.






Important



The exogenous/endogenous column in Table 3 is a preliminary suggestion with its supporting quote, pending expert adjudication. It is not a research finding. The whole point of this pass is that a human adjudicates these calls; see the open questions in Section 6.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
section: 2 What C1 surfaced, and what it missed
text:
2 What C1 surfaced, and what it missed
A keyword scan of the full C1 pool returns 30 distinct PIT measures spanning 1999–2023, in both English and Malay. A broader semantic sweep over all 1036 measures (reading every income-tax surface form, not just keyword hits) confirms the top-marginal-rate path runs 30 → 29 → 28 → 27 → 26 → 25, then reverses upward to 28 (2016) and 30 (2020).
The sweep also found two genuine recall misses: headline events that sit in the corpus with explicit rates but that C1 did not surface as measures. Both were recovered by reading the source text directly.


Show code
recall <- if (have_frozen) {
  pit_frozen$recall_scorecard[[1]] |>
    dplyr::rename(Stage = stage, Outcome = outcome)
} else {
  tibble::tribble(
    ~Stage, ~Outcome,
    "C1 measure identification", "Surfaced 7 of 9 announced PIT acts; missed the 2009 (28->27) and 2016 (25->28) events",
    "Recall miss - Budget 2009", "28->27 (YA2009) recovered from Budget Speech 2009 (BM) and Budget Speech 2010",
    "Recall miss - Budget 2016", "25->28 high-earner hike recovered from Budget Speech 2016 Appendix 1",
    "C0 act aggregation", "Fragmented the PIT measures (near-all singletons; EN/BM never bridged); not used as the event layer"
  )
}

recall |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 1: Recall scorecard. How each headline event fared through C1 measure identification and C0 act aggregation. ‘Recovered manually’ marks events present in the corpus that C1 did not surface as a measure.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Stage
                Outcome
              
        
        
        
                
                  Keyword scan (C1 pool, n=1,036)
                  30 hits over the PIT regex spanning 1999-2023; ~2 false positives excluded (a 2004 corporate 20%-band item; a SOCSO taxi-driver relief item).
                
                
                  Semantic sweep (all income-tax surface forms)
                  Confirmed the top-marginal-rate path 30->29->28->27->26->25 then back up to 28 (YA2016) and 30 (YA2020); relief/rebate/threshold tracks recorded as magnitude detail, not headline rows.
                
                
                  C1 measure identification
                  Surfaced P1, P2, P4, P5, P7, P8, P9; MISSED P3 (28->27, Budget 2009) and P6 (25->28 high-earner hike, Budget 2016).
                
                
                  Recall miss - Budget 2009 (28->27, YA2009)
                  Recovered from Budget Speech 2009 ch2 (BM: 'kadar cukai individu maksimum diturunkan dari 28 peratus kepada 27 peratus, mulai tahun taksiran 2009'); retrospectively confirmed in Budget Speech 2010 ch6.
                
                
                  Recall miss - Budget 2016 high-earner hike (25->28, YA2016)
                  Recovered from Budget Speech 2016 ch3/9 + Appendix 1 ('chargeable income exceeding RM1,000,000 be increased by 3 percentage points ... 25% ... 28%').
                
                
                  C0 act aggregation
                  Not used as the event layer (same fragmentation finding as CIT: near-all singletons; EN/BM never bridged).
                
        
      
    






The retrieval lesson is the same one the CIT pass surfaced: a statutory rate change is maximally relevant because it is a rate change, however briefly the documents discuss it, so an instrument-targeted recall pass (plus human reading) is needed rather than trust in C0’s clustering. Both PIT recall misses are top-of-schedule moves (one cut, one hike) that the broad measure-identification pass passed over.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
section: 3 The headline statutory top PIT rate
text:
3 The headline statutory top PIT rate
Reading the consolidated evidence yields a clean headline path for the top marginal resident rate: 30 → 29 → 28 → 27 → 26 → 25 → 28 → 30. Table 2 gives the rate-moving events; Figure 1 traces the rate. Two further acts (a 2018 middle-band cut and the 2023 M40/T20 restructure) change the schedule without moving the top rate and are noted below the figure.


Show code
if (have_frozen) {
  pit_frozen |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(rate_from)) |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      Event = shock_id,
      `From->To` = sprintf("%g%% -> %g%%", rate_from, rate_to),
      `Dpp` = sprintf("%+d", as.integer(delta_pp)),
      Direction = direction,
      `Effective (YA)` = effective_year,
      Announced = announced_year
    ) |>
    tt() |>
    tt_theme_report()
}




Table 2: Headline top-PIT-rate events. Identified statutory changes in the top marginal resident income-tax rate, with the budget that announced each and the year of assessment it took effect. Acts that move only lower brackets (P7, P9) are excluded here and discussed in Section 5.

                From->To
                Dpp
                Direction
                Effective (YA)
                Announced

                  30% -> 29%
                  -1
                  Cut
                  2000
                  1999
                
                
                  MY-PIT-02
                  29% -> 28%
                  -1
                  Cut
                  2002
                  2001
                
                
                  MY-PIT-03
                  28% -> 27%
                  -1
                  Cut
                  2009
                  2008

                  27% -> 26%
                  -1
                  Cut
                  2010
                  2009

                  26% -> 25%

                  25% -> 28%
                  +3
                  Hike
                  2016

                  28% -> 30%
                  +2
                  Hike
                  2020
                  2019
                
                
                  MY-PIT-09
                  30% -> 30%
                  +0
                  Neutral

                  2023
                
        
      
    








Show code
rate_path <- tibble::tribble(
  ~year, ~rate,
  1999,  30,
  2000,  29,
  2002,  28,
  2009,  27,
  2010,  26,
  2015,  25,
  2016,  28,
  2020,  30,
  2023,  30
)

ggplot(rate_path, aes(year, rate)) +
  geom_step(direction = "hv", linewidth = 0.8, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_point(data = dplyr::filter(rate_path, year != 2023), size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1), limits = c(24, 31)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, 5)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Top marginal PIT rate")





Figure 1: Headline top marginal PIT rate, Malaysia 1999-2023. Step path of the top resident individual income-tax rate from the identified events. The 2016 and 2020 steps are high-earner band introductions (>RM1m, >RM2m) that raised the statutory top rate; the 2018 middle-band cut and the 2023 M40/T20 restructure (top rate unchanged) are not part of this path.








The path is not monotone: a long sequence of competitiveness-driven cuts (2000–2015) is followed by two high-earner hikes (2016, 2020) that reintroduced rates above the prior 25% floor for the very top of the distribution. This non-monotonicity, and the mix of cut and hike framings, is exactly what makes the exogeneity adjudication consequential rather than mechanical.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
text:
4 Motivation evidence and preliminary exogeneity
For each act, the corpus carries motivation language. Table 3 pairs each event with the most diagnostic quote and a preliminary exogenous/endogenous read. The cuts of 2009 and 2010 carry explicit competitiveness/brain-drain framing; the 2000 cut sits inside the post-crisis demand-recovery package; the high-earner hikes and the 2023 restructure carry progressivity framing amid fiscal pressure.


Show code
if (have_frozen) {
  pit_frozen |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      Event = shock_id,
      `Motivation quote (source text)` = stringr::str_trunc(exogeneity_quote, 180),
      `Preliminary read` = exogenous_preliminary
    ) |>
    tt() |>
    tt_theme_report()
}




Table 3: Motivation evidence, pending adjudication. The stated rationale for each act and a preliminary exogenous/endogenous read. These are suggestions anchored in the quoted text, not settled classifications; the exogeneity calls are the object of expert adjudication (see Section 6).

                  ambiguous

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
text:
5 Bracket-only acts and non-rate tracks
Per the PIT scope, the top marginal rate is the headline series; sub-top changes are recorded as detail. Table 4 lists the two acts that change the schedule without moving the top rate, plus the relief/rebate/threshold tracks surfaced by C1 that are not rate shocks.


Show code
tracks <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Track, ~`Identified events`, ~Note,
  "Middle-band cut (P7)", "Budget 2018: 2pp cut on RM20k-70k bands (5->3, 10->8, 16->14%)", "Top marginal rate unchanged at 28%; kept as a row, rate fields NA",
  "M40/T20 restructure (P9)", "Budget 2023: M40 -2pp (RM35k-100k); T20 +0.5-2pp (RM100k-1m)", "Top rate unchanged at 30%; direction Neutral, split in magnitude_note",
  "Reliefs / rebates", "2001/2002 rebates; personal relief raised RM8k->RM9k (2010); tuition/parental/SOCSO reliefs (2016)", "Base-narrowing, not statutory-rate shocks",
  "Threshold / structure", "Chargeable-income band regrading (2002, 2015)", "Recorded in magnitude_note for the parent rate event"
)

tracks |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 4: Bracket-only acts and non-rate tracks. The 2018 middle-band cut and the 2023 M40/T20 restructure leave the top rate unchanged and are flagged as bracket changes. Reliefs, rebates and threshold moves are tracked as magnitude detail, not headline rows.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Track
                Identified events
                Note
              
        
        
        
                
                  Middle-band cut (P7)
                  Budget 2018: 2pp cut on RM20k-70k bands (5->3, 10->8, 16->14%)
                  Top marginal rate unchanged at 28%; kept as a row, rate fields NA
                
                
                  M40/T20 restructure (P9)
                  Budget 2023: M40 -2pp (RM35k-100k); T20 +0.5-2pp (RM100k-1m)
                  Top rate unchanged at 30%; direction Neutral, split in magnitude_note
                
                
                  Reliefs / rebates
                  2001/2002 rebates; personal relief raised RM8k->RM9k (2010); tuition/parental/SOCSO reliefs (2016)
                  Base-narrowing, not statutory-rate shocks
                
                
                  Threshold / structure
                  Chargeable-income band regrading (2002, 2015)
                  Recorded in magnitude_note for the parent rate event

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
text:
6 Open adjudication questions
Four calls are genuinely the researcher’s to make; they were adjudicated at the /identify-pit stamp (the frozen dataset in Section 7 encodes the decisions).






TipResolved at freeze (2026-06-25)




Mid-band-only acts kept as rows. P7 (Budget 2018) and P9 (Budget 2023) are kept as one row per narratively-announced act, flagged in magnitude_note and id_reasoning as bracket changes that do not move the top marginal rate (P7 carries NA rate fields; P9 is Neutral with delta_pp = 0).
P5 (YA2015 GST-offset cut) frozen as ambiguous, its own PIT row (not merged with the GST act), mirroring the CIT GST-offset call (MY-CIT-08).
P9 direction Neutral. The mixed M40-cut / T20-hike restructure leaves the top rate unchanged; the split is preserved in magnitude_note as the object of adjudication.
P1 top from/to 30 -> 29. Inferred from the documented “across-the-board 1pp” cut plus the 29% pre-2002 top rate; flagged in id_reasoning.

The narrative above is retained as the reasoning record behind these calls.








Note1. Exogenous vs endogenous across the cut sequence



The 2009 and 2010 cuts carry explicit competitiveness/brain-drain framing (preliminary exogenous); the 2000 cut sits in the post-crisis recovery package (preliminary endogenous); the 2002 cut blends competitiveness with a recovery budget (ambiguous). The same identifying separation that drives the CIT pass applies here.








Note2. The high-earner hikes



The 2016 (>RM1m, 25->28) and 2020 (>RM2m, 28->30) hikes are framed as progressivity but landed amid fiscal pressure (low oil/commodity revenue). Progressivity (long-run, exogenous) or deficit-driven (still exogenous, different reason)? Both are frozen ambiguous.








Note3. The 2023 restructure



A simultaneous M40 cut and T20 hike with the top rate unchanged: a single redistributive act, or two conceptual legs? Frozen as one Neutral row, pending adjudication.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Statutory PIT-Rate Shocks
text:
7 Structured frozen dataset
The narrative identification above is the human-curated content; the machine-readable form is frozen by the /identify-pit skill to data/validated/MY_PIT_shocks.qs, following the shared contract in docs/phase_1/tax_shock_schema.md (one row per announced act × tax type, with a member_chunks manifest linking each shock to its corpus chunks and a mandatory recall scorecard). That frozen file — not this notebook’s tribbles — is the reference input the deployment pipeline binds and feeds to C2a/C2b for motivation and exogeneity (tar_read(tax_shocks)).
Section 2 above is the recall scorecard for this pass. The frozen dataset and its per-shock member-chunk manifest render below once the skill has produced it.

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Government Spending Shocks
text:
Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, qs2)

here::i_am("notebooks/spending_identification.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
set_theme(theme_minimal())

# Frozen, human-stamped spending shock dataset (produced by the
# /identify-spending skill). Loaded here so both the recall scorecard
# (@sec-recall) and the structured dataset (@sec-frozen) render from the single
# source of truth when it exists.
frozen_path <- here("data", "validated", "MY_SPENDING_shocks.qs")
have_frozen <- file.exists(frozen_path)
if (have_frozen) spend_frozen <- qs2::qs_read(frozen_path)
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Government Spending Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Government Spending Shocks
text:
1 Purpose
This note documents a focused, AI-assisted manual identification of Malaysia’s discretionary government spending changes — named stimulus/relief packages, subsidy-policy changes, large allocations, and Five-Year Plan launches — run directly over the deployment corpus. It is the spending-side analogue of notebooks/cit_identification.qmd, and it deliberately follows the spirit of the narrative-identification literature (Romer and Romer 2010; Das et al. 2026): close reading of source documents, with the LLM pipeline as a retrieval-and-extraction aide, not as the adjudicator.
The method mirrors our /manual-analysis protocol. Unlike the tax side, C1 does not pre-screen spending (C1 is scoped to tax liabilities only), so identification is driven by direct reading of the corpus (country_body / country_chunks), not the C1 measure pool. A human reads the surfaced evidence and makes the final call on what counts as a spending act, how scattered surface forms consolidate, and whether each change is exogenous. Claude pattern-matches against the source text; the researcher confers meaning.






Important



The exogenous/endogenous column in Table 3 is a preliminary suggestion with its supporting quote, pending expert adjudication, produced by the Das (2026) two-condition screen. It is not a research finding, and it is carried alongside — never replacing — C2b’s downstream label.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Government Spending Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Government Spending Shocks
section: 2 What the corpus surfaced, and what C1 missed
text:
2 What the corpus surfaced, and what C1 missed
Because C1 is tax-scoped, the keyword scan of the C1 pool is expected to surface almost no spending events — that is itself a finding. The events below are recovered by reading the anchor documents directly (Budget Speeches’ Development Expenditure sections, Five-Year Plans, Economic Reports, BNM Annual Reports). The mandatory recall scorecard records what was searched and missed.


Show code
recall <- if (have_frozen) {
  spend_frozen$recall_scorecard[[1]] |>
    dplyr::rename(Stage = stage, Outcome = outcome)
} else {
  tibble::tribble(
    ~Stage,                       ~Outcome,
    "C1 keyword scan",            "Expected near-empty: C1 is scoped to tax liabilities, surfaces ~0 spending measures",
    "Direct corpus sweep",        "Primary method — read Development Expenditure / Five-Year Plan / Economic Report sections directly",
    "Recall checkpoint — 1998",   "National Economic Recovery Plan (Asian-crisis fiscal response) — recovered from source",
    "Recall checkpoint — 2009",   "GFC stimulus packages — recovered from source",
    "Recall checkpoint — 2020",   "COVID-19 packages (PRIHATIN / PENJANA / PEMERKASA, RM250bn+) — recovered from source"
  )
}

recall |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 1: Recall scorecard. How each headline spending event fared through the identification pass. The near-empty C1 floor is logged as a finding; ‘Recovered manually’ marks events recovered by direct reading.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Stage
                Outcome
              
        
        
        
                
                  C1 keyword scan (C1 pool, n=1,036)
                  NOT near-empty: 189 spending-regex hits. BNM Annual Reports, Economic Reports and Budget Speeches discuss stimulus packages and subsidy policy alongside tax, so C1 surfaced most spending events. Deviates from the skill's expected near-empty C1 floor.
                
                
                  Direct corpus sweep (country_body / country_chunks)
                  Read Development-Expenditure / crisis-booklet / Economic-Report / BNM-AR sections directly. Confirmed C1-surfaced events; recovered_chunks ~empty for every act (events were already C1-visible, the opposite of the skill's 'expect most recovered').
                
                
                  Recall checkpoint - 1998 NERP
                  FOUND. National Economic Recovery Plan (Asian-crisis response): MY_NERP-1998, MY_BNM_AR-1998, MY_ECON_REPORT-1999, MY_BUDGET_SPEECH-1999. -> MY-SPEND-01.
                
                
                  Recall checkpoint - 2009 GFC stimulus
                  FOUND. First Economic Stimulus Package (RM7bn, Nov 2008) + Second Economic Stimulus Package (RM60bn, Mar 2009 mini-budget) = RM67bn: MY_BNM_AR-2008/2009, MY_MINI_BUDGET-2009, MY_ECON_REPORT-2010. -> MY-SPEND-05, MY-SPEND-06.
                
                
                  Recall checkpoint - 2020/21 COVID (PRIHATIN/PENJANA/PEMERKASA)
                  FOUND. 8 packages, RM530bn+ (NRP-2021). Per-package grain (reviewer decision): PRIHATIN, PENJANA, PERMAI, PEMERKASA, PEMERKASA+, PEMULIH each a row; KITA PRIHATIN / PRIHATIN SME+ folded into PRIHATIN. -> MY-SPEND-09..14.
                
                
                  Consolidation grain (reviewer adjudication)
                  RMK Five-Year Plans EXCLUDED (baseline development-expenditure framework, not discrete shocks). COVID split per named package. 2008 fuel-subsidy restructuring kept as a separate (endogenous) row distinct from the 2010 structural rationalisation.
                
                
                  C0 act aggregation
                  Not used as the event layer (C0 is tax-scoped; fragments tight spending tracks and does not bridge EN/BM). Consolidation done by direct reading.
crumbs: Manual Identification of Malaysia's Government Spending Shocks

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Government Spending Shocks
text:
4 Motivation evidence and preliminary exogeneity
For each spending act the corpus carries motivation language. Table 3 pairs each act with the most diagnostic quote and a preliminary exogenous/endogenous read from the Das et al. (2026) two-condition screen (exogenous iff the motive is non-cyclical and the narrative does not cite contemporaneous macro conditions as the rationale; “acknowledgment ≠ endogeneity”).


Show code
spend_frozen |>
  dplyr::select(shock_id,
                `Preliminary read` = exogenous_preliminary,
                `Motivation quote (source text)` = exogeneity_quote) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()




Table 3: Motivation evidence, pending adjudication. The stated rationale for each spending act and a preliminary exogenous/endogenous read. Suggestions anchored in quoted text, not settled classifications; the exogeneity calls are the object of expert adjudication, and C2b assigns the downstream label.

title: Manual Identification of Malaysia’s Government Spending Shocks
text:
5 Structured frozen dataset
The narrative identification above is the human-curated content; the machine-readable form is frozen by the /identify-spending skill to data/validated/MY_SPENDING_shocks.qs, following the contract in docs/phase_1/spending_shock_schema.md (one row per announced spending act, with a member_chunks manifest linking each shock to its corpus chunks and a mandatory recall scorecard). That frozen file — not this notebook’s tribbles — is the reference input the spending pipeline binds and feeds to the frozen C2a/C2b for motivation and exogeneity (tar_read(spending_shocks)).


Show code
spend_frozen |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    `Member chunks`    = purrr::map_int(member_chunks, nrow),
    `Recovered chunks` = purrr::map_int(recovered_chunks, nrow)
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(shock_id, act_label, `Member chunks`, `Recovered chunks`) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: PDF Extraction Quality Test
text:
This notebook tests whether the local PyMuPDF PDF extraction (with OCR for scanned documents) produces text that Claude 3.5 Sonnet can effectively use for:

Model A: Act detection (identify fiscal legislation passages)
Model B: Motivation classification (spending-driven, countercyclical, etc.)
Model C: Information extraction (timing, magnitudes from tables)

title: PDF Extraction Quality Test
text:
This notebook tests whether the local PyMuPDF PDF extraction (with OCR for scanned documents) produces text that Claude 3.5 Sonnet can effectively use for:

Model A: Act detection (identify fiscal legislation passages)
Model B: Motivation classification (spending-driven, countercyclical, etc.)
Model C: Information extraction (timing, magnitudes from tables)

title: PDF Extraction Quality Test
text:
Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(here)
here::i_am("notebooks/test_text_extraction.qmd")

# Source the local extraction function
source(here("R/pull_text_local.R"))

# Load ground truth data
us_shocks <- read_csv(here("data/raw/us_shocks.csv"))
us_labels <- read_csv(here("data/raw/us_labels.csv"))

title: PDF Extraction Quality Test
text:
Phase 1: Sample Selection
We select documents with well-known fiscal acts that have clear ground truth labels.

# Key test cases with known acts
test_cases <- tribble(
 ~act_name, ~year, ~motivation, ~expected_url_pattern,
 "Revenue Act of 1964", 1964, "Long-run", "ERP.*1965",
 "Tax Reform Act of 1986", 1986, "Long-run", "ERP.*1987",
 "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981", 1981, "Long-run", "ERP.*1982",
 "Tax Reduction Act of 1975", 1975, "Countercyclical", "ERP.*1976",
 "Revenue Act of 1950", 1950, "Spending-driven", "ERP.*1951"
)

test_cases

# A tibble: 5 × 4
  act_name                           year motivation      expected_url_pattern
  <chr>                             <dbl> <chr>           <chr>               
1 Revenue Act of 1964                1964 Long-run        ERP.*1965           
2 Tax Reform Act of 1986             1986 Long-run        ERP.*1987           
3 Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981  1981 Long-run        ERP.*1982           
4 Tax Reduction Act of 1975          1975 Countercyclical ERP.*1976           
5 Revenue Act of 1950                1950 Spending-driven ERP.*1951           



# Get actual URLs for test documents
# Using Fraser St. Louis Fed ERP archive
test_urls <- c(
 "https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/publications/ERP/1965/ERP_1965.pdf",
 "https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/publications/ERP/1982/ERP_1982.pdf"
)

cat("Test URLs:\n")

Test URLs:

cat(test_urls, sep = "\n")

https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/publications/ERP/1965/ERP_1965.pdf
https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/publications/ERP/1982/ERP_1982.pdf

title: PDF Extraction Quality Test
text:
Phase 2: Extract Sample Documents

# Extract text from sample PDFs using local PyMuPDF+OCR
# This may take 5-15 minutes for scanned documents (OCR needed)
message("Starting local extraction...")

sample_results <- pull_text_local(
 pdf_url = test_urls,
 output_dir = here("data/extracted"),
 workers = 4,
 ocr_dpi = 200
)

message("Extraction complete!")


Extraction Summary

sample_results |>
 mutate(
   doc_name = basename(test_urls)
 ) |>
 select(doc_name, n_pages, ocr_used, extraction_time, extracted_at)

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  doc_name     n_pages ocr_used extraction_time extracted_at       
  <chr>          <int> <lgl>              <dbl> <dttm>             
1 ERP_1965.pdf     300 TRUE                277. 2026-01-21 19:33:20
2 ERP_1982.pdf     368 TRUE                368. 2026-01-21 19:33:20

3.2 Check for Known Act Names

# Acts we expect to find in these documents
expected_acts <- c(
 "Revenue Act of 1964",
 "Economic Recovery Tax Act",
 "Tax Equity and Fiscal Responsibility Act"
)

# Search for act names in extracted text
all_text <- paste(unlist(sample_results$text), collapse = " ")

act_detection <- map_dfr(expected_acts, function(act) {
 found <- str_detect(all_text, fixed(act))
 tibble(
   act_name = act,
   found = found,
   mentions = sum(str_count(all_text, fixed(act)))
 )
})

act_detection

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  act_name                                 found mentions
  <chr>                                    <lgl>    <int>
1 Revenue Act of 1964                      TRUE         4
2 Economic Recovery Tax Act                TRUE        17
3 Tax Equity and Fiscal Responsibility Act FALSE        0



# Act detection rate
detection_rate <- mean(act_detection$found)
cat(sprintf("Act detection rate: %.0f%% (%d/%d)\n",
           detection_rate * 100,
           sum(act_detection$found),
           nrow(act_detection)))

Act detection rate: 67% (2/3)

if (detection_rate < 0.8) {
 warning("Act detection rate below 80% - may need to check extraction quality")
}



3.3 Numeric Value Preservation

# Check for dollar amounts (critical for Model C)
dollar_pattern <- "\\$\\s*\\d+\\.?\\d*\\s*(billion|million|B|M)"
year_pattern <- "\\b(19[4-9]\\d|20[0-2]\\d)\\b"

numeric_metrics <- tibble(
 metric = c("Dollar amounts", "Year mentions", "Percentage values"),
 pattern = c(dollar_pattern, year_pattern, "\\d+\\.?\\d*\\s*percent"),
 count = c(
   sum(str_count(all_text, regex(dollar_pattern, ignore_case = TRUE))),
   sum(str_count(all_text, year_pattern)),
   sum(str_count(all_text, regex("\\d+\\.?\\d*\\s*percent", ignore_case = TRUE)))
 )
)

title: PDF Extraction Quality Test
text:
Phase 5: Quality Metrics Summary

# Compile all quality metrics
total_pages <- sum(sample_results$n_pages)
total_tables <- 0  # PyMuPDF doesn't extract tables separately

quality_report <- tribble(
 ~metric, ~value, ~target, ~status,
 "Documents extracted", as.character(nrow(sample_results)), "2", "PASS",
 "Pages extracted", as.character(total_pages), ">0",
   ifelse(total_pages > 0, "PASS", "FAIL"),
 "OCR used", as.character(sum(sample_results$ocr_used)), "as needed", "INFO",
 "Act name recall", sprintf("%.0f%%", detection_rate * 100), ">80%",
   ifelse(detection_rate >= 0.8, "PASS", "FAIL"),
 "Dollar amounts found", as.character(numeric_metrics$count[1]), ">0",
   ifelse(numeric_metrics$count[1] > 0, "PASS", "WARN"),
 "Year mentions", as.character(numeric_metrics$count[2]), ">10",
   ifelse(numeric_metrics$count[2] > 10, "PASS", "WARN"),

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Before running expensive S2 LOOCV API calls, this notebook runs 12 systematic checks on the data pipeline outputs. Each test produces a PASS/WARN/FAIL result. All tests must pass (or have documented exceptions) before proceeding.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, gt, here, jsonlite)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_api_inputs.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/gt_theme.R"))
source(here("R/identify_chunk_tiers.R"))
source(here("R/codebook_stage_0.R"))
source(here("R/generate_c1_examples.R"))
set_theme(theme_minimal())




Show code
us_body      <- tar_read(us_body)
chunks       <- tar_read(chunks)
aligned_data <- tar_read(aligned_data)
c1_chunk_data <- tar_read(c1_chunk_data)
c1_codebook  <- tar_read(c1_codebook)




Show code
# Accumulator for test results
test_results <- tibble(
  test_id = integer(),
  test_name = character(),
  result = character(),
  status = character()
)

add_result <- function(id, name, result, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(
    test_results,
    tibble(test_id = id, test_name = name, result = result, status = status)
  )
}

text:
Test 4: Act Name Alignment
The act names in aligned_data should match exactly with the act names in the tier data (both directions).


Show code
aligned_acts <- sort(unique(aligned_data$act_name))
tier_acts <- sort(unique(c(
  c1_chunk_data$tier1$act_name,
  c1_chunk_data$tier2$act_name
)))

in_aligned_not_tiers <- setdiff(aligned_acts, tier_acts)
in_tiers_not_aligned <- setdiff(tier_acts, aligned_acts)

alignment_ok <- length(in_aligned_not_tiers) == 0 && length(in_tiers_not_aligned) == 0

add_result(
  4, "Act name alignment",
  sprintf("Aligned→Tiers: %d missing, Tiers→Aligned: %d extra",
          length(in_aligned_not_tiers), length(in_tiers_not_aligned)),
  if (alignment_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)



Show code
if (length(in_aligned_not_tiers) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in aligned_data but not in tiers:**\n\n")
  for (act in in_aligned_not_tiers) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}
if (length(in_tiers_not_aligned) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in tiers but not in aligned_data:**\n\n")
  for (act in in_tiers_not_aligned) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}

Test 4: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
Pre-Flight API Input Verification
Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 7: No Within-Tier Duplicates
Tier 1 should have no duplicate (doc_id, chunk_id, act_name, passage_idx) combinations. Tier 2 should have no duplicate (doc_id, chunk_id, act_name) combinations. Multiple passages matching the same chunk are expected for Tier 1 and flagged as WARN.


Show code
# Tier 1: true duplicates (same chunk, act, passage)
tier1_n_before <- nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier1)
tier1_n_distinct <- c1_chunk_data$tier1 |>
  distinct(doc_id, chunk_id, act_name, passage_idx) |>
  nrow()
tier1_dupes <- tier1_n_before - tier1_n_distinct

# Tier 1: multi-passage matches (same chunk, different passages)
tier1_multi <- c1_chunk_data$tier1 |>
  count(doc_id, chunk_id, act_name) |>
  filter(n > 1) |>
  nrow()

# Tier 2: true duplicates
tier2_n_before <- nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier2)
tier2_n_distinct <- c1_chunk_data$tier2 |>
  distinct(doc_id, chunk_id, act_name) |>
  nrow()
tier2_dupes <- tier2_n_before - tier2_n_distinct

total_dupes <- tier1_dupes + tier2_dupes

add_result(
  7, "No within-tier duplicates",
  sprintf("T1: %d true dupes, %d multi-passage chunks; T2: %d dupes",
          tier1_dupes, tier1_multi, tier2_dupes),
  if (total_dupes == 0 && tier1_multi == 0) "PASS"
  else if (total_dupes == 0 && tier1_multi > 0) "WARN"
  else "FAIL"
)


Test 7: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 8: Token Budget Fit
All chunks plus overhead (codebook prompt + few-shot examples + system prompt + output buffer) must fit within the 200K context window.


Show code
# Compute actual codebook prompt tokens
codebook_prompt <- construct_codebook_prompt(c1_codebook)
codebook_tokens <- nchar(codebook_prompt) / 4

# Overhead components
few_shot_tokens <- 3100   # estimated from generate_c1_loocv_fold_examples()
system_tokens <- 125      # system prompt
output_tokens <- 2000     # output buffer
overhead <- codebook_tokens + few_shot_tokens + system_tokens + output_tokens

context_limit <- 200000

# Get max tokens across all tiers
max_tier1_tokens <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier1) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier1$approx_tokens, na.rm = TRUE) else 0
max_tier2_tokens <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier2) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier2$approx_tokens, na.rm = TRUE) else 0
max_neg_tokens <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$negatives) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$negatives$approx_tokens, na.rm = TRUE) else 0
max_chunk_tokens <- max(max_tier1_tokens, max_tier2_tokens, max_neg_tokens)

total_max <- max_chunk_tokens + overhead
fits <- total_max < context_limit

budget_tbl <- tibble(
  component = c("Codebook prompt", "Few-shot examples", "System prompt",
                "Output buffer", "Max chunk", "Total"),
  tokens = c(round(codebook_tokens), few_shot_tokens, system_tokens,
             output_tokens, max_chunk_tokens, round(total_max))
)

budget_tbl |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(component = "Component", tokens = "Tokens (est.)") |>
  fmt_number(columns = tokens, decimals = 0) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = if (fits) "#ccffcc" else "#ffcccc"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = component == "Total")
  ) |>
  gt_theme_report()

  8, "Token budget fit",
  sprintf("Max total: %s / %s", scales::comma(round(total_max)), scales::comma(context_limit)),
  if (fits) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 8: PASS (max 67,905 tokens vs. 200,000 limit)

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 9: Text Encoding Safety
Sample chunks from each tier and check for null bytes and control characters that could corrupt API calls.


Show code
# Control character pattern (excludes \x00 which R can't store, and \t \n \r which are safe)
control_pattern <- "[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f]"

check_encoding <- function(texts, label) {
  n <- min(100, length(texts))
  if (n == 0) return(tibble(tier = label, n_checked = 0, n_unsafe = 0))
  sample_texts <- texts[sample(length(texts), n)]
  n_unsafe <- sum(str_detect(sample_texts, control_pattern))
  tibble(tier = label, n_checked = n, n_unsafe = n_unsafe)
}

set.seed(44)
encoding_results <- bind_rows(
  check_encoding(c1_chunk_data$tier1$text, "Tier 1"),
  check_encoding(c1_chunk_data$tier2$text, "Tier 2"),
  check_encoding(c1_chunk_data$negatives$text, "Negative")
)

total_unsafe <- sum(encoding_results$n_unsafe)

encoding_results |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(tier = "Tier", n_checked = "Sampled", n_unsafe = "Unsafe") |>
  gt_theme_report()








Tier
Sampled
Unsafe




Tier 1
70
0


Tier 2
100
0


Negative
100
0







Show code
add_result(
  9, "Text encoding safety",
  sprintf("%d unsafe chars in %d sampled chunks",
          total_unsafe, sum(encoding_results$n_checked)),
  if (total_unsafe == 0) "PASS" else "WARN"
)


Test 9: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 10: Year Filter Consistency
The tier data should respect the max_doc_year = 2007 parameter. No chunk in Tier 1 or Tier 2 should come from a document published after 2007.


Show code
max_doc_year <- 2007L

tier1_max_year <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier1) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier1$year, na.rm = TRUE) else NA
tier2_max_year <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier2) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier2$year, na.rm = TRUE) else NA

year_ok <- (is.na(tier1_max_year) || tier1_max_year <= max_doc_year) &&
           (is.na(tier2_max_year) || tier2_max_year <= max_doc_year)

add_result(
  10, "Year filter consistency",
  sprintf("Tier 1 max year: %s, Tier 2 max year: %s (limit: %d)",
          as.character(tier1_max_year), as.character(tier2_max_year), max_doc_year),
  if (year_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 10: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 11: Few-Shot Example Preview
Generate examples for one LOOCV fold (first act held out) and verify they render correctly. This confirms that generate_c1_loocv_fold_examples() produces usable training data.


Show code
# Hold out the first act
held_out_act <- aligned_data$act_name[1]
train_data <- aligned_data |> filter(act_name != held_out_act)

examples <- generate_c1_loocv_fold_examples(
  train_data,
  c1_chunk_data$negatives,
  n_per_class = 5,
  codebook = c1_codebook,
  seed = 20251206
)

n_examples <- length(examples)
n_positive <- sum(map_chr(examples, ~ .x$output$label) == "FISCAL_MEASURE")
n_negative <- n_examples - n_positive

add_result(
  11, "Few-shot example preview",
  sprintf("%d examples (%d pos, %d neg) for fold '%s'",
          n_examples, n_positive, n_negative,
          str_trunc(held_out_act, 30)),
  if (n_examples >= 2) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)



Show code
cat(sprintf("\n\n**Held-out act:** %s\n\n", held_out_act))

Held-out act: Changes in Depreciation Guidelines and Revenue Act of 1962

Show code
cat(sprintf("**Examples generated:** %d (%d positive, %d negative)\n\n",
            n_examples, n_positive, n_negative))

Examples generated: 10 (5 positive, 5 negative)

Show code
for (i in seq_along(examples)) {
  ex <- examples[[i]]
  label <- ex$output$label
  act_name <- ex$output$measure_name
  reasoning <- ex$output$reasoning
  input_trunc <- str_trunc(ex$input, 400)

  cat(sprintf("\n\n#### Example %d: %s\n\n", i, label))
  if (!is.null(act_name)) cat(sprintf("**Act:** %s\n\n", act_name))
  cat(sprintf("**Reasoning:** %s\n\n", reasoning))
  cat(sprintf("**Input (truncated):**\n\n> %s\n\n",
              str_replace_all(input_trunc, "\n", "\n> ")))
}

T2: No short chunks (min_chars filter)
0 chunks with <=100 chars (of 15061 total)
PASS


T3: No text-less tier rows
Tier 1: 0 missing, Tier 2: 0 missing
PASS


T4: Act name alignment
Aligned→Tiers: 0 missing, Tiers→Aligned: 0 extra
PASS


T5: Passage delimiter integrity
39/39 multi-passage acts split correctly
PASS


T6: Usable passages per act
39/39 acts have >=1 usable passage
PASS


T7: No within-tier duplicates
T1: 0 true dupes, 0 multi-passage chunks; T2: 0 dupes
PASS


T8: Token budget fit
Max total: 67,905 / 200,000
PASS


T9: Text encoding safety
0 unsafe chars in 270 sampled chunks
PASS


T10: Year filter consistency
Tier 1 max year: 2003, Tier 2 max year: 2007 (limit: 2007)
PASS


T11: Few-shot example preview
10 examples (5 pos, 5 neg) for fold 'Changes in Depreciation Gui...'
PASS







Overall: 11 PASS, 0 WARN, 0 FAIL out of 11 tests.
All clear: Data pipeline is validated for S2 LOOCV.

title: C2 Input Verification
text:
Before building the C2 codebook or running evaluation, this notebook verifies the C1→C2 data handoff. Two targets are checked:

c1_classified_chunks: C1 S2 classifications merged with chunk text and ground truth labels from aligned_data
c2_input_data: Filtered to pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE" and discusses_motivation == TRUE

Eight tests check coverage, label completeness, text joins, C1 output fields, filter correctness, post-filter act coverage, class balance, and token distribution.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, gt, here, scales)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_c2_inputs.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/gt_theme.R"))




Show code
c1_classified <- tar_read(c1_classified_chunks)
c2_input      <- tar_read(c2_input_data)
aligned_data  <- tar_read(aligned_data)




Show code
test_results <- tibble(
  test_id = integer(),
  test_name = character(),
  result = character(),
  status = character()
)

add_result <- function(id, name, result, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(
    test_results,
    tibble(test_id = id, test_name = name, result = result, status = status)
  )
}

Before building the C2 codebook or running evaluation, this notebook verifies the C1→C2 data handoff. Two targets are checked:

c1_classified_chunks: C1 S2 classifications merged with chunk text and ground truth labels from aligned_data
c2_input_data: Filtered to pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE" and discusses_motivation == TRUE

Eight tests check coverage, label completeness, text joins, C1 output fields, filter correctness, post-filter act coverage, class balance, and token distribution.

c1_classified <- tar_read(c1_classified_chunks)

test_results <- tibble(

title: C2 Input Verification
text:
Part A: c1_classified_chunks (Precursor)

Test 1: Act Coverage
Every act in aligned_data should appear in c1_classified_chunks.


Show code
aligned_acts <- sort(unique(aligned_data$act_name))
classified_acts <- sort(unique(c1_classified$act_name))

missing <- setdiff(aligned_acts, classified_acts)
extra <- setdiff(classified_acts, aligned_acts)
coverage_ok <- length(missing) == 0 && length(extra) == 0

add_result(
  1, "Act coverage",
  sprintf("%d/%d acts present", length(classified_acts), length(aligned_acts)),
  if (coverage_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)



Show code
if (length(missing) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in aligned_data but missing from c1_classified_chunks:**\n\n")
  for (act in missing) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}
if (length(extra) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in c1_classified_chunks but not in aligned_data:**\n\n")
  for (act in extra) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}

Test 1: PASS


Test 2: Label Completeness
Every row must have non-NA motivation_category and exogenous_flag.


Show code
n_missing_motivation <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$motivation_category))
n_missing_exogenous <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$exogenous_flag))
labels_ok <- n_missing_motivation == 0 && n_missing_exogenous == 0

add_result(
  2, "Label completeness",
  sprintf("motivation: %d NA, exogenous: %d NA",
          n_missing_motivation, n_missing_exogenous),
  if (labels_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 2: PASS


Test 3: Text Join Completeness
All rows should have non-NA, non-empty text after joining from chunks.


Show code
n_na_text <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$text))
n_empty_text <- sum(!is.na(c1_classified$text) & nchar(c1_classified$text) == 0)
text_ok <- n_na_text == 0 && n_empty_text == 0

add_result(
  3, "Text join completeness",
  sprintf("%d NA, %d empty (of %d rows)", n_na_text, n_empty_text,
          nrow(c1_classified)),
  if (text_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 3: PASS


Test 4: C1 Output Field Completeness
pred_label should be non-NA for all rows. Report the distribution of discusses_motivation, discusses_timing, and discusses_magnitude.


Show code
n_na_pred <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$pred_label))
pred_ok <- n_na_pred == 0

add_result(
  4, "C1 output fields",
  sprintf("pred_label: %d NA; discusses_motivation: %d TRUE / %d FALSE / %d NA",
          n_na_pred,
          sum(c1_classified$discusses_motivation == TRUE, na.rm = TRUE),
          sum(c1_classified$discusses_motivation == FALSE, na.rm = TRUE),
          sum(is.na(c1_classified$discusses_motivation))),
  if (pred_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)




Show code
c1_classified |>
  count(pred_label, name = "n") |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(pred_label = "Predicted Label", n = "Chunks", pct = "Share") |>
  fmt_percent(columns = pct, decimals = 1) |>
  gt_theme_report()

    motivation_TRUE = sum(discusses_motivation == TRUE, na.rm = TRUE),

title: C2 Input Verification
text:
Part B: c2_input_data (Filtered)

Test 5: Filter Correctness
All rows must have pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE" and discusses_motivation == TRUE.


Show code
n_wrong_label <- sum(c2_input$pred_label != "FISCAL_MEASURE", na.rm = TRUE)
n_wrong_motiv <- sum(c2_input$discusses_motivation != TRUE, na.rm = TRUE)
filter_ok <- n_wrong_label == 0 && n_wrong_motiv == 0

add_result(
  5, "Filter correctness",
  sprintf("Non-FISCAL_MEASURE: %d, discusses_motivation != TRUE: %d",
          n_wrong_label, n_wrong_motiv),
  if (filter_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 5: PASS


Test 6: Act Coverage Post-Filter
How many aligned acts survive the motivation filter? Acts that lose all chunks cannot be evaluated by C2.


Show code
c2_acts <- unique(c2_input$act_name)
lost_acts <- setdiff(aligned_acts, c2_acts)
n_lost <- length(lost_acts)
post_filter_ok <- n_lost == 0

add_result(
  6, "Act coverage post-filter",
  sprintf("%d/%d acts retained (%d lost)",
          length(c2_acts), length(aligned_acts), n_lost),
  if (post_filter_ok) "PASS" else "WARN"
)



Show code
if (n_lost > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts with no FISCAL_MEASURE + discusses_motivation chunks:**\n\n")
  lost_info <- aligned_data |>
    filter(act_name %in% lost_acts) |>
    select(act_name, year, motivation_category) |>
    distinct()
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(lost_info))) {
    cat(sprintf("- %s (%d) — %s\n",
                lost_info$act_name[i], lost_info$year[i],
                lost_info$motivation_category[i]))
  }
}

Test 6: PASS


Test 7: Motivation Class Balance
Distribution of motivation_category across C2 input chunks, compared to aligned_data.


Show code
c2_act_dist <- c2_input |>
  distinct(act_name, motivation_category) |>
  count(motivation_category, name = "n_c2") |>
  arrange(desc(n_c2))

aligned_dist <- aligned_data |>
  count(motivation_category, name = "n_aligned") |>
  arrange(desc(n_aligned))

balance_check <- c2_act_dist |>
  full_join(aligned_dist, by = "motivation_category") |>
  mutate(
    n_c2 = replace_na(n_c2, 0L),
    n_aligned = replace_na(n_aligned, 0L),
    match = n_c2 == n_aligned
  )

balance_ok <- all(balance_check$match)

add_result(
  7, "Motivation class balance",
  sprintf("%d/%d categories match aligned_data",
          sum(balance_check$match), nrow(balance_check)),
  if (balance_ok) "PASS" else "WARN"
)




Show code
exog_map <- aligned_data |>
  distinct(motivation_category, exogenous_flag)

balance_check |>
  left_join(exog_map, by = "motivation_category") |>
  select(motivation_category, exogenous_flag, n_c2, n_aligned) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    motivation_category = "Category",
    exogenous_flag = "Exogenous?",
    n_c2 = "c2_input",
    n_aligned = "aligned_data"
  ) |>
  gt_theme_report()

Test 8: Token Distribution
Per-chunk token distribution. Chunks were constructed to fit within the context window, so all should be well under 200K.

token_summary <- c2_input |>

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
This notebook verifies the text-extraction quality of every country in the deployment pipeline (currently Malaysia; future Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam). It runs on country_body and country_chunks after they are built, before C1 deployment.
The US precedent is verify_body.qmd. Six of its seven tests port directly: URL resolution, boundary documents, temporal coverage, text quality, anomaly detection, and a new chunk-integrity test that the US notebook does not run. The seventh US test, “Known Act Validation”, is omitted because deployment countries have no labeled passages. Anchor-event detection (1997 Asian Financial Crisis, 2015 GST, 2020 COVID packages) is reserved for the C1-output deployment evaluation notebook, not here.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, digest, stringi)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_country_body.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
theme_set(theme_minimal())

# Country-branched targets use iteration = "list"; bind each into one tibble.
country_urls    <- tar_read(country_urls)    |> bind_rows()
country_body    <- tar_read(country_body)    |> bind_rows()
country_chunks  <- tar_read(country_chunks)  |> bind_rows()
country_configs <- tar_read(country_configs)

# Flatten country_configs list for joins
configs_tbl <- tibble(
  country          = map_chr(country_configs, "country"),
  pilot_year_min   = map_int(country_configs, "pilot_year_min"),
  pilot_year_max   = map_int(country_configs, "pilot_year_max"),
  primary_language = map_chr(country_configs, "primary_language")
)

# Join chunks back to body metadata so country/source/body are available
chunks_meta <- country_chunks |>
  left_join(
    country_body |> select(package_id, country, source, body, doc_language),
    by = c("doc_id" = "package_id")
  )

# Helper: apply PASS/WARN/FAIL/INFO/SKIP colors to a tinytable status column.
# Takes a tt object, the raw status vector (used to find row indices), and the
# column position (integer) in the rendered table to color.
style_status <- function(tt_obj, status_vec, j) {
  colors <- c(
    PASS = "#2E7D32", WARN = "#F57C00",
    FAIL = "#C62828", INFO = "#1565C0",
    SKIP = "#757575"
  )
  for (s in names(colors)) {
    rows <- which(status_vec == s)
    if (length(rows) > 0) {
      tt_obj <- tinytable::style_tt(
        tt_obj, i = rows, j = j,
        color = colors[[s]], bold = TRUE
      )
    }
  }
  tt_obj
}

# Test-result accumulator: one row per (country, test)
test_results <- tibble(
  country = character(),
  test    = character(),
  metric  = character(),
  value   = character(),
  target  = character(),
  status  = character()
)
record_result <- function(country, test, metric, value, target, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(test_results, tibble(
    country = country, test = test, metric = metric,
    value = as.character(value), target = target, status = status
  ))
}






Show code
chunks_per_country <- chunks_meta |>
  count(country, name = "Chunks")

corpus_snapshot <- country_body |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    Documents          = n(),
    Extracted          = sum(n_pages > 0),
    Pages              = sum(n_pages),
    `Year range`       = sprintf("%d-%d", min(year), max(year)),
    `Document bodies`  = n_distinct(body),
    Sources            = n_distinct(source),
    `OCR docs`         = sum(ocr_used, na.rm = TRUE),
    `OCR pages`        = sum(n_pages_ocr, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups            = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(chunks_per_country, by = "country")

corpus_snapshot |>
  mutate(across(c(Documents, Extracted, Pages, `OCR docs`, `OCR pages`, Chunks),
                ~ scales::comma(.x))) |>
  rename(Country = country) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
This notebook verifies the text-extraction quality of every country in the deployment pipeline (currently Malaysia; future Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam). It runs on country_body and country_chunks after they are built, before C1 deployment.
The US precedent is verify_body.qmd. Six of its seven tests port directly: URL resolution, boundary documents, temporal coverage, text quality, anomaly detection, and a new chunk-integrity test that the US notebook does not run. The seventh US test, “Known Act Validation”, is omitted because deployment countries have no labeled passages. Anchor-event detection (1997 Asian Financial Crisis, 2015 GST, 2020 COVID packages) is reserved for the C1-output deployment evaluation notebook, not here.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, digest, stringi)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_country_body.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
theme_set(theme_minimal())

# Country-branched targets use iteration = "list"; bind each into one tibble.
country_urls    <- tar_read(country_urls)    |> bind_rows()
country_body    <- tar_read(country_body)    |> bind_rows()
country_chunks  <- tar_read(country_chunks)  |> bind_rows()
country_configs <- tar_read(country_configs)

# Flatten country_configs list for joins
configs_tbl <- tibble(
  country          = map_chr(country_configs, "country"),
  pilot_year_min   = map_int(country_configs, "pilot_year_min"),
  pilot_year_max   = map_int(country_configs, "pilot_year_max"),
  primary_language = map_chr(country_configs, "primary_language")
)

# Join chunks back to body metadata so country/source/body are available
chunks_meta <- country_chunks |>
  left_join(
    country_body |> select(package_id, country, source, body, doc_language),
    by = c("doc_id" = "package_id")
  )

# Helper: apply PASS/WARN/FAIL/INFO/SKIP colors to a tinytable status column.
# Takes a tt object, the raw status vector (used to find row indices), and the
# column position (integer) in the rendered table to color.
style_status <- function(tt_obj, status_vec, j) {
  colors <- c(
    PASS = "#2E7D32", WARN = "#F57C00",
    FAIL = "#C62828", INFO = "#1565C0",
    SKIP = "#757575"
  )
  for (s in names(colors)) {
    rows <- which(status_vec == s)
    if (length(rows) > 0) {
      tt_obj <- tinytable::style_tt(
        tt_obj, i = rows, j = j,
        color = colors[[s]], bold = TRUE
      )
    }
  }
  tt_obj
}

# Test-result accumulator: one row per (country, test)
test_results <- tibble(
  country = character(),
  test    = character(),
  metric  = character(),
  value   = character(),
  target  = character(),
  status  = character()
)
record_result <- function(country, test, metric, value, target, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(test_results, tibble(
    country = country, test = test, metric = metric,
    value = as.character(value), target = target, status = status
  ))
}






Show code
chunks_per_country <- chunks_meta |>
  count(country, name = "Chunks")

corpus_snapshot <- country_body |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    Documents          = n(),
    Extracted          = sum(n_pages > 0),
    Pages              = sum(n_pages),
    `Year range`       = sprintf("%d-%d", min(year), max(year)),
    `Document bodies`  = n_distinct(body),
    Sources            = n_distinct(source),
    `OCR docs`         = sum(ocr_used, na.rm = TRUE),
    `OCR pages`        = sum(n_pages_ocr, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups            = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(chunks_per_country, by = "country")

corpus_snapshot |>
  mutate(across(c(Documents, Extracted, Pages, `OCR docs`, `OCR pages`, Chunks),
                ~ scales::comma(.x))) |>
  rename(Country = country) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
section: Test (i): URL resolution and extraction success
text:
Test (i): URL resolution and extraction success
For each country, the share of documents in country_urls that produced extracted text in country_body. Documents with access_status == "manual_pending" are reported separately as known acquisition gaps, not extraction failures.


Show code
# Join URL manifest to body to classify each expected document
url_status <- country_urls |>
  left_join(
    country_body |> select(country, package_id, n_pages),
    by = c("country", "package_id")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_pages = replace_na(n_pages, 0L),
    outcome = case_when(
      access_status == "manual_pending"               ~ "Pending",
      access_status %in% c("auto", "manual_ready") &
        n_pages > 0                                   ~ "Extracted",
      access_status %in% c("auto", "manual_ready") &
        n_pages == 0                                  ~ "Failed",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Failed"
    )
  )

test_i_summary <- url_status |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_expected      = n(),
    n_pending       = sum(outcome == "Pending"),
    n_resolvable    = sum(outcome != "Pending"),
    n_extracted     = sum(outcome == "Extracted"),
    n_failed        = sum(outcome == "Failed"),
    success_rate    = if (n_resolvable > 0) n_extracted / n_resolvable else NA_real_,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    status = case_when(
      is.na(success_rate)   ~ "INFO",
      success_rate >= 0.95  ~ "PASS",
      success_rate >= 0.85  ~ "WARN",
      TRUE                  ~ "FAIL"
    )
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_i_summary))) {
  r <- test_i_summary[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "i", "URL resolution rate (excl. pending)",
    sprintf("%.1f%% (%d/%d)", 100 * r$success_rate, r$n_extracted, r$n_resolvable),
    "≥95%", r$status
  )
}




Show code
test_i_summary |>
  mutate(
    `Success rate` = sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * success_rate)
  ) |>
  select(
    Country     = country,
    Expected    = n_expected,
    Pending     = n_pending,
    Resolvable  = n_resolvable,
    Extracted   = n_extracted,
    Failed      = n_failed,
    `Success rate`,
    Status      = status
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  style_status(test_i_summary$status, j = 8) |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
Test (ii): Boundary documents
For every country × source × body combination, the earliest and latest documents with extracted text must be at least 10 pages long. This catches systematic extraction failure at the edges of the corpus where document formats are most likely to drift.


Show code
boundary_docs <- bind_rows(
  country_body |>
    filter(n_pages > 0) |>
    group_by(country, source, body) |>
    slice_min(year, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
    mutate(boundary_type = "Earliest") |>
    ungroup(),
  country_body |>
    filter(n_pages > 0) |>
    group_by(country, source, body) |>
    slice_max(year, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
    mutate(boundary_type = "Latest") |>
    ungroup()
)

test_ii_per_country <- boundary_docs |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_boundary = n(),
    n_short    = sum(n_pages < 10),
    status     = if_else(n_short == 0, "PASS", "WARN"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_ii_per_country))) {
  r <- test_ii_per_country[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "ii", "Boundary docs ≥10 pages",
    sprintf("%d/%d", r$n_boundary - r$n_short, r$n_boundary),
    "All ≥10 pages", r$status
  )
}




Show code
boundary_docs |>
  select(country, source, body, year, boundary_type, n_pages, ocr_used) |>
  arrange(country, source, body, boundary_type) |>
  rename(
    Country   = country, Source = source, Body = body, Year = year,
    Boundary  = boundary_type, Pages = n_pages, OCR = ocr_used
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
Deployment Corpus Verification
Deployment Corpus Verification
Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
Test (vi): Chunk integrity
The US verify_body.qmd stops at body-level checks, but the deployment pipeline feeds C1 from country_chunks. This test verifies the chunk layer.


Show code
CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS <- 190700L     # 200K minus ~9.3K codebook+fewshot+output overhead
MIN_CHARS              <- 100L        # make_chunks() default
PAGE_BREAK_MARKER      <- "--- PAGE BREAK ---"

# Per-document expected end_page comes from country_body
doc_totals <- country_body |>
  filter(n_pages > 0) |>
  select(package_id, country, total_pages = n_pages)

chunk_checks <- chunks_meta |>
  left_join(doc_totals |> select(-country), by = c("doc_id" = "package_id")) |>
  group_by(country, doc_id) |>
  arrange(chunk_id, .by_group = TRUE) |>
  summarize(
    first_chunk_starts_at_1 = first(start_page) == 1,
    last_chunk_ends_at_total = last(end_page) == first(total_pages),
    chunk_ids_contiguous = identical(chunk_id, seq_len(n())),
    any_short_chunk = any(nchar(text) <= MIN_CHARS),
    any_oversized_chunk = any(approx_tokens > CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS),
    marker_count_ok = all(
      str_count(text, fixed(PAGE_BREAK_MARKER)) == pmax(n_pages - 1, 0)
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    doc_passes = first_chunk_starts_at_1 & last_chunk_ends_at_total &
      chunk_ids_contiguous & !any_short_chunk & !any_oversized_chunk &
      marker_count_ok
  )

test_vi_per_country <- chunk_checks |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_docs           = n(),
    n_passing        = sum(doc_passes),
    n_starts_bad     = sum(!first_chunk_starts_at_1),
    n_ends_bad       = sum(!last_chunk_ends_at_total),
    n_gaps           = sum(!chunk_ids_contiguous),
    n_short          = sum(any_short_chunk),
    n_oversized      = sum(any_oversized_chunk),
    n_markers_bad    = sum(!marker_count_ok),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    status = if_else(n_passing == n_docs, "PASS", "FAIL")
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_vi_per_country))) {
  r <- test_vi_per_country[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "vi", "Chunk-integrity-pass docs",
    sprintf("%d/%d", r$n_passing, r$n_docs),
    "All pass", r$status
  )
}




Show code
test_vi_per_country |>
  select(
    Country         = country,
    Documents       = n_docs,
    Passing         = n_passing,
    `Bad start`     = n_starts_bad,
    `Bad end`       = n_ends_bad,
    `Gap in IDs`    = n_gaps,
    `Short chunks`  = n_short,
    Oversized       = n_oversized,
    `Bad markers`   = n_markers_bad,
    Status          = status
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  style_status(test_vi_per_country$status, j = 10) |>
  tt_theme_report()

  ggplot(aes(x = approx_tokens)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS,
             linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Approximate tokens", y = "Chunks") +
  annotate("text", x = CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS,
           y = Inf, label = "Available context",
           hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.5, color = "red", size = 3)





Figure 7: Chunk token distribution with available-context reference line








Any FAIL here is a pipeline bug, not a corpus problem. The most likely failure modes are: a document whose extraction returned zero pages but still produced a chunk row, a marker-count mismatch from a stray --- PAGE BREAK --- inside the source text, or an oversized chunk produced by a very long page that the sliding-window logic could not split.

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
Test (vii): Language audit
doc_language claims English for all current Malaysia rows. Bahasa Malaysia contamination is plausible for Hansard fallbacks and bilingual editions. Per chunk we compare a small Bahasa stopword list against an English stopword list and flag chunks where the Bahasa share exceeds the English share.


Show code
LANGUAGE_SAMPLE_PER_COUNTRY <- 500L

en_stopwords <- c("\\bthe\\b", "\\band\\b", "\\bof\\b", "\\bto\\b",
                  "\\bin\\b", "\\bfor\\b", "\\bis\\b", "\\bwith\\b")
bm_stopwords <- c("\\byang\\b", "\\bdan\\b", "\\buntuk\\b", "\\bialah\\b",
                  "\\bdengan\\b", "\\badalah\\b", "\\bkepada\\b", "\\bpada\\b")

count_matches <- function(text, patterns) {
  rgx <- regex(paste(patterns, collapse = "|"), ignore_case = TRUE)
  str_count(text, rgx)
}

set.seed(43)
lang_sample <- chunks_meta |>
  group_by(country) |>
  group_modify(~ slice_sample(
    .x, n = min(LANGUAGE_SAMPLE_PER_COUNTRY, nrow(.x))
  )) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    en_count = count_matches(text, en_stopwords),
    bm_count = count_matches(text, bm_stopwords),
    bm_dominant = bm_count > en_count & bm_count >= 5
  )

test_vii_per_country <- lang_sample |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_sampled       = n(),
    n_bm_dominant   = sum(bm_dominant),
    pct_bm_dominant = mean(bm_dominant),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Language audit is diagnostic, never a deployment blocker.
    # Any Bahasa-dominant chunks in claimed-English docs => WARN.
    status = if_else(n_bm_dominant == 0, "PASS", "WARN")
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_vii_per_country))) {
  r <- test_vii_per_country[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "vii", "Bahasa-dominant chunks (claimed English)",
    sprintf("%d/%d (%.1f%%)", r$n_bm_dominant, r$n_sampled,
            100 * r$pct_bm_dominant),
    "0", r$status
  )
}




Show code
test_vii_per_country |>
  mutate(`Bahasa-dominant rate` = sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * pct_bm_dominant)) |>
  select(
    Country                   = country,
    `Chunks sampled`          = n_sampled,
    `Bahasa-dominant chunks`  = n_bm_dominant,
    `Bahasa-dominant rate`,
    Status                    = status
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  style_status(test_vii_per_country$status, j = 5) |>
  tt_theme_report()

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
The problem. For most emerging markets, we lack consistent and comparable measures of exogenous fiscal shocks: the cornerstone input for credible fiscal multiplier estimation and fiscal policy analysis. The gold-standard “narrative approach” (Romer and Romer 2010) has been applied to the United States, where it required years of manual work. For low- and middle-income countries, replication costs have been prohibitive.
Our contribution. This project builds a validated, LLM-assisted methodology for fiscal shock identification that can transfer across countries without retraining. We integrate the Romer & Romer methodology with a rigorous LLM evaluation framework (Halterman and Keith 2025) to produce the first systematic application of modern AI evaluation standards to economic history research.
Current status. We have built the document infrastructure (approximately 97,000 pages of US fiscal documents extracted and structured) and demonstrated through a proof-of-concept that LLMs can identify fiscal policy language with high accuracy. We are now implementing a four-codebook architecture with systematic validation before deployment.
What we’re asking

API budget for development phase: ~$200–250 (codebook validation through early March)
API budget for production phase: ~$250–400 (US and Malaysia deployment, contingent on development results)
Identification of a Malaysia fiscal policy expert for validation (~25–40 hours)

The development phase produces a clear feasibility signal: if codebooks do not meet performance targets on US ground-truth data by early March, we will know the approach is not viable before committing to production costs.
Timeline

Full codebook evaluation suite validated: Early March 2026 (go/no-go decision point)
US benchmark deployment with ground-truth comparison: Late March 2026
Malaysia pilot deployment: April 2026
Full Southeast Asia dataset (Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam): June 2026
