title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Why This Matters
Governments change taxes and spending constantly, but most of these changes respond to current economic conditions. Stimulus during recessions and tax hikes during booms are endogenous to the business cycle. Using them to estimate fiscal multipliers produces biased results, because the policy change and the economic outcome are driven by the same underlying conditions.
Credible fiscal policy analysis requires isolating exogenous shocks: tax or spending changes that happened for reasons unrelated to current economic conditions (deficit reduction motivated by past decisions, or long-run structural reforms). Romer and Romer (2010) pioneered the “narrative approach” to identify these shocks by reading historical government documents and classifying the stated motivation for each fiscal action.
This has been done for the United States. For emerging markets, where fiscal policy decisions have enormous consequences for development outcomes, we have no comparable data.
Recent advances in Large Language Models may make it possible to scale this methodology for the first time. This project investigates whether LLMs, guided by carefully validated codebooks, can assist experts in identifying fiscal shocks across countries at a fraction of the traditional cost and time.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Our Approach

Integrating Two Rigorous Frameworks
Our methodology synthesizes two established frameworks:

Romer and Romer (2010) (Fiscal Shock Identification): The gold-standard 6-phase methodology that defines what we measure: how to identify fiscal acts, classify their motivations, extract timing, and quantify magnitudes, taking original government documents into a fully identified dataset of fiscal shocks
Halterman and Keith (2025) (LLM Content Analysis): A 5-stage validation framework that defines how we ensure quality through systematic testing of LLM outputs before trusting them for research

This combination is the core methodological contribution: we apply modern AI evaluation standards to an established economic methodology, producing results that are both economically grounded and statistically validated.


Four Domain-Specific Codebooks
Each stage of the Romer & Romer methodology maps to a dedicated codebook with its own evaluation criteria:








Codebook Architecture


Each codebook handles one stage of the R&R methodology


Codebook
Task
Key Question
Replaces




C1: Measure Identification
Find fiscal measures in raw documents using the 'significant mention' rule
Is there a fiscal act here?
Model A


C2: Motivation Classification
Classify why each measure was enacted: deficit reduction, countercyclical, long-run growth, or spending
Is this shock exogenous to the business cycle?
Model B


C3: Timing Extraction1
Extract the quarter(s) when each measure takes effect
When does it take effect?
Model C (timing)


C4: Magnitude Estimation
Extract the fiscal impact (revenue or spending change) in billions of dollars
How large is it?
Model C (magnitude)



1 C3 and C4 were previously combined; separating them allows independent evaluation of each task.










Systematic Validation Before Deployment
Each codebook passes through five stages of testing before we use it on new countries. The key insight from Halterman and Keith (2025) is that LLM outputs must be validated before deployment, not after:








Five-Stage Validation Pipeline


Applied to each codebook before production deployment


Stage
What We Do
Why It Matters
Pass Criteria




S0: Codebook Design
Write precise definitions with examples; domain expert reviews
Garbage in, garbage out; clear instructions are the foundation
Expert approval of definitions and examples


S1: Competency Tests
Test whether the model follows instructions correctly and produces valid outputs
Catches fundamental failures before we invest in full evaluation
100% valid outputs; correctly handles all training examples; stable under reordering


S2: Performance Evaluation
Evaluate on all 44 labeled US fiscal acts, holding each out in turn
Measures real accuracy against ground truth with no data leakage
Meet target metrics per codebook (see below)


S3: Error Analysis
When the model fails, diagnose whether errors are random or systematic
Guides targeted improvements to codebook design
Documented failure patterns with improvement plan


S4: Model Retraining1
Retrain the model on our specific task (last resort)
Risks overfitting to US data, reducing ability to transfer to Malaysia
Only attempted if codebook improvements are exhausted



1 We actively avoid this stage. Retraining may cause the model to memorize US-specific patterns and fail on Malaysia.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
What Model A Taught Us
Our January proof-of-concept (Model A) demonstrated that LLMs understand fiscal policy language well enough to identify fiscal acts with 92% accuracy, 100% recall, and 86% precision. This validated the core premise of the project.
However, Model A classified pre-selected passages rather than extracting information from raw documents. This is a critical distinction:








Gap Between Proof-of-Concept and Production


What Model A Did
What Production Requires




Classify pre-selected passages
Extract relevant passages from raw documents


Binary yes/no on curated examples
Find fiscal acts across ~97,000 pages


US-specific prompt engineering
Cross-country transfer to Malaysia and Southeast Asia







The revised codebook architecture addresses each of these gaps directly. Model A confirmed that the LLM capability is there; the codebook framework ensures we can deploy it rigorously at scale.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Success Criteria

What “Good Enough” Looks Like
Each codebook is evaluated against concrete targets:








Performance Targets by Codebook


Codebook
Requirement
Why This Threshold




C1: Measure ID
Find ≥90% of real fiscal acts
Missing real acts means incomplete dataset


C1: Measure ID
At least 80% of flagged items are real acts
Some false positives are acceptable; experts review candidates


C2: Motivation
Classify motivation correctly ≥70% of the time
Baseline for 4-category classification


C2: Motivation
When labeled exogenous, be right ≥85% of the time
Critical: only exogenous shocks are used for fiscal multiplier estimation


C3: Timing
Extract exact implementation quarter ≥85% of the time
Must match R&R implementation timing


C3: Timing
Within one quarter (3 months) ≥95% of the time
Acceptable tolerance for phased implementations


C4: Magnitude
Average magnitude error <30%
Must approximate R&R magnitude estimates


C4: Magnitude
Get direction right (increase vs. cut) ≥95% of the time
A tax increase coded as a cut would reverse the estimated effect









Cross-Country Transfer (The Key Test)
The central hypothesis is that codebooks validated on US data can transfer to Malaysia without retraining:

C1 (Measure Identification): Expert agreement ≥80%
C2 (Motivation Classification): Expert agreement ≥70%

Because Malaysia has no ground-truth labels, we rely on expert validation. If these thresholds are not met, we add Malaysia-specific examples to codebooks and re-evaluate, without retraining the base model.

Budget
API and expert costs are modest relative to the traditional alternative (6–12 months of full-time research assistant work per country). Once codebooks are validated, each additional country costs only ~$100–150 in API fees plus expert review hours, making regional scaling economically viable. Critically, the budget is structured in two phases: the development phase answers the feasibility question before production costs are incurred.

Using Claude Sonnet (~$3/M input, ~$15/M output tokens)

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
What We’ve Built So Far
The project has already delivered reusable infrastructure and validated the core approach:

Document corpus: Approximately 97,000 pages of US fiscal documents (Economic Reports of the President, Budget documents, Treasury Annual Reports) spanning 1946 to the present, extracted, structured, and ready for processing
Proof of concept: Model A demonstrated that LLMs identify fiscal policy language with 92% accuracy, confirming the feasibility of the approach
Methodological framework: A rigorous integration of R&R fiscal shock methodology with H&K LLM evaluation standards. This is the first application of this kind to economic history research
Validated codebook architecture: Four domain-specific codebooks (C1–C4) designed for systematic evaluation and cross-country transfer

The revised approach builds on this foundation. The document infrastructure and methodological framework are in place; what remains is codebook validation and deployment.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
References


Halterman, Andrew, and Katherine A. Keith. 2025. “Codebook LLMs: Evaluating LLMs as Measurement Tools for Political Science Concepts.” Political Analysis, September, 1–17.


Romer, Christina D., and David H. Romer. 2010. “The Macroeconomic Effects of Tax Changes: Estimates Based on a New Measure of Fiscal Shocks.” American Economic Review 100 (3): 763–801.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Our March 4 report concluded with the first of two classification steps approaching its performance targets. This report covers six weeks of development that produced two milestones.
The pipeline classifies fiscal policy changes in two steps. First, Measure Identification (C1) scans government documents to find passages that describe a fiscal policy change. Second, Motivation Classification (C2) determines why each measure was enacted—and, critically, whether it qualifies as an exogenous shock (a policy change not driven by current economic conditions). C2 itself has two sub-steps: evidence extraction (C2a) pulls motivation-relevant quotes from each passage, then act-level classification (C2b) synthesizes that evidence into a final judgment. Together, C1 and C2 produce the core deliverable: a set of fiscal policy changes identified as exogenous.
Milestone 1: C1 is validated and ready for production. C1 passed its error analysis gate (v0.6.0, iteration 28). Manual inspection of all predictions found zero semantic errors—every misclassification traced to ambiguity in how we defined the scope of “fiscal measure,” not to the model misunderstanding its instructions.
Milestone 2: C2 completed its first full evaluation cycle. The motivation classifier reached weighted F1 of 0.611 (target: 0.70) and exogenous precision of 0.789 (target: 0.85) on all 39 labeled acts. Manual error analysis revealed that the codebook, not the model, is the primary bottleneck: 10 of 15 errors trace to gaps in the classification instructions (category F), while only 3 are genuine model reasoning failures (category E). This is a positive finding—instruction gaps are addressable through revision.
The Halterman and Keith (2025) validation framework is working as designed. Each stage produced actionable diagnostics: behavioral tests (S1) confirmed instruction compliance, formal evaluation (S2) quantified performance, and error analysis (S3) identified specific revisions to close the gap. The same iterative process validated C1 in 28 iterations and is now driving C2 toward its targets.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Classification Pipeline
Figure 1 shows the two-step classification architecture. C1 filters ~15,000 document passages down to those describing fiscal policy changes. C2 then determines why each measure was enacted: the evidence extraction step (C2a) pulls motivation-relevant quotes from each passage, and the classification step (C2b) synthesizes evidence across all passages for a given act into a single motivation judgment. The motivation category determines whether the act is exogenous (a shock suitable for causal analysis) or endogenous (a policy response to current conditions).

1 Deficit-driven and spending-driven are endogenous categories (not fiscal shocks). Long-run and countercyclical determine exogeneity.











Deficit-driven acts are classified near-perfectly (F1 = 0.842, 100% recall). The weakness is the boundary between long-run structural reforms and countercyclical stabilization: 7 of 15 long-run acts were misclassified as countercyclical. This is the dominant error pattern, and it directly affects exogeneity determination—long-run acts misclassified as countercyclical are incorrectly labeled endogenous.


Error Analysis: Classification Instructions are the Bottleneck
Manual analysis of all 39 acts using the Halterman and Keith (2025) six-category error framework revealed a favorable error distribution (Table 4).

1 Category F errors are addressable through codebook revision. Category E errors may require model upgrade.











The key finding: 66.7% of errors (10/15) are category F — the codebook lacks guidance for specific boundary cases, particularly distinguishing structural reform from cyclical stabilization when both use similar language. Only 3 errors (20%) are genuine model reasoning failures. Two specific codebook gaps account for 7 of the 10 category F errors:

Return-to-normal test too literal. The classification instructions’ rule for distinguishing long-run from countercyclical takes policymaker rhetoric at face value. Romer and Romer (2010) read through rhetorical framing to underlying policy intent—a distinction the current instructions do not encode.
Frequency rule disadvantages long-run. The “most frequently cited motivation” decision rule systematically favors countercyclical because cyclical language fills more space in budget documents than structural intent statements.

Both gaps have clear revision paths. After revision, the realistic ceiling is approximately 95% accuracy (36–37 of 39 acts), with the remaining errors requiring additional congressional documents in the corpus.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Next Steps

C2 codebook revision (immediate): Sharpen the return-to-normal test and modify the frequency-based decision rule to weigh provision structure alongside stated framing. Rerun behavioral tests (S1) to confirm compliance, then formal evaluation (S2) and error analysis (S3) for updated metrics.
Model exploration (if codebook revision plateaus): The error analysis found that the model relies on label name semantics over definitions for boundary cases, suggesting Haiku may be too small for the multi-step reasoning these cases require. A larger model (Sonnet) is the fallback.
US production pilot (late April target): Once C2 passes its evaluation gate, run the validated C1+C2 pipeline on the full US corpus (~15,000 passages) to produce the first complete set of exogenous fiscal shock identifications for 1945–2022, and compare to Romer and Romer (2010) dataset.
South East Asia deployment (mid May): After validating the production machinery on the US, we can start analysing government documents from SEA to produce exogenous only fiscal shock datasets.
Timing and magnitude (future extension): Extracting when each measure takes effect and how large the fiscal impact is are natural extensions of this pipeline. These enrichments can be pursued if the World Bank office determines they are valuable, but the core deliverable—identifying which fiscal policy changes are exogenous—does not depend on them.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
References


Halterman, Andrew, and Katherine A. Keith. 2025. “Codebook LLMs: Evaluating LLMs as Measurement Tools for Political Science Concepts.” Political Analysis, September, 1–17.


Romer, Christina D., and David H. Romer. 2010. “The Macroeconomic Effects of Tax Changes: Estimates Based on a New Measure of Fiscal Shocks.” American Economic Review 100 (3): 763–801.
